readings.title: Rates And Returns Module 1.1: Interest Rates And Return Measurement
readings.summary:
## 📌 이자율(Interest Rates)의 세 가지 해석

**이자율**은 단순한 숫자가 아니라 세 가지 관점으로 해석할 수 있다:
• **요구수익률(Required Rate of Return)**: 투자자가 자금을 기꺼이 빌려주기 위해 요구하는 최소 수익
• **할인율(Discount Rate)**: 미래 현금흐름을 현재가치로 환산할 때 사용하는 비율
• **기회비용(Opportunity Cost)**: 현재 소비 대신 저축을 선택함으로써 얻는 추가 수익 (예: 5% 이자율 = 현재 소비 시 포기하는 5%)

---

## 📌 이자율의 구성 요소

**명목 무위험이자율(Nominal Risk-Free Rate)** = 실질 무위험이자율 + 인플레이션 프리미엄

• **실질 무위험이자율(Real Risk-Free Rate)**: 인플레이션·채무불이행 가능성이 없는 이론적 금리 → 현재 소비 대비 미래 소비의 선호도(**시간선호도**) 반영
• **T-bill 금리** = 명목 무위험이자율 (인플레이션 프리미엄 포함, 실질금리 ≠ T-bill)

| 위험 유형 | 설명 | 프리미엄 |
|-----------|------|----------|
| **채무불이행 위험(Default Risk)** | 차입자가 약속한 지급을 이행 못할 위험 | 채무불이행 프리미엄 |
| **유동성 위험(Liquidity Risk)** | 빠른 매각 시 공정가치 이하로 받을 위험 | 유동성 프리미엄 |
| **만기 위험(Maturity Risk)** | 장기채권일수록 금리 변동에 따른 가격 변동성 증가 | 만기 리스크 프리미엄 |

**요구수익률 공식**: `요구수익률 = 실질 무위험이자율 + 인플레이션 프리미엄 + 채무불이행 프리미엄 + 유동성 프리미엄 + 만기 리스크 프리미엄`

---

## 📌 수익률 측정 방법

**보유기간수익률(HPR, Holding Period Return)**은 가장 기본적인 수익률 측정치:
• `HPR = (종가 + 배당금) / 기초가 - 1`
• 예: 기초 €20, 배당 €1, 종가 €22 → HPR = (22+1)/20 - 1 = **15%**

세 가지 평균수익률 비교:
• **산술평균(Arithmetic Mean)**: 단순 평균, 불편추정량 — 단일 기간 기대수익률 추정에 적합
• **기하평균(Geometric Mean)**: 복리 수익률 — 다기간 실제 성과 측정에 적합
• **조화평균(Harmonic Mean)**: 정기 고정금액 투자 시 평균 매입단가 계산에 적합

**핵심 부등식**: `조화평균 ≤ 기하평균 ≤ 산술평균` (값이 동일하지 않을 때)
**수학적 관계**: `산술평균 × 조화평균 = (기하평균)²`
key_points: 이자율은 ①요구수익률, ②할인율, ③현재 소비의 기회비용 — 이 세 가지로 해석 가능하며, 시험에서 '이자율을 가장 잘 표현한 것'을 묻는 문제에 자주 출제됨
명목 무위험이자율 ≈ 실질 무위험이자율 + 기대 인플레이션율 (근사식); 정확식은 (1+명목) = (1+실질)(1+인플레이션)
T-bill 금리 = 명목 무위험이자율 (인플레이션 프리미엄 포함) → 실질(real) 금리가 아님에 주의
HPR = (종가 + 배당금) / 기초가 - 1; 다기간 HPR = (1+HPR₁)(1+HPR₂)(1+HPR₃) - 1 (복리 적용)
기하평균은 항상 산술평균 이하이며, 수익률 변동성이 클수록 두 값의 차이가 커짐; 변동성이 없을 때만 두 값이 같음
조화평균은 정액분할매수(cost averaging) 시 평균 매입단가 계산에 사용: H = N / Σ(1/Xᵢ); 항상 기하평균 이하
이상치(outlier) 처리: 절사평균(trimmed mean) — 극단값 제거; 윈저화평균(winsorized mean) — 극단값을 경계값으로 대체
요구수익률 = 실질 무위험이자율 + 인플레이션 프리미엄 + 채무불이행 프리미엄 + 유동성 프리미엄 + 만기 리스크 프리미엄 (각 위험에 대한 보상이 순차적으로 추가됨)

readings.title: The Time Value Of Money In Finance
readings.summary:
Reading 2 applies time value of money (TVM) mechanics to real financial instruments across fixed income, equity, foreign exchange, and derivatives. The core relationship is FV = PV × (1 + r)^t for discrete compounding, or FV = PV × e^(rt) for continuous compounding. Mastery of the TI BA II Plus calculator — with P/Y set to 1 — is essential for exam success, as manual algebraic solutions are too slow under exam conditions.

For fixed-income securities, the reading covers zero-coupon bonds (priced as PV of a single future cash flow), fixed-coupon bonds (PV of an annuity of coupon payments plus PV of par at maturity), perpetuities (PV = PMT/r), and amortizing bonds (level payments covering both interest and principal). A bond trades at a premium when its coupon rate exceeds its yield to maturity, and at a discount when the opposite is true. Negative yields produce zero-coupon bond prices above face value.

Equity valuation follows the same PV logic. Preferred stock paying a fixed dividend is valued as a perpetuity (P = D/r). Common stock is valued using the Gordon Growth Model (P = D1 / (r − g)), where D1 is the next dividend, r is the required return, and g is the constant dividend growth rate. These models require r > g to produce a meaningful (positive, finite) price.

TVM also appears in foreign exchange (covered interest rate parity links spot and forward FX rates through interest rate differentials) and options pricing (the no-arbitrage forward price of an asset equals its spot price compounded at the risk-free rate). These cross-topic applications reinforce that discounting and compounding are universal valuation tools throughout the CFA curriculum.
key_points: Set TI BA II Plus P/Y = 1 before all TVM problems; enter cash outflows as negative (PV for purchases) and inflows as positive (FV for receipts).
Zero-coupon bond price: PV = FV / (1 + r)^t. A negative yield means the bond prices above face value (premium).
Coupon bond value: N, PMT (= coupon rate × par), FV (= par), I/Y (= YTM) → CPT PV. Bond price and YTM move inversely; price > par when coupon rate > YTM.
Perpetuity PV = PMT / r. Preferred stock valued as perpetuity: P = D / r.
Gordon Growth Model for common stock: P = D1 / (r − g), where D1 = next period dividend, r = required return, g = constant growth rate. Only valid when r > g.
Amortizing bond / loan payment: solve for PMT with FV = 0. Each payment covers interest first; the remainder reduces principal.
Covered interest rate parity (FX): Forward rate = Spot × (1 + r_domestic) / (1 + r_foreign). No-arbitrage forward asset price = Spot × (1 + r_f)^t.

readings.title: Statistical Measures Of Asset Returns
readings.summary: This reading covers the core statistical tools used to describe and analyze investment return data. Measures of central tendency — arithmetic mean, median, and mode — each serve different purposes. The arithmetic mean is the most common but is sensitive to outliers. The median, the middle value in an ordered dataset, is preferred when outliers are present. The mode identifies the most frequent value and is most useful for discrete or grouped data. For dealing with outliers, two techniques are introduced: the trimmed mean (drops the most extreme observations by a stated percentage) and the winsorized mean (replaces extreme values with boundary percentile values rather than discarding them).
key_points: Arithmetic mean uses all data points (sum / n) and is sensitive to outliers; median is the middle value and is resistant to outliers — know when each is more appropriate.
Trimmed mean removes a stated % of extreme observations from both tails; winsorized mean replaces extreme values with the boundary percentile value — both reduce outlier impact but in different ways.
Quantiles divide a distribution into equal parts: quartiles (4), quintiles (5), deciles (10), percentiles (100). The interquartile range (IQR) = Q3 − Q1 and measures middle-50% spread.
Sample variance uses n−1 in the denominator (not n) to produce an unbiased estimator of population variance; sample standard deviation is the square root of sample variance.
Dispersion measures risk around the central tendency: range = max − min; MAD = average of absolute deviations from the mean; variance and standard deviation are the most important for exam purposes.
Coefficient of variation (CV) = standard deviation / mean — it measures risk per unit of return, allowing comparison across assets with different means. Lower CV is better.
A box and whisker plot visualizes the IQR (the box), the median, and the full data range (whiskers); asymmetry in whisker length signals potential outliers or skewness.

readings.title: Probability Trees And Conditional Expectations
readings.summary: This reading introduces the concept of expected value as the probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes for a random variable. To calculate it, multiply each possible outcome by its probability and sum the results. This applies directly to investment problems such as estimating expected earnings per share (EPS) or expected portfolio returns under different economic scenarios.
key_points: Expected value formula: E(X) = Σ P(xi) × xi — multiply each outcome by its probability and sum across all outcomes.

Variance (probability model) = Σ P(xi) × [xi − E(X)]²; standard deviation = √variance. Unlike sample statistics, no division by n−1 since no observations exist — probability weights replace sample frequencies.
A probability tree maps joint probabilities by multiplying along branches (e.g., P(good economy) × P(good results | good economy)); all terminal branch probabilities must sum to 1.
Conditional expected value revises an expected outcome based on new information (e.g., expected stock return given a tariff is imposed differs from the unconditional expected return).
Bayes' formula: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B), where P(B) = P(B|A)×P(A) + P(B|Aᶜ)×P(Aᶜ) by the total probability rule.

In Bayes' problems, always build a probability tree first: compute all joint probabilities, identify the conditioning event's total probability by summing relevant branches, then divide the target joint probability by that total.
Key distinction: prior probability is what you believed before new information; posterior (updated) probability is what you believe after applying Bayes' formula to the new information.

readings.title: Portfolio Mathematics Module 5.1: Probability Models
readings.summary:
This reading covers the foundational mathematics for measuring portfolio return and risk. The expected return of a portfolio is the weighted average of the expected returns of its component assets, where each weight equals the market value of that asset divided by the total portfolio value. Understanding how to calculate and interpret these weights is the starting point for all portfolio-level analysis.

Covariance measures how two assets' returns move together. A positive covariance means the assets tend to move in the same direction relative to their means; a negative covariance means they tend to move in opposite directions. The covariance of an asset with itself equals its variance. A covariance matrix organizes all pairwise covariances for a set of n assets, with variances on the diagonal and n(n−1)/2 unique off-diagonal covariance terms. Correlation standardizes covariance by dividing by the product of the two assets' standard deviations, producing a value bounded between −1 and +1.

Portfolio variance is calculated using asset weights, individual variances, and pairwise covariances (or equivalently, correlations). For a two-asset portfolio, variance equals the sum of each asset's squared weight times its variance, plus twice the product of both weights and their covariance. The key insight is that lower (or negative) covariances reduce portfolio variance, forming the quantitative basis for diversification. This extends to three or more assets by adding additional cross-covariance terms.

Covariance and correlation can also be computed from a joint probability function by weighting the product of each asset's deviation from its mean by the probability of each scenario. This approach is used when scenario-based return distributions are given rather than historical data.

Roy's safety-first criterion addresses shortfall risk — the probability that portfolio returns fall below a minimum acceptable threshold return (R_L). The optimal portfolio is the one that maximizes the safety-first ratio: (E(R_p) − R_L) / σ_p. Assuming normally distributed returns, this ratio equals the number of standard deviations the expected return sits above the threshold, and the portfolio with the highest ratio has the lowest probability of falling below R_L.
key_points: Portfolio expected return = weighted average of individual asset expected returns: E(R_p) = Σ w_i × E(R_i)
Portfolio variance for two assets: σ²_p = w²_A σ²_A + w²_B σ²_B + 2w_A w_B Cov(R_A, R_B); lower/negative covariance reduces portfolio risk
Covariance matrix: diagonal terms are variances; off-diagonal terms are covariances; for n assets there are n variances and n(n−1)/2 unique covariances
Correlation = Cov(R_A, R_B) / (σ_A × σ_B); bounded [−1, +1]; can substitute into variance formula using a correlation matrix instead of covariance matrix
To compute covariance from a joint probability function: Cov(R_A, R_B) = Σ P(s) × [R_A(s) − E(R_A)] × [R_B(s) − E(R_B)] across all scenarios
Shortfall risk = probability that portfolio return falls below threshold R_L; minimized by maximizing Roy's safety-first ratio = [E(R_p) − R_L] / σ_p

The safety-first ratio is interpreted as the number of standard deviations between the expected return and the threshold; higher ratio = lower shortfall probability on the standard normal distribution

readings.title: Simulation Methods Module 6.1: Lognormal
readings.summary:
The lognormal distribution arises when a variable x is normally distributed and we take e^x. Because asset prices cannot go below zero, the lognormal distribution is well-suited for modeling them. The key link is that if an asset's future price equals its current price multiplied by e raised to a continuously compounded return, and that return is normally distributed (or approximately so via the central limit theorem), then the future price is lognormally distributed. This is why lognormal is the standard assumption in option pricing and other asset valuation models.

Two important assumptions often made about returns in pricing models are that they are independently distributed (past returns don't predict future returns) and identically distributed (mean and variance are constant over time — a property called stationarity). Together these are the i.i.d. assumptions. They underpin many quantitative finance models and are worth recognizing on the exam.

Monte Carlo simulation generates a distribution of security values by repeatedly drawing random values for risk factors (e.g., stock prices, interest rates) from assumed probability distributions and plugging them into a pricing model. After thousands of iterations, the mean of the resulting distribution serves as the estimated security value. Monte Carlo is highly flexible — it can value complex derivatives, estimate VaR, and test scenarios outside historical experience — but it is a statistical rather than analytic method, and its output quality depends entirely on the quality of the input assumptions.

Bootstrap resampling is an alternative simulation approach that draws its inputs from observed historical data rather than assumed distributions. Repeated sub-samples of size n are drawn with replacement from the original sample, allowing observations to appear more than once. The standard deviation of the resulting sample means estimates the standard error of the sample mean. While bootstrapping avoids the need to specify a theoretical distribution, its outputs are inherently constrained by the historical data used — it cannot model scenarios that never occurred.
key_points: If x is normal, then e^x is lognormal; asset prices are modeled as lognormal because PT = P0 * e^(r), where r is a normally distributed continuously compounded return.
Lognormal distribution is bounded below by zero (no negative asset prices) and is right-skewed — mean > median > mode.
i.i.d. returns: 'independently distributed' means past returns don't predict future returns; 'identically distributed' means constant mean and variance (stationarity).
Monte Carlo simulation: specify distributions for risk factors → randomly generate values → price the security → repeat thousands of times → use mean of distribution as estimate.
Monte Carlo limitation: it is statistical (not analytic), cannot provide closed-form insights, and output is only as good as the assumed distributions and pricing model.
Monte Carlo advantage: inputs are NOT limited to historical data, so analysts can stress-test scenarios that have never occurred.

Bootstrap resampling draws repeated samples WITH replacement from observed data; the standard deviation of sample means estimates standard error — but unlike Monte Carlo, inputs are constrained by actual historical outcomes.

readings.title: Estimation And Inference Module 7.1: Sampling Techniques
readings.summary:
Sampling methods fall into two broad categories: probability sampling (where each population member has a known selection probability) and nonprobability sampling (where selection is based on convenience or researcher judgment). Probability methods include simple random sampling, stratified random sampling, cluster sampling, and systematic sampling. Nonprobability methods include convenience sampling and judgmental sampling. In general, less randomness in the selection process leads to greater sampling error.

Simple random sampling gives every member of the population an equal chance of selection. Stratified random sampling divides the population into subgroups (strata) based on distinguishing characteristics, then randomly samples from each stratum proportionally. This is widely used in bond indexing to ensure representation across key risk factor categories (e.g., maturity, coupon rate) without having to replicate an entire index. The key advantage is that it guarantees proportional representation from every stratum, unlike simple random sampling.

Cluster sampling also uses subgroups, but assumes each cluster is representative of the whole population. In one-stage cluster sampling, entire clusters are selected; in two-stage, random samples are drawn from within selected clusters. Cluster sampling trades accuracy for cost efficiency — it generally produces more sampling error than simple random or stratified sampling, but is cheaper and faster. It is best suited for pilot studies or situations where cost constraints are significant.

Nonprobability methods introduce researcher-driven biases. Convenience sampling uses whatever data is easily accessible, while judgmental sampling relies on the researcher's expertise to hand-pick observations. Both can produce higher sampling error than probability methods, though judgmental sampling by a skilled analyst may sometimes yield a more focused, representative sample for a specific question.

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is foundational to statistical inference. It states that for samples of size n ≥ 30 drawn from any population with mean μ and finite variance σ², the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution with mean μ and variance σ²/n. This allows analysts to make probability statements and construct confidence intervals about population means regardless of the underlying population distribution, as long as the sample is sufficiently large.
key_points: Probability sampling (simple random, stratified, cluster) gives each member a known selection probability; nonprobability sampling (convenience, judgmental) does not — less randomness = more sampling error.
Stratified random sampling guarantees proportional representation from every stratum; it is the preferred method for bond index replication (stratify by maturity, coupon, duration, etc.).

Cluster sampling assumes each cluster mirrors the whole population; one-stage (all items in selected clusters) has less sampling error than two-stage (random items from selected clusters); lower cost is its main advantage.
Central Limit Theorem: for n ≥ 30, the distribution of sample means is approximately normal with mean μ and variance σ²/n, regardless of the population's distribution.
Standard error of the sample mean = σ/√n (or s/√n when population σ is unknown); larger samples produce smaller standard errors and more precise estimates.

When sampling across a structural break (e.g., pre- vs. post-financial-crisis bank data), pooling data from different regimes produces a sample mean that is not representative of either period — always check for distributional stability.
Systematic sampling (every nth member) is an approximation of simple random sampling and is acceptable when the population has no periodic pattern that aligns with the sampling interval.

readings.title: Hypothesis Testing Module 8.1: Hypothesis Testing
readings.summary:
Hypothesis testing is a statistical procedure used to evaluate whether a statement about a population parameter is reasonable based on sample data. The process begins by stating a null hypothesis (H0), which always includes an 'equal to' condition and represents what the researcher wants to reject, and an alternative hypothesis (Ha), which represents the conclusion drawn if H0 is rejected. These two hypotheses are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, covering all possible outcomes.

The test statistic is the core calculation: it measures the difference between the sample statistic and the hypothesized population value, scaled by the standard error. The critical value—derived from the appropriate distribution (z, t, chi-square, or F)—serves as the benchmark. If the computed test statistic falls in the rejection region (beyond the critical value), the null is rejected; otherwise, we fail to reject it. For a two-tailed z-test at α = 0.05, the critical values are ±1.96.

Two types of errors can occur in hypothesis testing. A Type I error is rejecting a true null hypothesis; its probability equals the significance level α. A Type II error is failing to reject a false null hypothesis. The power of a test is defined as 1 − P(Type II error), representing the probability of correctly rejecting a false null. There is an inherent trade-off: lowering α (reducing Type I error risk) increases the probability of a Type II error and reduces the power of the test.

Sample size plays a critical role in managing error probabilities. Increasing the sample size reduces the standard error of the test statistic, which simultaneously lowers both Type I and Type II error probabilities—effectively increasing the power of the test without sacrificing significance level. Researchers should choose the test statistic that yields the most powerful test among valid alternatives.
key_points: The null hypothesis (H0) ALWAYS includes the 'equal to' condition (e.g., H0: μ = μ0); the alternative hypothesis (Ha) is what you are trying to support.
Type I error = rejecting a TRUE null (probability = α, the significance level); Type II error = failing to reject a FALSE null (probability = β).
Power of a test = 1 − P(Type II error) = probability of correctly rejecting a false null; always prefer the test statistic with the highest power.
For a two-tailed z-test at α = 0.05, critical values are ±1.96; at α = 0.01, critical values are ±2.576.
Test statistic formula: (sample statistic − hypothesized value) / standard error of the sample statistic.
Decreasing α (e.g., from 5% to 1%) reduces Type I error risk but increases Type II error risk (inverse relationship between the two error types).
Increasing sample size reduces the standard error, which increases test power and can reduce both types of errors simultaneously.

readings.title: Parametric And Non-Parametric Tests Of Independence
readings.summary:
This reading covers how to test whether two variables are statistically related, using both parametric and nonparametric methods. The core parametric test asks whether a population correlation coefficient (ρ) equals zero. When both variables are normally distributed, we use a t-statistic calculated as r√(n−2)/√(1−r²), which follows a t-distribution with n−2 degrees of freedom. A significant result means we reject the null that ρ = 0 and conclude the variables are correlated.

When data are not normally distributed or we only have ranked data, the Spearman rank correlation (rs) is the appropriate nonparametric alternative. It is computed from the differences in paired ranks: rs = 1 − (6Σdi²)/(n(n²−1)). Ties are handled by averaging the tied ranks. The significance of rs is tested using the same t-statistic formula as the parametric test, and for samples larger than 30, the statistic follows a t-distribution with n−2 degrees of freedom.

For categorical data, the chi-square test of independence uses a contingency (two-way) table. Each cell contains the observed count of items sharing two characteristics. Expected frequencies are computed as (row total × column total) / overall total, representing what we would see if the two characteristics were truly independent. The chi-square statistic sums (O−E)²/E across all cells.

The degrees of freedom for the chi-square test are (r−1)(c−1), where r and c are the number of row and column categories. If the computed chi-square statistic exceeds the critical value at the chosen significance level, we reject the hypothesis of independence and conclude the two characteristics are related. This test is widely applicable to practical finance questions such as whether earnings growth and dividend yield categories co-move.
key_points: Parametric correlation test: t = r√(n−2)/√(1−r²), t-distribution with n−2 degrees of freedom (NOT n−1).
The t-statistic for correlation increases with both larger r and larger sample size n.
Spearman rank correlation formula: rs = 1 − (6Σdi²)/(n(n²−1)); use when data are ranked or non-normal.
Spearman rank correlation significance is tested with the same t-statistic and n−2 df (valid for n > 30).
Contingency table chi-square test: χ² = Σ(O−E)²/E; degrees of freedom = (r−1)(c−1).
Expected cell frequency = (row total × column total) / grand total; reflects independence assumption.
Rejecting the chi-square null means the two categorical characteristics are NOT independent (i.e., they are related).

readings.title: Simple Linear Regression Module 10.1: Linear Regression
readings.summary:
Simple linear regression explains the variation in a dependent variable (also called the explained, endogenous, or predicted variable) using a single independent variable (also called the explanatory, exogenous, or predicting variable). The goal is to find a straight line — called the regression line or line of best fit — that best captures the linear relationship between the two variables as observed in a scatter plot of the data.

The regression line is estimated using the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) criterion, which minimizes the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE) — the sum of the squared vertical distances between each actual Y-value and the corresponding predicted Y-value from the regression equation. This is why the method is called 'least squares.' The estimated regression equation takes the form Ŷ = b̂₀ + b̂₁X, where b̂₁ is the slope and b̂₀ is the intercept.

The slope coefficient (b̂₁) measures the expected change in the dependent variable for a one-unit change in the independent variable. It is calculated as the covariance of X and Y divided by the variance of X. The intercept (b̂₀) represents the estimated value of the dependent variable when the independent variable equals zero, and is computed as the mean of Y minus the slope times the mean of X. A key property of OLS is that the regression line always passes through the point (X̄, Ȳ).

Interpreting regression coefficients correctly is essential for the exam. For example, in a regression of ABC stock excess returns on S&P 500 excess returns, a slope of 0.64 means ABC excess returns are expected to change by 0.64% for every 1% change in S&P 500 excess returns. This slope coefficient in a market model is known as beta — a measure of systematic risk. A beta less than 1 indicates below-average market risk; greater than 1 indicates above-average risk.

Importantly, the magnitude of the slope coefficient alone does not indicate the strength of the relationship. Statistical significance must be assessed through hypothesis testing or confidence intervals before concluding that the independent variable meaningfully explains variation in the dependent variable.
key_points: Dependent variable = explained/endogenous/predicted; Independent variable = explanatory/exogenous/predicting — know all synonyms.
OLS minimizes the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE); the resulting estimates are called least squares estimates and the method is also called ordinary least squares (OLS) regression.
Slope formula: b̂₁ = Cov(X,Y) / Var(X); Intercept formula: b̂₀ = Ȳ − b̂₁X̄. The regression line always passes through (X̄, Ȳ).
Slope interpretation: expected change in Y for a one-unit increase in X. Intercept interpretation: expected value of Y when X = 0.

In a market model regression (excess stock return on excess market return), the slope coefficient equals the stock's beta — a measure of systematic risk (beta < 1 = below-average risk, beta > 1 = above-average risk).
A large or small slope coefficient does NOT indicate a strong or weak relationship — statistical significance (hypothesis test or confidence interval) must be evaluated separately.
Residual (error) = Actual Y − Predicted Ŷ. The regression line minimizes the sum of these squared residuals, not the residuals themselves.

readings.title: Introduction To Big Data Techniques
readings.summary:
Fintech refers to technology developments applied to financial services, covering everything from handling large datasets to AI-driven analytics. Fintech companies focus on expanding data processing capabilities and building tools to extract actionable insights from vast amounts of information generated across the modern economy.

Big Data encompasses all potentially useful information generated in the economy, including traditional sources (financial markets, company reports, government statistics) and alternative sources such as social media posts, corporate exhaust (bank records, retail scanner data), and IoT sensor data. Big Data is characterized by the three Vs: Volume (growing from gigabytes to petabytes), Velocity (speed of data communication, with real-time feeds being low latency and periodic updates being high latency), and Variety (ranging from structured spreadsheets to semi-structured web code to unstructured video).

Data science extracts meaning from Big Data through five processing methods—Capture, Curation, Storage, Search, and Transfer—and through visualization techniques like charts, word clouds, and mind maps. Key challenges include ensuring data quality (handling outliers, missing data, and sampling bias) and organizing qualitative or unstructured data, which is where artificial intelligence becomes valuable. Neural networks simulate human brain processing and form a core AI tool.

Machine learning is a critical AI development in which algorithms learn patterns from data without human guidance or distributional assumptions. It follows a three-dataset workflow: training (find relationships), validation (refine models), and test (evaluate predictive power). Supervised learning uses labeled inputs and outputs; unsupervised learning uses unlabeled inputs to find structure; deep learning uses layered neural networks for increasingly complex pattern recognition. Key pitfalls are overfitting (model too complex, learns noise) and underfitting (model too simple, misses true patterns), plus the 'black box' problem of unexplainable outcomes.

Fintech applications in investment management include text analytics (analyzing unstructured text/voice, e.g., regulatory filings), natural language processing (interpreting human language for compliance checks or sentiment analysis of research reports), risk governance (using machine learning and real-time data for stress testing and risk modeling), and algorithmic trading (rule-based computerized trading for optimal execution, large order splitting across exchanges, and high-frequency trading).
key_points: Big Data has three defining characteristics — the 3 Vs: Volume (petabytes scale), Velocity (low latency = real-time; high latency = periodic), and Variety (structured → semi-structured → unstructured).
Alternative data sources include individual-generated data (social media, emails), corporate exhaust (bank/scanner records), and IoT sensors — all nontraditional inputs for financial analysis.
Data science processing steps: Capture → Curation (quality control) → Storage → Search → Transfer. Curation addresses outliers, bad/missing data, and sampling bias.
Machine learning workflow: Train on training dataset → Refine on validation dataset → Test predictive ability on test dataset. No assumptions about probability distributions.
Supervised learning uses labeled input/output data; unsupervised learning uses unlabeled input data to find structure; deep learning uses layered neural networks and can use either approach.
Overfitting = model too complex, treats noise as signal (spurious patterns); Underfitting = model too simple, misses true patterns. Both are machine learning failure modes to recognize.

Four fintech investment management applications: Text analytics (unstructured data analysis), Natural language processing (human language interpretation), Risk governance (ML-driven stress testing), and Algorithmic trading (rule-based execution, high-frequency trading).

readings.title: Firms And Market Structures Module 12.1: Breakeven, Shutdown, and Scale
readings.summary:
This reading distinguishes between the short run and long run for firms. In the short run, at least one factor of production (typically capital) is fixed, meaning a firm cannot avoid certain costs even if it shuts down. In the long run, all costs become variable and the firm can exit entirely. This distinction drives the different shutdown rules that apply in each time horizon.

For a price-taker firm under perfect competition, the breakeven point occurs where price (average revenue) equals average total cost, yielding zero economic profit. The short-run shutdown point is where price falls below average variable cost (AR < AVC): at this level, losses from operating exceed total fixed costs, so shutting down minimizes losses. Between AVC and ATC, the firm has losses but should continue operating short-run because it still covers variable costs and contributes toward fixed costs. In the long run, the firm shuts down if price remains below minimum ATC.

For price-searcher firms under imperfect competition, the same logic applies but is better analyzed using total revenue (TR) versus total cost (TC) and total variable cost (TVC), since marginal revenue no longer equals price. The conditions are: TR = TC means break even; TC > TR > TVC means operate short-run but exit long-run; TR < TVC means shut down immediately in both the short and long run.

The reading also covers economies and diseconomies of scale, analyzed through the long-run average total cost (LRATC) curve. This U-shaped curve envelopes all short-run ATC curves for different plant sizes. When LRATC falls as output increases, the firm enjoys economies of scale; when LRATC rises, diseconomies of scale set in. The minimum point of the LRATC curve is the minimum efficient scale. Under perfect competition, long-run equilibrium forces firms to operate at this minimum efficient scale, where LRATC equals market price and economic profit equals zero.
key_points: Short-run shutdown rule: Shut down if AR < AVC (losses exceed fixed costs); continue operating if AR > AVC even with losses, because you cover at least some fixed costs.
Long-run shutdown rule: Exit the market if AR < ATC (price cannot cover all costs); stay if AR ≥ ATC.
Breakeven point: AR = ATC, meaning TR = TC and economic profit = zero (not accounting profit).
For imperfect competition (price-searchers), use total revenue vs. total cost: TR < TVC → shut down now; TVC < TR < TC → operate short-run only; TR ≥ TC → stay in market.
The LRATC curve is U-shaped and envelopes all SRATC curves; its minimum point is the minimum efficient scale where long-run per-unit costs are lowest.
Economies of scale: LRATC falls as output/scale increases. Diseconomies of scale: LRATC rises as output/scale increases (often due to management complexity).
Under perfect competition, long-run equilibrium requires firms operate at minimum efficient scale with zero economic profit and P = LRATC = minimum ATC.

readings.title: Understanding Business Cycles
readings.summary:
The business cycle describes recurring fluctuations in economic activity measured by real GDP. It has four phases: expansion (rising GDP), peak (GDP stops rising), contraction/recession (falling GDP), and trough (GDP stops falling). Economists typically define a recession as two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP, though agencies like the NBER use broader data including unemployment, industrial production, and inflation to identify turning points. Three ways to measure cycles exist: the classical cycle (GDP vs. a base value), the growth cycle (GDP vs. long-term trend), and the growth rate cycle (annualized growth rate changes) — the last being preferred by practitioners.

Credit cycles refer to cyclical swings in the availability and cost of credit. During expansions, lenders loosen standards and lower rates; during contractions, credit tightens. Credit cycles can amplify business cycles — loose credit during expansions may inflate asset bubbles (e.g., subprime mortgages pre-2008), making expansions stronger and recessions deeper. Importantly, credit cycles and business cycles do not always coincide, and credit cycles tend to be longer in duration.

Inventories and labor are key indicators of cycle turning points. Near a peak, slowing sales cause unplanned inventory accumulation (rising inventory-sales ratio), prompting firms to cut production — a leading signal of contraction. Near a trough, depleted inventories signal recovery as firms ramp production. Firms adjust labor gradually: they first change hours and overtime before hiring or laying off, and similarly use existing capital more or less intensively before investing in or disposing of plant and equipment.

Consumer spending, the largest GDP component, is cyclically sensitive but varies by category. Durable goods spending (appliances, autos, furniture) is highly cyclical because consumers defer big-ticket purchases during uncertainty. Services spending is moderately cyclical — discretionary services (travel, dining) fluctuate more than necessities (healthcare, insurance). Nondurable goods (food, household products) are relatively stable across the cycle.

Housing and external trade sectors also follow cyclical patterns. Housing is sensitive to mortgage rates and consumer confidence, often leading the broader cycle. External trade is influenced by the relative strength of domestic vs. foreign economies and currency movements — a stronger domestic economy typically widens the trade deficit as imports rise. Economic indicators are classified as leading (predict turning points), coincident (move with GDP), or lagging (confirm trends after the fact), and analysts use composite indexes of these to track and forecast cycle phases.
key_points: Four business cycle phases in order: Expansion → Peak → Contraction/Recession → Trough; a recession is conventionally defined as two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP.

Three cycle measurement methods: classical cycle (vs. base GDP), growth cycle (vs. trend GDP), and growth rate cycle (annualized growth rate changes) — the growth rate cycle is preferred by economists and shows peaks/troughs earliest.

Credit cycles amplify business cycles: loose credit fuels expansions and asset bubbles; tight credit deepens recessions. Credit cycles are generally longer in duration than business cycles and do not always coincide with them.
Rising inventory-sales ratio above normal → leading warning sign of an approaching peak/contraction; falling ratio below normal → early signal of recovery/expansion.
Firms adjust labor and capital gradually: hours/overtime are changed first, then hiring/layoffs; existing capital is used more/less intensively before new investment or equipment sales.
Consumer spending cyclicality by category (most to least): Durable goods (most cyclical) > Discretionary services > Non-discretionary services > Nondurable goods (most stable).

Economic indicators are classified as leading (e.g., stock prices, building permits), coincident (e.g., industrial production, personal income), or lagging (e.g., unemployment rate, CPI) — leading indicators are most useful for predicting cycle turning points.

readings.title: Fiscal Policy Module 14.1: Fiscal Policy
readings.summary:
Fiscal policy refers to a government's use of spending and taxation to influence economic activity, while monetary policy refers to central bank actions affecting money and credit supply. A budget surplus occurs when tax revenues exceed expenditures; a deficit occurs when expenditures exceed revenues. Expansionary fiscal policy (higher deficits) increases GDP and aggregate demand, while contractionary fiscal policy (lower deficits) reduces them. Both fiscal and monetary policy aim to maintain price stability and promote economic growth, but only fiscal policy is also used for income and wealth redistribution.

Fiscal policy objectives include influencing aggregate demand, redistributing wealth, and allocating resources. Keynesian economists argue fiscal policy has a strong effect on growth when the economy is below full employment. Monetarists, by contrast, believe fiscal stimulus is only temporary and prefer monetary policy for managing inflation over time — but do not support using monetary policy to counter cyclical fluctuations. Discretionary fiscal policy involves deliberate government spending and tax decisions, while automatic stabilizers (e.g., unemployment insurance, tax revenue changes) naturally expand deficits in recessions and contract them in booms without legislative action.

When governments run persistent deficits, they accumulate debt. A key metric is the debt-to-GDP ratio. If the real interest rate on government debt exceeds real GDP growth, the debt ratio rises over time; if real GDP growth exceeds the real interest rate, the debt ratio falls. Concerns about large deficits include higher future taxes (disincentives to work), loss of investor confidence leading to default or inflation via money printing, and the crowding-out effect — where government borrowing raises interest rates and displaces private investment.

Arguments against worrying about deficit size include: domestically held debt is less of a burden, productive capital investment generates future returns to repay debt, deficits can prompt tax reform, Ricardian equivalence suggests private savings offset government deficits, and deficits do not crowd out private capital when the economy is below full capacity. Understanding when each argument applies is critical for exam questions on fiscal sustainability.

Fiscal spending tools include transfer payments (not counted in GDP), current government spending on goods and services, and capital spending on infrastructure. Revenue tools include direct taxes (income, wealth, corporate, capital gains) and indirect taxes (sales, VAT, excise). Progressive direct taxes help redistribute income, while indirect taxes can discourage consumption of specific goods. Desirable tax attributes include simplicity, efficiency, and fairness.
key_points: Expansionary fiscal policy = increased deficit (higher spending or lower taxes); contractionary = decreased deficit. Memorize direction for both budget balance and GDP impact.

Keynesian view: fiscal policy strongly affects aggregate demand below full employment. Monetarist view: fiscal stimulus is temporary; monetary policy manages inflation but should NOT target cyclical demand.
Automatic stabilizers (unemployment insurance, tax revenues) act without legislation — expansionary in recessions, contractionary in booms. Distinguish from discretionary fiscal policy.
Debt-to-GDP ratio rises when real interest rate on debt > real GDP growth rate; falls when real GDP growth > real interest rate. This is a frequently tested relationship.
Crowding-out effect: government borrowing raises interest rates, reducing private investment and partially offsetting the expansionary impact of deficit spending.
Ricardian equivalence: if private sector saves in anticipation of future taxes to repay government debt, fiscal deficits have no net stimulative effect — private savings exactly offset the deficit.
Transfer payments (Social Security, unemployment insurance) redistribute wealth but are NOT included in GDP calculations — a common exam trap.

readings.title: Monetary Policy Module 15.1: Central Bank
readings.summary:
Central banks serve six core roles in an economy: sole supplier of currency (issuing fiat money backed by law rather than gold), banker to governments and commercial banks, regulator and supervisor of the payments system, lender of last resort (preventing bank runs by guaranteeing liquidity), holder of gold and foreign exchange reserves, and conductor of monetary policy. The primary objective of virtually all central banks is price stability through inflation control, since high inflation generates menu costs (businesses constantly repricing) and shoe leather costs (individuals frequently moving cash to avoid depreciation).

Beyond price stability, central banks may pursue secondary goals including exchange rate stability, full employment, sustainable economic growth, and moderate long-term interest rates. Most developed nations target inflation of around 2–3%; a zero-inflation target is deliberately avoided because it risks deflation. The U.S. Federal Reserve is notable for its dual mandate — price stability plus maximum employment — which is why it lacks an explicit numerical inflation target, unlike most peers.

Central banks implement monetary policy through three main tools. The policy rate (discount rate in the U.S., refinancing rate for the ECB, two-week repo rate for the Bank of England) sets the cost of borrowing reserves; lower rates stimulate lending and reduce interest rates broadly. Reserve requirements dictate what fraction of deposits banks must hold back; raising them tightens the money supply while lowering them expands it. Open market operations — buying and selling securities — are the most frequently used tool in the U.S.; buying securities injects reserves and lowers rates, while selling drains reserves and raises rates.

The monetary transmission mechanism describes how a change in the policy rate ripples through the economy to affect prices and inflation via four channels: (1) other short-term lending rates rise or fall in tandem, affecting consumer credit and business investment; (2) asset prices (bonds, equities, real estate) move inversely with discount rates, creating wealth effects on consumption; (3) exchange rates shift as higher domestic rates attract foreign capital, appreciating the currency and lowering import prices; and (4) expectations adjust — tighter policy signals lower future growth, reducing expenditure. Contractionary policy works through all four channels to reduce aggregate demand and cool inflation, while expansionary policy does the opposite.
key_points: Central banks have SIX roles: currency supplier, government/bank banker, payments regulator, lender of last resort, FX/gold reserve holder, and monetary policy conductor.
Primary goal is PRICE STABILITY; most developed countries target 2–3% inflation. Zero-inflation target is avoided because it risks deflation.
The U.S. Fed has a DUAL MANDATE (price stability + maximum employment) and no explicit numerical inflation target — unlike the ECB and Bank of England.
THREE monetary policy tools: (1) Policy rate — discount rate (U.S.), refinancing rate (ECB), repo rate (UK); (2) Reserve requirements; (3) Open market operations (most common Fed tool).
Open market operations: Central bank BUYING securities → more reserves → lower rates (expansionary); SELLING securities → fewer reserves → higher rates (contractionary).
FOUR transmission channels from policy rate to prices: short-term lending rates, asset prices (wealth effect), exchange rates (currency appreciation lowers import prices), and inflation expectations.

Exchange rate PEGGING: a country selling domestic currency for dollars to prevent appreciation must align its monetary policy with the anchor country, effectively importing that country's inflation rate.

readings.title: Introduction To Geopolitics Module 16.1: Geopolitics
readings.summary:
Geopolitics encompasses the interactions among nations through both state actors (national governments) and nonstate actors (corporations, NGOs, individuals), as well as the study of how geography shapes these interactions. Countries can be analyzed along a cooperation spectrum—ranging from fully cooperative to noncooperative—across diplomatic, military, economic, and cultural dimensions. A country's willingness to cooperate is driven primarily by national interests, with survival at the top of the hierarchy, and is influenced by geophysical endowments, domestic politics, and cultural factors like shared language or historical ties. Soft power—the ability to influence others without force—is one tool countries use through cultural exchange and institutional strength.

Globalization refers to the long-term trend toward worldwide economic and cultural integration, measurable by international trade as a percentage of GDP (rising from ~25% in the early 1970s to ~60% before the 2008 crisis). The globalization-nationalism spectrum, combined with the cooperation-noncooperation spectrum, creates a four-quadrant framework: Autarky (nationalist + noncooperative), Hegemony (globalist + noncooperative), Bilateralism (nationalist + cooperative), and Multilateralism (globalist + cooperative). Nonstate actors such as businesses and investors often push toward globalization through portfolio investment flows and foreign direct investment, even when their governments lean nationalist.

Three key international organizations facilitate global trade and economic stability. The IMF promotes international monetary cooperation, exchange rate stability, balanced trade growth, and provides resources to members facing balance-of-payments difficulties. The World Bank focuses on poverty reduction and development by providing financial and technical assistance to developing countries. The World Trade Organization (WTO) administers trade agreements, provides a dispute-resolution forum, and works to reduce trade barriers among its member nations. Together, these institutions form the institutional backbone of the multilateral global trading system.
key_points: Geopolitics involves both STATE actors (governments) and NONSTATE actors (corporations, NGOs, individuals); nonstate actors often favor globalization more than their governments.
Cooperation vs. noncooperation is a SPECTRUM, not binary — a country can be cooperative on some issues and not others, and this can change with domestic political cycles.

Four geopolitical archetypes from the 2x2 framework: AUTARKY (noncooperative + nationalist), HEGEMONY (noncooperative + globalist), BILATERALISM (cooperative + nationalist), MULTILATERALISM (cooperative + globalist).
Globalization is measured by international trade as % of GDP — rose from ~25% (early 1970s) to ~60% (pre-2008) and has stayed near that level; NATIONALISM is its opposition in this context.
IMF's five goals: monetary cooperation, balanced trade growth, exchange stability, multilateral payments system, and balance-of-payments assistance to members.
World Bank = financial/technical assistance to DEVELOPING countries to fight poverty; WTO = administers trade agreements and resolves trade DISPUTES among member nations.
SOFT POWER = influencing other countries without force or threats, often via cultural exchange; strong legal/ethical institutions (e.g., IP protection) enable both soft power and globalization.

readings.title: International Trade Module 17.1: International Trade
readings.summary:
International trade generates gains for participating countries primarily through comparative advantage — countries specialize in goods they produce at lower relative cost and import goods others produce more efficiently. Newer trade models expand on this by highlighting gains from economies of scale, greater product variety, increased competition improving quality and reducing costs, and more efficient resource allocation. Free trade also reduces domestic monopoly pricing power and enables consumers to access differentiated products across global markets.

Despite aggregate gains, international trade imposes real costs on specific groups. In an importing country, workers and producers in the competing domestic industry face job losses and wage pressure. In the exporting country, domestic consumers may face higher prices as production shifts toward export demand. Labor-intensive industries facing low-cost foreign competition are particularly vulnerable to wage suppression and employment losses, potentially worsening income inequality.

The core economic argument for free trade is that aggregate gains exceed aggregate losses — in theory, winners could compensate losers and both countries would still benefit. In the long run, displaced workers retrain and find employment in growing industries, mitigating short-run adjustment costs. This long-run efficiency argument underpins most economists' support for open trade despite acknowledging transitional hardships.

Governments impose trade restrictions for various reasons. Economically defensible justifications include protecting infant industries until they reach competitive scale and safeguarding industries critical to national security. Less defensible arguments — protecting domestic jobs, shielding politically connected industries, and retaliation — typically harm consumers through higher prices and reduce overall welfare.

The main trade restriction tools are tariffs (taxes on imports), quotas (quantity limits), export subsidies (payments to exporters), minimum domestic content requirements, and voluntary export restraints. Tariffs and equivalent quotas both raise domestic prices, increase domestic production, reduce imports, and harm domestic consumers. The key difference: tariff revenue goes to the domestic government, while quota rents go to foreign exporters (unless the government auctions import licenses). Both create deadweight loss — the net welfare loss to society after accounting for producer surplus gains and government revenue.
key_points: Comparative advantage drives classical trade gains: countries specialize where they have lower relative (not absolute) production costs — total output and wealth rise for both trading partners.

Free trade creates winners and losers within each country: domestic consumers and export-sector workers gain; import-competing workers and producers lose. Net gains are positive in aggregate but unevenly distributed.

Tariff vs. quota equivalence: an equivalent quota produces the same domestic price increase and import reduction as a tariff, but tariff revenue stays with the domestic government, whereas quota rents accrue to foreign exporters (unless import licenses are auctioned).
Both tariffs and quotas create deadweight loss — the two triangles in the welfare diagram — representing the net efficiency loss to the domestic economy beyond what any party captures.

Economically supported reasons for trade restrictions: infant industry protection (temporary, to reach competitive scale) and national security (ensuring domestic supply of defense-critical goods). Protecting jobs and industries generally lacks theoretical support.

Export subsidies allow domestic producers to sell abroad below cost, harming foreign competitors; the domestic government bears the subsidy cost while domestic consumers may face higher prices if supply shifts to export markets.

Voluntary export restraints (VERs) are quota-like: the exporting country restricts its own exports, typically to preempt harsher tariffs or quotas — rents go to foreign producers, making VERs worse for the importing country than equivalent tariffs.

readings.title: Capital Flows And The Fx Market
readings.summary: The foreign exchange (FX) market exists primarily to facilitate cross-border trade in goods, services, and financial assets. While trade in goods and services drives some currency demand, capital flows—purchases of foreign physical and financial assets—represent an even larger driver of FX activity. Participants are divided into the sell side (large multinational banks that act as primary dealers and originators of forward contracts) and the buy side (corporations, investment accounts, governments, central banks, and retail participants).
key_points: Exchange rate notation: P/B means 'price of one unit of base currency in price currency units' — e.g., 1.25 USD/EUR means 1 euro costs $1.25; USD is price currency, EUR is base currency.
Direct vs. indirect quote: A P/B quote is a DIRECT quote for investors in the price-currency country and an INDIRECT quote for investors in the base-currency country.
Nominal exchange rate change: If USD/EUR rises, the USD has weakened (costs more USD to buy 1 EUR); if USD/EUR falls, the USD has strengthened.

Real exchange rate formula: Real P/B (end) = Nominal P/B (end) × (CPI_price / CPI_base). A higher price level in the price-currency country LOWERS the real exchange rate (increases purchasing power of price currency in base country).
Hedging vs. speculating: Taking an FX position to REDUCE existing currency risk = hedging; taking an FX position that INCREASES currency risk = speculating.
Real money accounts (mutual funds, pension funds, insurers) do NOT use derivatives; leveraged accounts (hedge funds, prop trading firms) DO use derivatives including FX forwards and options.
Central banks intervene in FX markets to influence exchange rates in the short term as a matter of government monetary/economic policy — a key buy-side participant type to remember.

readings.title: Exchange Rate Calculations Module 19.1: Foreign Exchange
readings.summary: Forward exchange rates can be quoted in three ways: outright forward rate, forward points (added to or subtracted from the spot rate, where each point equals the last decimal place of the spot quote), or as a percentage premium/discount relative to spot. Mastering the conversion between these formats is essential for exam calculations. The currency with the higher interest rate must trade at a forward discount (depreciate forward) by approximately the interest rate differential.
key_points: Cross-rate formula: set up quotations so units cancel algebraically. E.g., MXN/AUD = (MXN/USD) × (USD/AUD), or CHF/NZD = (CHF/USD) ÷ (NZD/USD). Take the inverse of a rate when needed to get matching units.
No-arbitrage forward rate formula: F = S × [(1 + r_price currency) / (1 + r_base currency)]. For periods shorter than one year, use the actual period rate (money market yield × days/360 or days/365).

The currency with the HIGHER interest rate trades at a FORWARD DISCOUNT (forward rate > spot in price/base terms where base is the high-rate currency). The interest rate differential approximately equals the forward premium/discount percentage.

Forward points: each point = last decimal place of spot (e.g., 0.0001 for a 4-decimal spot). Add points to spot to get the outright forward rate. Positive points = forward premium on base currency; negative = forward discount.
Percentage forward quote: F = S × (1 + forward %). A negative percentage means forward rate is below spot (base currency depreciates forward).

Arbitrage mechanics to know: borrow domestic, convert at spot, invest at foreign rate, sell proceeds forward. Profit exists if return from this cycle exceeds domestic borrowing cost — arbitrageurs then buy/sell spot and forward until parity holds.

Do NOT interpret the no-arbitrage forward rate as the expected future spot rate. Empirically, interest rate differentials are poor predictors of future spot rates; the forward rate is simply the rate that eliminates arbitrage at a given moment.

readings.title: Organizational Forms, Corporate Issuer Features, and Ownership
readings.summary:
This reading compares four primary business organizational forms: sole proprietorships, general partnerships, limited partnerships, and corporations. Sole proprietorships and general partnerships are simple structures where owners have unlimited personal liability and profits are taxed as personal income. Limited partnerships introduce two tiers — general partners with unlimited liability who run the business, and limited partners whose liability is capped at their investment. Corporations are fundamentally different: they are separate legal entities, all shareholders have limited liability, and the separation of ownership from management is formalized through a board of directors.

Corporations can be public or private. Public companies list shares on a stock exchange, providing liquidity and price transparency, but face significant regulatory and reporting requirements. Private companies have fewer disclosure obligations, can take a longer-term view, and raise equity through private placements restricted to accredited investors. A key downside of corporations is the potential for double taxation — corporate profits taxed at the entity level, then dividends taxed again as personal income to shareholders.

Private companies can go public via three routes: an IPO (issuing new shares to the public), a direct listing (existing shares listed without new issuance), or acquisition by a special purpose acquisition company (SPAC). Each method has different implications for capital raising and existing shareholder dilution. Understanding the trade-offs among organizational forms — liability, taxation, capital access, and governance — is central to this reading.
key_points: Unlimited liability applies to sole proprietors and ALL general partners (including general partners in a limited partnership); limited partners and corporate shareholders have limited liability capped at their investment.

Double taxation is unique to corporations: profits taxed at the corporate level first, then dividends taxed again as personal income. The effective tax burden decreases when companies retain earnings rather than paying dividends.
A corporation is a separate legal entity — it can enter contracts, hire employees, borrow money, and sue/be sued independently of its owners.
Free float = shares actively tradeable by the public, excluding insider/strategic/sponsor holdings; expressed as a percentage of total shares outstanding.

Three ways a private company can go public: IPO (new shares issued, meets exchange requirements), direct listing (existing shares listed, no new capital raised), or SPAC acquisition (shell company merges with private firm).

Private placements are restricted to accredited investors (institutional investors, high net worth individuals); private companies have fewer regulatory requirements but less liquidity and harder share transfers.
In a limited liability partnership (LLP), all partners have limited liability; in the U.S., LLPs are restricted to professional service firms (law, accounting) with caps on partner count and equity.

readings.title: Investors And Other Stakeholders
readings.summary:
Reading 23 establishes the fundamental distinction between debt and equity claims on a corporation. Lenders hold a contractual, senior claim to promised interest and principal payments, while equity holders have a residual claim to net assets after all liabilities are settled. Debt is less risky and therefore less costly than equity, but debtholders have no upside beyond promised payments — their exposure is asymmetric (downside only). Equity, by contrast, has theoretically unlimited upside. Leverage amplifies ROE when asset returns exceed the cost of debt, but also magnifies losses when revenues decline, illustrating the risk-return tradeoff inherent in capital structure decisions.

Stakeholder theory broadens corporate governance beyond shareholder primacy. Key stakeholder groups include shareholders (residual claimants with voting rights), lenders (public bondholders and private debtholders such as banks), the board of directors, senior managers, employees, customers, suppliers, and governments. Each group has distinct interests and potential conflicts with others. Private debtholders often have access to non-public information, reducing information asymmetry, which makes them especially important financing sources for smaller firms. Bondholders rely solely on public information and protect themselves primarily through covenants.

The board of directors serves as the central governance mechanism, responsible for hiring and overseeing senior management, setting strategic direction, and protecting shareholder interests. Boards may be structured as one-tier (inside and independent directors together) or two-tier (supervisory and management boards, common in continental Europe). Staggered boards reduce shareholder power to rapidly overhaul board composition. Senior managers are incentivized through salary, performance bonuses, and perks, aligning their interests with firm profitability — though agency conflicts remain a core concern.

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) factors have become central to how companies interact with all stakeholders. ESG considerations influence investment analysis, risk assessment, and corporate strategy. Companies face growing pressure from shareholders, regulators, and the public to integrate ESG factors into their operations and reporting. Greenwashing — misrepresenting ESG credentials — is a key risk. ESG integration can affect firm value through regulatory exposure, reputational risk, and long-term operational sustainability.
key_points: Debtholders have a senior, contractual claim; equity holders have a residual claim — in default, debt is paid first and equity can go to zero.
Debt has no upside beyond promised payments; equity has unlimited upside — this asymmetry drives the fundamental conflict between debtholders and shareholders.
Leverage increases ROE when return on assets exceeds the cost of debt, but amplifies losses when revenues fall — illustrated by the 50/50 vs. 100% equity ROE examples.
Debtholders protect themselves through covenants (e.g., maximum leverage ratios, minimum interest coverage ratios) because taking on more risk does not increase their return.
Private debtholders (banks) have access to non-public information and can renegotiate covenants; public bondholders rely only on public information and have little operational influence.
Board independence is critical: one-tier boards (U.S./U.K.) must have a majority of independent directors; two-tier boards (continental Europe) separate supervisory and management functions.
ESG integration encompasses environmental impact, social responsibility, and governance quality — greenwashing (false ESG claims) is a material risk that analysts must identify.

readings.title: Corporate Governance: Conflicts, Mechanisms, Risks,
readings.summary:
Corporate governance refers to the system of internal controls and procedures by which companies are managed, defining the rights, roles, and responsibilities of various groups within an organization. At its core is the principal-agent relationship, where one party (the principal) hires another (the agent) to act on their behalf. Because agents may have interests that diverge from principals, agency conflicts arise. Agency costs—both direct (monitoring costs) and indirect (opportunity costs)—are the result. In the corporate context, shareholders are the principals and managers/directors are their agents.

Three major types of stakeholder conflicts exist. First, shareholders vs. managers/directors: managers may exert insufficient effort, empire-build through acquisitions tied to compensation based on firm size, entrench themselves, or engage in self-dealing. Information asymmetry worsens this conflict since managers know more about the firm than shareholders. Second, controlling vs. minority shareholders: a dominant shareholder may exploit voting power—especially in dual-class share structures—to benefit themselves at minority shareholders' expense. CFA Institute opposes dual-class structures for this reason. Third, creditors vs. shareholders: shareholders may prefer higher risk since they benefit from the upside, while creditors bear more downside. Actions like issuing new debt or raising dividends can harm existing creditors.

Corporate governance mechanisms exist to mitigate these conflicts. Key mechanisms include: annual general meetings (AGMs) where shareholders vote on resolutions and receive financial disclosures; proxy voting that allows absent shareholders to exercise their votes; board structure with a majority of independent directors and separate audit, remuneration, and nomination committees; and transparent public reporting (annual reports, proxy statements) to reduce information asymmetry. Shareholder rights such as voting on major transactions, approving director compensation, and supermajority requirements for fundamental changes are also protective mechanisms.

Governance risks arise when these mechanisms fail. Poor governance leads to misaligned incentives, value destruction, reputational damage, and legal liability. Benefits of strong governance include lower cost of capital, better operational performance, reduced agency costs, and improved stakeholder trust. ESG (environmental, social, and governance) factors are increasingly used by investors to assess governance quality. Companies with strong governance practices tend to have better long-term performance and are less prone to scandals or fraud.
key_points: Principal-agent conflict arises when an agent's interests diverge from the principal's; in corporations, shareholders are principals and managers/directors are agents. Agency costs can be direct (monitoring) or indirect (opportunity costs).

Three key conflict types to memorize: (1) Shareholders vs. managers—empire building, entrenchment, self-dealing, insufficient effort; (2) Controlling vs. minority shareholders—dual-class shares let controlling shareholders extract value; (3) Creditors vs. shareholders—shareholders prefer more risk since they capture the upside, creditors bear the downside.

Information asymmetry is worse for large, complex, multi-segment companies and those with low institutional ownership—this limits shareholders' and non-executive directors' ability to monitor management.
CFA Institute opposes dual-class share structures because they allow one shareholder group to benefit at others' expense by decoupling voting power from economic interest.

Key governance mechanisms: annual general meetings, proxy voting, independent board majority, separate audit/remuneration/nomination committees, and transparent public reporting (annual reports, proxy statements).

Ordinary resolutions require a simple majority; special/supermajority resolutions (e.g., mergers, charter amendments) require a higher threshold—know this distinction for exam questions on shareholder voting.

Strong corporate governance benefits: lower cost of capital, reduced agency costs, better operational performance, and enhanced stakeholder trust. Weak governance increases legal, reputational, and financial risk.

readings.title: Working Capital And Liquidity
readings.summary:
The cash conversion cycle (CCC) measures how efficiently a company converts its investments in inventory and other resources into cash. It is calculated as Days of Inventory on Hand (DOH) plus Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) minus Days Payable Outstanding (DPO). A lower CCC is generally better, indicating faster cash generation and less capital tied up in working capital. Companies can shorten their CCC by reducing inventory, tightening credit to customers, or extending payables — but each approach carries trade-offs such as production bottlenecks, lost sales, or strained supplier relationships.

Supplier payment terms (e.g., 2/10 net 30) create implicit financing. If a company forgoes an early-payment discount, it effectively borrows from the supplier at a potentially very high effective annual rate (EAR). The EAR formula is: (1 + a/(1−a))^(365/(c−b)) − 1. In most cases, this implicit financing cost far exceeds explicit bank borrowing rates, so companies should typically borrow from banks to capture early-payment discounts.

Liquidity for a corporate issuer refers to its ability to meet short-term obligations using available cash and liquid assets. Primary sources include cash on hand, marketable securities, bank credit lines, and operating cash flows. Secondary sources — such as suspending dividends, selling assets, issuing equity, or restructuring debt — are more costly and signal financial stress to the market. Cash and marketable securities act as a buffer against CCC fluctuations caused by seasonality or demand shocks.

Drags on liquidity occur when cash inflows slow (rising DOH or DSO), while pulls on liquidity occur when cash outflows accelerate (falling DPO). Three key liquidity ratios measure a firm's short-term health with increasing stringency: the current ratio (current assets / current liabilities), the quick ratio (excludes inventory), and the cash ratio (excludes both inventory and receivables). Because these ratios vary widely by industry, comparisons are only meaningful within the same sector or across time for the same company.
key_points: CCC = DOH + DSO − DPO; a lower CCC means faster cash generation and less capital tied up in working capital.
EAR of supplier financing = (1 + a/(1−a))^(365/(c−b)) − 1; this rate is typically much higher than bank borrowing, so companies should borrow to take early-payment discounts.

Primary liquidity sources (cash, marketable securities, bank lines, operating cash flow) are preferred; secondary sources (asset sales, equity issuance, debt restructuring) are costlier and send negative market signals.
Drag on liquidity = cash inflows slow (DOH or DSO rises); Pull on liquidity = cash outflows accelerate (DPO falls).
Three liquidity ratios in order of stringency: Current Ratio (CA/CL) > Quick Ratio (excludes inventory) > Cash Ratio (excludes inventory and receivables).
CCC comparisons are only valid within the same industry; airlines have low CCCs (prepaid sales, no inventory), while pharmaceutical companies have high CCCs (large inventories of high-margin drugs).
Net working capital uses only operating current assets minus operating current liabilities and is closely linked to the CCC; it is preferred over total working capital for analytical purposes.

readings.title: Capital Investments And Capital Allocation
readings.summary:
Capital investments fall into four categories: going concern projects (maintaining operations or reducing costs), regulatory/compliance projects (required by government or insurers, minimal revenue), expansion projects (entering new markets or launching products, requiring detailed demand forecasting), and other projects (outside existing business lines, similar to startups or acquisitions with high uncertainty). Going concern projects require little analysis; expansion and other projects demand rigorous cash flow forecasting and risk assessment.

The capital allocation process is a four-step framework: (1) idea generation from internal and external sources, (2) analyzing project proposals via cash flow forecasting, (3) creating a firm-wide capital budget aligned with strategy and resources, and (4) monitoring outcomes through post-audits to detect systematic forecasting errors. This process is considered the most critical financial management responsibility because it determines long-term firm success and shareholder value.

The three primary capital allocation metrics are NPV, IRR, and ROIC. NPV is the sum of present values of incremental after-tax cash flows discounted at the firm's risk-adjusted cost of capital — accept if NPV > 0. IRR is the discount rate that sets NPV to zero — accept if IRR exceeds the hurdle rate. Both NPV and IRR give the same accept/reject decision for independent, conventional projects, but NPV is theoretically superior because it measures absolute wealth creation and assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital rather than the IRR.

Conflicts between NPV and IRR arise in mutually exclusive projects due to differences in project scale or timing of cash flows. In these cases, NPV is the preferred decision criterion because it directly measures the dollar increase in shareholder wealth. The IRR can also produce multiple solutions when a project has non-conventional cash flows (more than one sign change), making it unreliable. ROIC (NOPAT / invested capital) measures operating efficiency and value creation relative to capital employed, serving as a complementary performance metric rather than a primary selection tool.

Key practical considerations include the use of incremental after-tax cash flows (not accounting profits), proper treatment of sunk costs (excluded) and opportunity costs (included), and adjustment of the discount rate for project-specific risk. The match funding principle suggests financing projects with capital sources whose duration matches the project life to reduce financing risk. Annual depreciation expense is commonly used as a proxy for going concern capital investment needs.
key_points: Four types of capital investments: going concern (maintain/reduce costs), regulatory/compliance (little revenue, no choice), expansion (new markets/products, complex analysis), and other (new business lines, startup-like risk).
NPV decision rule: Accept if NPV > 0 (increases shareholder wealth); reject if NPV < 0. NPV uses the firm's risk-adjusted cost of capital as the discount rate.
IRR decision rule: Accept if IRR > hurdle rate (required rate of return); reject if IRR < hurdle rate. IRR is the discount rate that sets NPV = 0.

When NPV and IRR conflict on mutually exclusive projects, always choose the higher-NPV project — NPV is superior because it measures absolute wealth creation and assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital (more realistic than IRR's reinvestment assumption).
Non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes) can produce multiple IRRs, making IRR unreliable — use NPV in these cases.
Sunk costs are excluded from capital allocation analysis; opportunity costs and externalities (cannibalization) must be included as incremental cash flows.
The four-step capital allocation process: idea generation → project analysis (cash flow forecast) → firm-wide capital budget → post-audit monitoring to improve future forecasts.

readings.title: Capital Structure Module 27.1: Weighted-Average
readings.summary:
The weighted-average cost of capital (WACC) is a blended rate that combines a firm's after-tax cost of debt and cost of equity, weighted by their proportions in the capital structure. The formula is: WACC = [weight of debt × pretax cost of debt × (1 − tax rate)] + (weight of equity × cost of equity). Debt is cheaper than equity because it has priority of claims, and interest expense is tax-deductible, making the after-tax cost of debt even lower. Weights can be based on target capital structure or market values, with market value weights preferred for estimating the current opportunity cost of capital.

A company's optimal capital structure minimizes its WACC while matching financing duration to asset life. The key internal factors affecting capital structure are the stability and growth of revenues and cash flows, amount of business risk, asset type and liquidity (tangible vs. intangible, collateral value), existing debt levels, and the corporate tax rate. External factors include macroeconomic conditions, credit market cycles, regulation, and industry norms.

Companies with more stable, predictable, recurring revenues — such as subscription-based businesses or those in noncyclical industries with low operating leverage — can support higher debt proportions. Tangible, liquid, fungible assets make better collateral and improve access to cheap debt. Companies that own assets outright (vs. franchise models) have more collateral advantage. Leverage ratios (D/E, D/EBITDA) and coverage ratios (interest coverage = EBIT/interest) are key tools for assessing debt capacity.

A firm's life cycle stage strongly influences its capital structure. Start-ups rely almost entirely on equity due to high business risk, low/negative cash flows, and limited collateral. Growth-stage companies begin accessing secured debt as revenues rise and risk falls. Mature companies enjoy stable cash flows, low business risk, and broad access to unsecured debt at relatively low costs.
