Top-down macroeconomic factors — inflation, real GDP growth, monetary policy, exchange rates — also affect benchmark rates and credit spreads, with spreads widening during economic downturns, especially for cyclical industries.
key_points: WACC formula: [Wd × Kd × (1 − t)] + [We × Ke]. Always use after-tax cost of debt because interest is tax-deductible.
Debt is cheaper than equity due to priority of claims AND the tax shield on interest expense.
Market value weights are preferred over book value weights for WACC because they reflect current opportunity cost of capital.
Higher debt capacity is associated with: stable/recurring revenues, low business risk, low operating leverage, tangible/liquid assets, and noncyclical industries.
Life cycle and debt: Start-up = equity only (high risk, no collateral); Growth = some secured debt; Mature = broad access to unsecured debt.
Key ratios for debt capacity analysis: leverage ratios (D/E, D/operating profit) and coverage ratios (interest coverage = EBIT / interest expense).
Top-down factors (inflation, GDP growth, monetary policy) affect benchmark rates and credit spreads — spreads widen in downturns, especially for cyclical issuers.

readings.title: Business Models Module 28.1: Business Model
readings.summary:
A business model explains how a firm provides a product or service, finds customers, delivers its offering, and generates profit. The framework for analyzing a business model answers five key questions: who are the customers (segmenting), what is the product/service and its differentiation, how does the firm operate using key assets and suppliers, where and how does the firm sell (channel strategy), and how much does it charge (pricing strategy). Firms selling to other businesses are B2B, while those selling to consumers are B2C. A strategy using both digital and physical channels is called omnichannel.

Pricing is a central feature of any business model. Commodity producers are price takers due to undifferentiated products, while firms with patents or strong differentiation enjoy pricing power. Price discrimination strategies include tiered pricing, dynamic pricing, value-based pricing, and auction pricing. Multi-product pricing models include bundling, razors-and-blades (cheap hardware, high-margin consumables), and add-on pricing. Other strategies include penetration pricing (low initial price to gain share), freemium (basic for free, charge for premium features), and hidden revenue models (free content funded by advertising).

Alternatives to outright purchase include subscription models (e.g., Microsoft Office 365), licensing (e.g., biotech firms licensing drugs to established manufacturers), and franchising (franchisee pays a royalty for rights to sell in a territory). A firm's value proposition describes how customers value its offering relative to competitors, and the value chain describes how the firm executes that proposition through inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing, and sales and service (Porter's five value chain activities).

Beyond conventional industry-specific models, innovative business models are often introduced by new entrants leveraging technology. Notable types include private label/contract manufacturing, value-added resellers, and network-effect businesses (e.g., WhatsApp, Airbnb, eBay) where value grows as the user base expands. Two-sided or multi-sided networks connect distinct user groups. Crowdsourcing models (e.g., Wikipedia, Waze, open-source software) harness user contributions to create value, and SaaS and ultra-low-cost airlines are cited as disruptive innovations that challenged incumbents with new business model structures.
key_points: Business model framework = Who (customer segmentation), What (product/differentiation), How (assets/suppliers), Where (channel strategy), How Much (pricing) — memorize all five dimensions.
Channel strategy types: direct sales vs. intermediaries (wholesalers, retailers, agents, franchisees); omnichannel = both digital and physical channels combined.

Pricing models to distinguish: penetration (low price for market share), freemium (free basic + paid premium), hidden revenue (free to user, revenue from ads), razors-and-blades (cheap device + high-margin consumable), bundling, add-on pricing.
Alternative revenue models: subscription (recurring access fee), licensing (IP rights for a fee), franchising (territory rights + royalty to franchisor).
Network effects = value of a network increases as its user base grows; supports penetration pricing strategy; two-sided/multi-sided networks (e.g., Airbnb) connect multiple distinct user groups.

Porter's value chain has five activities: (1) Inbound logistics, (2) Operations, (3) Outbound logistics, (4) Marketing, (5) Sales and service — firm competitive advantage comes from executing these well.
Value proposition = how customers value the product vs. competitors; value chain = how the firm executes that proposition through its assets and organization.

readings.title: Analyzing Income Statements Module 30.1: Revenue Recognition
readings.summary: Revenue recognition follows the accrual principle: revenue is recognized when earned, not necessarily when cash is received. If goods are sold on credit, revenue is recognized at the point of sale and an accounts receivable asset is created. Conversely, if cash is received before goods or services are delivered, a liability called unearned revenue is recorded and converted to revenue only as the performance obligation is fulfilled — the magazine subscription model is the classic example.
key_points: The 5-step revenue recognition process: (1) Identify contract, (2) Identify performance obligations, (3) Determine transaction price, (4) Allocate price to obligations, (5) Recognize revenue when each obligation is satisfied.
Revenue is recognized when control of a good or service transfers to the customer — indicators include physical possession, legal title, customer acceptance, and seller's right to payment.

Under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP, 'collectability must be probable' for a contract to exist, but 'probable' is defined differently — identical transactions can be recorded differently under each framework.

For long-term contracts, revenue is recognized based on progress toward completion, measured either by inputs (e.g., % of costs incurred) or outputs (e.g., engineering milestones). Contract acquisition costs (e.g., sales commissions) must be capitalized and expensed over the contract life.

Agent vs. principal distinction: an agent reports only net commission as revenue; a principal reports gross revenue and a corresponding expense. Gross profit is the same, but gross margin differs significantly.
A firm must recognize a refund liability (and offsetting right-to-return asset) when revenue cannot be reliably estimated — revenue should only be recognized when reversal is highly improbable.

Licensing and franchising: upfront license fees may be recognized immediately if the license provides a right to use IP at a point in time; royalties and usage-based fees are recognized as the underlying activity occurs.

readings.title: Analyzing Balance Sheets Module 31.1: Intangible Assets And
readings.summary:
Intangible assets are nonmonetary, non-physical assets that are either identifiable (patents, trademarks, copyrights — separable or arising from rights) or unidentifiable (goodwill — inseparable, potentially unlimited life). Under IFRS, purchased identifiable intangibles may be carried at cost or fair value via the revaluation model (only if an active market exists), while U.S. GAAP permits only the cost model. Internally generated intangibles are generally expensed, with a key IFRS exception: development-stage costs may be capitalized if the project meets feasibility, resource, market, and intent criteria — whereas U.S. GAAP requires expensing all R&D.

Finite-lived intangibles are amortized over their useful lives and tested for impairment like PP&E, with annual reviews of method and life estimates. Indefinite-lived intangibles (including goodwill) are never amortized but must be tested for impairment at least annually. Both IFRS and U.S. GAAP require immediate expensing of start-up costs, advertising, administrative overhead, relocation, and termination costs.

Goodwill arises solely from business acquisitions and equals the purchase price minus the fair value of identifiable net assets acquired. It reflects unrecorded assets (brand, customer loyalty), off-balance-sheet R&D, and expected synergies. Goodwill is not amortized; impairment losses flow through the income statement but do not affect cash flow. Acquirers may manipulate earnings by over-allocating purchase price to goodwill (reducing depreciable asset bases and future D&A expense).

Financial instruments on the asset side include investment securities, derivatives, loans, and receivables, measured at historical cost, amortized cost, or fair value depending on classification. Trading securities and securities designated at fair value through profit or loss (FVTPL) are marked to market with gains/losses in the income statement. Available-for-sale (AFS) / fair value through other comprehensive income (FVOCI) securities carry unrealized gains/losses in OCI, bypassing net income. Held-to-maturity (HTM) / amortized cost securities are carried at amortized cost with no mark-to-market.

For analytical purposes, analysts should eliminate goodwill from balance sheets and goodwill impairment from income statements to improve comparability across firms. They should also assess each intangible asset individually rather than automatically writing them off, and evaluate acquisitions by comparing price paid to the earning power of acquired assets. Examining the history of goodwill impairments is a useful tool for judging the quality of a company's past acquisition decisions.
key_points: IFRS allows capitalization of DEVELOPMENT-stage R&D costs if technically feasible, resourced, marketable, and intended — RESEARCH stage is always expensed; U.S. GAAP expenses ALL R&D.

Goodwill = Purchase Price − Fair Value of Identifiable Net Assets; only created in acquisitions, never amortized, tested for impairment at least annually; impairment loss hits income statement but NOT cash flow.
Indefinite-lived intangibles (including goodwill): NO amortization, annual impairment test. Finite-lived intangibles: amortize over useful life + impairment test like PP&E.
Under IFRS, purchased intangibles can use cost model OR revaluation model (active market required); U.S. GAAP permits cost model ONLY.
Trading / FVTPL securities: unrealized gains/losses go through net income. AFS / FVOCI securities: unrealized gains/losses go through OCI (not net income). HTM / amortized cost: no mark-to-market.
Always expense as incurred (both IFRS & GAAP): start-up costs, advertising, admin overhead, relocation, reorganization, and termination costs.
For ratio analysis: eliminate goodwill from the balance sheet and goodwill impairment charges from the income statement to improve cross-company comparability.

readings.title: Analyzing Statements Of Cash Flows I
readings.summary:
The cash flow statement bridges the gap between accrual-based income statements and actual cash movements, reconciling beginning and ending cash balances on the balance sheet across three activity types: operating (CFO), investing (CFI), and financing (CFF). Analysts use it to assess liquidity, solvency, and financial flexibility — determining whether a firm generates enough cash to sustain operations, service debt, and respond to new opportunities. High earnings quality is indicated when CFO is close to or exceeds reported net income; persistent gaps where net income exceeds CFO signal accruals-heavy earnings not backed by real cash generation.

Cash flow from operations is linked to current assets and current liabilities on the balance sheet, while investing activities relate to noncurrent assets and financing activities relate to noncurrent liabilities and equity. Timing differences between revenue/expense recognition and actual cash receipt/payment create changes in balance sheet accounts. For example, sales exceeding cash collections increase accounts receivable, while cash collections exceeding sales decrease it. The key formula — Beginning AR + Sales − Cash Collections = Ending AR — allows analysts to solve for any unknown variable given the other three.

CFO can be presented using either the direct or indirect method; CFI and CFF are presented identically under both. The direct method lists actual cash inflows and outflows (e.g., cash collected from customers, cash paid for COGS, interest, and taxes), providing more transparency for forecasting. To compute each line item, start with the income statement figure, then adjust for changes in related balance sheet accounts using the rules: increases in assets are uses of cash (subtract); decreases in assets are sources of cash (add); increases in liabilities are sources of cash (add); decreases in liabilities are uses of cash (subtract). These adjustments convert accrual-based figures into cash-basis amounts.

Unearned revenue is an important special case: cash received in advance from customers appears in CFO when received, but revenue recognition is deferred to the income statement over the contract life. Understanding these interrelationships is also crucial for detecting earnings manipulation, as firms can obscure cash flow problems through aggressive accrual accounting. Analysts who reconcile income statement items to cash flows are better equipped to evaluate true financial performance.
key_points: High earnings quality: CFO ≥ Net Income. If net income consistently exceeds CFO, earnings are accruals-heavy and lower quality.
Balance sheet linkage: Operating activities → current assets/liabilities; Investing → noncurrent assets; Financing → noncurrent liabilities and equity.
AR cash collections formula: Beginning AR + Sales − Cash Collections = Ending AR. Rearranged: Cash Collections = Sales − ΔAR.
Direct method adjustment rules: Increase in asset = subtract (use of cash); Decrease in asset = add (source). Increase in liability = add (source); Decrease in liability = subtract (use).
CFO differs between direct and indirect methods in presentation only — the final CFO number is identical under both methods. CFI and CFF are always presented the same way regardless of method.
Unearned revenue: Cash appears in CFO when customer pays (in advance); revenue hits the income statement later over the contract life — an increase in unearned revenue is a source of cash.
The cash flow statement reconciles beginning and ending cash on the balance sheet: ΔCash = CFO + CFI + CFF.

readings.title: Analyzing Statements Of Cash Flows Ii
readings.summary:
The cash flow statement is a critical tool for assessing a firm's liquidity, solvency, and financial flexibility. Analysts use it to determine whether operations generate sufficient cash to sustain the business, service existing debt, and meet unexpected obligations. Cash flow patterns change across a firm's life cycle: early-stage firms often have negative operating cash flow funded by external financing, but mature firms must generate operating cash flows that exceed capital expenditures and provide returns to all capital providers.

A key analytical task is evaluating the quality of operating cash flow. Earnings-related activities should be the primary driver of CFO; however, firms can temporarily boost CFO by drawing down working capital (liquidating inventory and receivables or stretching payables). This is unsustainable. A stable relationship between net income and CFO signals high earnings quality, while net income that significantly exceeds CFO may indicate aggressive revenue recognition or expense deferral.

Investing and financing sections also carry important signals. Rising capital expenditures typically indicate growth, while cuts to capex or asset sales may signal cash stress and could require costly future replacements. The financing section reveals whether a firm is raising new debt or equity, repaying obligations, buying back stock, or paying dividends—context that matters greatly when evaluating capital allocation decisions.

Common-size cash flow statements express each line item as a percentage of revenue (or alternatively as a percentage of total inflows/outflows). The revenue-based format is especially useful for spotting trends and forecasting future cash flows, since items tied to revenue can be projected once a revenue forecast is established.

Free cash flow measures capture cash available after capital expenditures. Free cash flow to the firm (FCFF) represents cash available to all investors and equals CFO plus after-tax interest paid minus fixed capital investment. Free cash flow to equity (FCFE) represents cash available to common shareholders and equals CFO minus fixed capital investment plus net borrowing. These measures underpin valuation models and are heavily tested on the exam alongside cash flow coverage and performance ratios.
key_points: FCFF = CFO + [Interest Paid × (1 − Tax Rate)] − Fixed Capital Investment. It is cash available to BOTH debt and equity holders; after-tax interest is added back because it belongs to debtholders.
FCFE = CFO − Fixed Capital Investment + Net Borrowing (subtract net borrowing if negative). It is cash available to common shareholders only.
Earnings quality check: if net income consistently and significantly exceeds CFO, suspect aggressive accounting (early revenue recognition or deferred expense recognition).
Working capital liquidation (drawing down inventory/receivables or stretching payables) temporarily boosts CFO but is NOT sustainable—watch for this as a red flag.
Common-size cash flow statements use revenue as the base; each line item is expressed as % of revenue to identify trends and enable forecasting once revenue is projected.
Under IFRS, interest paid may be classified as financing (not operating), so no CFO adjustment is needed when computing FCFF; also add back dividends paid if the firm included them in CFO.
Increasing capex = growth signal; reducing capex or selling assets = potential cash stress, with risk of higher future replacement costs—always investigate the reason.

readings.title: Analysis Of Inventories Introduction
readings.summary:
Reading 34 covers how inventory is measured and reported under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP. Under IFRS, inventory is carried at the lower of cost or net realizable value (NRV), where NRV = expected sales price minus selling and completion costs. If inventory value falls below cost, it is written down and the loss flows through the income statement; importantly, IFRS allows subsequent write-ups (reversals) up to the original cost if value recovers. Under U.S. GAAP, non-LIFO/non-retail companies also use lower of cost or NRV, while LIFO and retail method companies use lower of cost or market, where 'market' is constrained to a range between NRV minus a normal profit margin (floor) and NRV (ceiling). U.S. GAAP prohibits any write-up after a write-down, regardless of recovery.

The choice of inventory cost flow method — FIFO, LIFO, or weighted average — has significant implications during periods of inflation or deflation. Under inflation, LIFO produces higher COGS, lower ending inventory, lower net income, and lower taxes compared to FIFO. FIFO better reflects current inventory values on the balance sheet. Analysts often convert LIFO financials to FIFO using the LIFO reserve (disclosed in footnotes): FIFO inventory = LIFO inventory + LIFO reserve; FIFO COGS = LIFO COGS minus the change in LIFO reserve. This adjustment enables meaningful cross-company comparisons.

Inventory write-downs affect a wide range of financial ratios in the period they occur. A write-down increases COGS, reducing gross margin, operating margin, and net margin. Because inventory is a current asset, both current assets and total assets fall, increasing asset turnover and debt ratios. The current ratio drops while the quick ratio is unaffected. Inventory turnover increases (which may look favorable but is distorted). ROA and ROE both decline in the write-down period. In subsequent periods, lower carrying values reduce COGS, boosting profitability metrics and inflating ROA/ROE — analysts must be alert to this mechanical effect when comparing ratios across periods.
key_points: IFRS: lower of cost or NRV; write-downs allowed AND reversals (write-ups) allowed up to original cost. U.S. GAAP: NO write-up reversals are ever permitted after a write-down.
U.S. GAAP 'market' for LIFO/retail method is bounded: floor = NRV minus normal profit margin; ceiling = NRV. Market is typically replacement cost if it falls within this range.
LIFO reserve adjustment to convert to FIFO: FIFO Inventory = LIFO Inventory + LIFO reserve; FIFO COGS = LIFO COGS − ΔLIFO reserve (increase in reserve).
Under inflation: LIFO → higher COGS, lower taxes, lower net income, lower ending inventory. FIFO → lower COGS, higher net income, higher ending inventory (closer to current replacement cost).

Inventory write-down effects in write-down period: current ratio ↓, inventory turnover ↑, asset turnover ↑, debt/assets ↑, debt/equity ↑, gross/operating/net margin ↓, ROA ↓, ROE ↓. Quick ratio is UNAFFECTED.
Post-write-down periods: lower inventory carrying values reduce future COGS → inflated ROA and ROE; analysts must adjust for this distortion when comparing profitability across periods.
LIFO firms are LESS likely to record inventory write-downs than FIFO firms because LIFO ending inventory is already based on older, lower costs — less likely to exceed NRV.

readings.title: Analysis Of Long-Term Assets Introduction
readings.summary:
Reading 35 covers the financial reporting and analysis of long-term assets, focusing on intangible assets, impairment, and derecognition. Intangible assets lack physical substance (patents, brand names, copyrights) and may have either finite or indefinite lives. Finite-lived intangibles are amortized over their useful life, while indefinite-lived intangibles are not amortized but must be tested for impairment at least annually. Intangibles can be identifiable (separable or arising from contractual/legal rights) or unidentifiable (e.g., goodwill), and this distinction drives how they are reported on the balance sheet.

The accounting treatment for intangible assets depends heavily on how they were obtained. Internally created intangibles are generally expensed as incurred under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP, with key exceptions for development costs (capitalizable under IFRS if specific criteria are met) and software development costs (capitalized after technological feasibility under U.S. GAAP, or after probable completion for internal-use software). Purchased intangibles are capitalized at fair value. In a business combination, the acquisition method requires allocating the purchase price to all identifiable assets and liabilities at fair value; any excess purchase price becomes goodwill, which is only recognized when arising from a business combination — never for internally generated goodwill.

Impairment occurs when an asset's carrying value exceeds its recoverable amount. Under IFRS, the recoverable amount is the higher of fair value less costs to sell and value in use; under U.S. GAAP, impairment is recognized when carrying value exceeds undiscounted future cash flows, and the loss equals the difference between carrying value and fair value. Impairment losses reduce net income and asset values in the period recognized. IFRS permits impairment reversals for assets other than goodwill; U.S. GAAP does not allow reversals for any long-lived assets.

Derecognition occurs when an asset is sold or disposed of. A gain or loss is recognized on the income statement equal to the difference between the proceeds received and the asset's carrying value at the time of disposal. Analysts should be aware that gains or losses on asset sales are non-recurring items that can distort profitability ratios. The choice between the cost model and revaluation model (IFRS only) also significantly affects reported asset values, depreciation expense, and equity, creating comparability challenges between IFRS and U.S. GAAP reporters.
key_points: Finite-lived intangibles are AMORTIZED; indefinite-lived intangibles are NOT amortized but tested for impairment at least annually.
Under IFRS, research costs are EXPENSED; development costs may be CAPITALIZED if specific criteria are met. Under U.S. GAAP, both R&D are expensed (except software after technological feasibility).
Goodwill = Purchase Price − Fair Value of Net Identifiable Assets acquired; only recognized in a business combination, NEVER for internally generated goodwill.
IFRS impairment loss = Carrying Value − Recoverable Amount (higher of fair value less costs to sell vs. value in use); U.S. GAAP uses a two-step test with no reversal allowed.
IFRS allows impairment REVERSALS for PP&E and intangibles (excluding goodwill); U.S. GAAP prohibits reversals for all long-lived assets.

Capitalizing vs. expensing: capitalizing increases assets, equity, and net income in year one, but reduces net income in later years via depreciation/amortization; operating cash flow is higher when costs are capitalized.
A company that internally generates intangibles will show LOWER balance sheet assets than a comparable company that purchases intangibles, distorting ratio comparisons (ROA, asset turnover, etc.).

readings.title: Topics In Long-Term Liabilities And Equity
readings.summary:
This reading focuses on the financial reporting of leases under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP, examining the treatment from both the lessee's and lessor's perspectives. A lease gives a lessee the right to use a specific asset for a defined period in exchange for periodic payments, and must meet three criteria: it must reference a specific asset, grant the lessee substantially all economic benefits, and give the lessee the right to direct the asset's use. Leases are classified as either finance leases (where substantially all benefits and risks of ownership transfer to the lessee) or operating leases (where they do not), with five specific conditions triggering finance lease classification.

For lessees under IFRS, all leases (except short-term leases ≤12 months and low-value assets ≤$5,000) require recognition of a right-of-use (ROU) asset and a lease liability on the balance sheet, both initially measured at the present value of future lease payments. Under a finance lease, the ROU asset is amortized straight-line while the lease liability is amortized like a loan — producing separate amortization and interest expense on the income statement. Under U.S. GAAP for operating leases, the ROU asset and liability remain equal throughout the lease, and the combined cost is reported as a single 'lease expense' equal to each periodic payment.

Cash flow classification differs importantly between lease types and accounting standards. For a finance lease, principal repayment is a financing outflow; under IFRS, interest may be operating or financing, while under U.S. GAAP, interest is always operating. For an operating lease under U.S. GAAP, the entire lease payment is classified as an operating outflow. These differences affect key financial ratios, making it critical to understand how each classification impacts leverage, coverage, and profitability metrics.

From the lessor's perspective, finance leases result in removal of the asset from the balance sheet and recognition of a lease receivable, with the lease treated as a combination of a sale and financing arrangement generating interest income. Operating leases keep the asset on the lessor's balance sheet, with lease payments recognized as rental income over the lease term. The reading also covers other long-term liability and equity topics such as debt covenants, bond issuance at premium or discount, and the distinction between debt and equity instruments including preferred stock and convertible securities.
key_points: Finance lease classification requires ANY ONE of five conditions: ownership transfers, bargain purchase option, lease covers most of asset's useful life, PV of payments ≥ fair value of asset, or asset is specialized with no alternative use for the lessor.

Under IFRS, ALL leases (except ≤12-month or ≤$5,000 low-value) require a ROU asset and lease liability on the balance sheet at PV of lease payments — there is no off-balance-sheet operating lease treatment for lessees.

Finance lease vs. operating lease income statement impact: finance lease shows separate amortization expense + interest expense (front-loaded total cost); operating lease shows equal lease expense each period matching the payment amount.

Cash flow statement: finance lease splits payments into financing (principal) and operating (interest under U.S. GAAP) outflows; operating lease under U.S. GAAP classifies the ENTIRE payment as operating outflow — making operating CF look worse for operating leases.

For the lessor: finance lease removes the asset from the balance sheet (replaced by a lease receivable) and generates interest income; operating lease keeps the asset on the balance sheet and records rental income straight-line.

Key ratio effect: finance leases increase reported assets and liabilities versus operating leases, increasing leverage ratios (D/E) and reducing asset turnover, but improving operating cash flow relative to operating lease treatment.

IFRS vs. U.S. GAAP lessee difference: under IFRS, operating leases are treated the same as finance leases on the balance sheet (ROU asset ≠ liability over time); under U.S. GAAP operating leases, the ROU asset always equals the lease liability, and a single combined lease expense is reported.

readings.title: Analysis Of Income Taxes Introduction
readings.summary:
This reading focuses on the differences between how income taxes are reported for financial accounting purposes versus how they are calculated for tax return purposes. Because IFRS and U.S. GAAP rules differ from tax laws, a company's income tax expense on its income statement will often differ from the actual taxes payable to the government. Understanding the terminology on both sides — tax return terms (taxable income, taxes payable, tax base) and financial reporting terms (accounting profit, income tax expense, carrying value) — is essential for analyzing a company's tax position.

The core concept is the distinction between temporary and permanent differences. Permanent differences arise when items are recognized in one system but never in the other, and they do not create deferred tax assets or liabilities. Temporary differences, by contrast, reverse over time — the same total amount eventually flows through both systems, just in different periods. These timing mismatches create deferred tax liabilities (DTLs) and deferred tax assets (DTAs) on the balance sheet.

A deferred tax liability arises when income tax expense exceeds taxes payable — meaning the company pays less tax now but will owe more in the future. The most classic example is using accelerated depreciation for tax purposes while using straight-line depreciation for financial reporting. In early years, tax depreciation is higher, taxable income is lower, and taxes paid are lower — creating a DTL that reverses in later years when the relationship flips.

A deferred tax asset arises when taxes payable exceed income tax expense — meaning the company pays more tax now than its financial statements recognize, expecting future tax savings. Common causes include warranty expenses (recognized on the income statement before they are tax-deductible), unearned revenue taxed before it is earned, post-employment benefit obligations, and tax loss carryforwards. If it is unlikely a DTA will be realized, a valuation allowance reduces its carrying value on the balance sheet.
key_points: Taxes payable (current tax expense) = actual tax owed to government per the tax return; income tax expense = taxes payable ± changes in DTLs and DTAs on the income statement.
Permanent differences NEVER reverse and do NOT create deferred tax assets or liabilities; temporary differences DO reverse and create DTAs or DTLs.
DTL is created when income tax expense > taxes payable (tax paid now is less; more owed later). Most common cause: accelerated depreciation for tax, straight-line for books.
DTA is created when taxes payable > income tax expense (tax paid now is more; savings come later). Key causes: warranty expense, unearned revenue, post-employment benefits, tax loss carryforwards.
A valuation allowance reduces a DTA when it is more likely than not that the asset will NOT be realized (e.g., company expects future losses). Increasing the allowance reduces net income.
Tax base = value of asset or liability for tax purposes; carrying value = book value on the balance sheet. The gap between these two values drives temporary differences.
A change in the enacted tax rate requires immediate revaluation of all existing DTAs and DTLs on the balance sheet, with the adjustment flowing through income tax expense in that period.

readings.title: Financial Reporting Quality Module 38.1: Reporting Quality
readings.summary:
Financial reporting quality and the quality of reported results are two distinct but related concepts. Financial reporting quality refers to how well a firm's financial statements adhere to GAAP and serve as decision-useful information. High-quality financial reporting must be both relevant (material and useful to decision-makers) and a faithful representation (complete, neutral, and free of errors). Crucially, GAAP compliance alone does not guarantee high reporting quality, since GAAP allows management significant discretion in methods, estimates, and treatment of items.

The quality of reported earnings is a separate dimension focused on sustainability and adequacy. Sustainable earnings are those expected to continue into future periods — such as gains from improved efficiency or growing market share — whereas one-time items like asset sales or favorable currency moves are considered low-quality earnings. High-quality earnings also must be sufficient to sustain operations and provide adequate returns to investors. A company can therefore have high financial reporting quality (GAAP-compliant, decision-useful) but still have low earnings quality (unsustainable or inadequate earnings).

Financial reporting quality can be assessed along a spectrum from best to worst. At the top are GAAP-compliant, decision-useful reports with sustainable, adequate earnings. Moving down the spectrum, reports may be GAAP-compliant but show low-quality or managed earnings, then non-compliant reports based on actual activities, and finally fictitious or fraudulent reports at the worst end. This six-level framework helps analysts gauge the reliability of both the reporting process and the underlying results.

Within GAAP, management choices can be characterized as conservative (reducing current earnings/assets, deferring recognition) or aggressive (boosting current earnings/assets). Neither is inherently good or bad — both represent deviations from neutral, faithful representation. A common practice called earnings smoothing uses both biases across periods: conservative accounting in high-earnings periods puts profits in the 'cookie jar' to be released in low-earnings periods via aggressive choices. This reduces earnings volatility, which management believes supports higher share valuations. Analysts must identify these biases to properly assess underlying economic performance.
key_points: Financial reporting quality (how statements are prepared) and earnings quality (sustainability and adequacy of results) are two separate dimensions — a firm can have high reporting quality but low earnings quality.
High-quality financial reporting must be decision-useful: both relevant (material, useful for decisions) and a faithful representation (complete, neutral, free of errors).
Memorize the six-level reporting quality spectrum from best to worst: (1) GAAP-compliant, decision-useful, sustainable/adequate earnings → (6) fictitious/fraudulent numbers.

Conservative accounting decreases current-period earnings and balance sheet values (e.g., faster depreciation, higher accruals); aggressive accounting increases them (e.g., slower depreciation, lower reserves). Both deviate from neutrality.

Earnings smoothing uses conservative bias in strong periods to defer earnings ('cookie jar') and aggressive bias in weak periods to release them — reducing volatility but impairing faithful representation.

Sustainability is the key criterion for earnings quality: gains from efficiency/market share are sustainable; gains from one-time asset sales or currency moves are not, and thus add less value to the firm.
Low-quality earnings warning signs include inadequate liability accruals and overstated asset values — both inflate current earnings while reducing the quality and sustainability of reported results.

readings.title: Financial Analysis Techniques
readings.summary: Financial Analysis Techniques introduces the core analytical tools analysts use to interpret financial statements. The primary tools covered are ratio analysis, common-size analysis, graphical analysis, and regression analysis. Each tool serves to convert raw financial data into formats that support comparisons across firms and over time. Critically, analysts must understand not just how to calculate each metric, but what it measures and whether a higher or lower value is preferable in context.
key_points: Ratios are most useful for raising questions and identifying areas needing investigation — they do not answer questions in isolation; always compare to historical performance or industry peers.

Vertical common-size balance sheet expresses all items as % of total assets; vertical common-size income statement expresses all items as % of sales — gross margin, operating margin, and net margin are directly readable from the income statement version.
Horizontal common-size analysis sets the base year = 1.0 and tracks growth trends over time — useful for spotting relative changes in line items across periods.

DuPont decomposition of ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier (3-factor); the 5-factor version further decomposes into Tax Burden × Interest Burden × EBIT Margin × Asset Turnover × Leverage — know both.

Key ratio categories to know: Activity (efficiency), Liquidity (short-term solvency), Solvency (long-term solvency), Profitability, and Valuation ratios — each category measures a distinct dimension of firm health.

Ratio definitions vary across analysts (e.g., debt may include all liabilities or only interest-bearing debt) — the CFA exam will define the ratio used; consistency within analysis matters more than any single definition.

Limitations of ratios include: not useful in isolation, comparability issues across firms using different accounting methods (especially IFRS vs. GAAP), difficulty with multi-segment companies, and no single ratio is sufficient for conclusions.

readings.title: Introduction To Financial Statement Modeling
readings.summary: A sales-based pro forma model projects a company's future financial statements starting from estimated future revenues. The eight-step process begins with estimating revenue growth, then sequentially estimates COGS, SG&A, financing costs, and income taxes before building the balance sheet from working capital flows, capital expenditures, and net PP&E. The pro forma cash flow statement is the final output, derived from the completed income statement and balance sheet. Each step feeds the next, so errors in revenue estimation cascade through the entire model.
key_points: The 8-step pro forma model order: Revenue → COGS → SG&A → Financing costs → Tax → Balance sheet (working capital) → PP&E/CapEx → Cash flow statement.
Overconfidence bias: analysts have too-narrow confidence intervals; mitigate with peer critique, reviewing past forecast errors, and scenario analysis.
Illusion of control bias: over-complex models hide assumptions and overfit; mitigate by using only variables with proven explanatory power.
Conservatism (anchoring) bias: slow to update forecasts on new information; mitigate with simpler models and periodic error evaluation.
Representativeness bias includes base-rate neglect (ignoring the 'outside view'); analysts should balance company-specific (inside) and industry-level (outside) perspectives.
Confirmation bias: seeking only confirming data; mitigate by reading opposing analysts and being skeptical of management's self-serving representations.
Porter's Five Forces affect prices and costs: low substitutes/high switching costs → pricing power; high supplier power → cost pressure; high entry barriers → sustained economic profits.

readings.title: Market Organization And Structure
readings.summary:
The financial system serves three core functions: enabling entities to save, borrow, raise equity, manage risk, and trade assets; determining equilibrium interest rates that balance supply of savings with demand for borrowing; and allocating capital to its most efficient uses. These functions work best when markets are liquid, transaction costs are low, information is freely available, and contracts are reliably enforced. Participants include individuals, firms, governments, and other entities who use the system to transfer assets and risks across counterparties and over time.

Assets are classified as either financial (securities, derivatives, currencies) or real (real estate, equipment, commodities). Financial securities are further divided into debt (promises to repay) and equity (ownership stakes), and can be public (exchange-traded, regulated) or private (illiquid, unregulated). Markets are categorized by delivery timing (spot vs. forward/futures/options), issuance stage (primary vs. secondary), maturity (money markets ≤1 year vs. capital markets), and asset type (traditional debt/equity vs. alternatives like hedge funds and real estate).

Derivative contracts derive their value from an underlying asset — financial derivatives reference equities, indexes, or debt, while physical derivatives reference commodities. Key derivative types include forwards (customized, OTC), futures (standardized, exchange-traded), options (right but not obligation to buy/sell), swaps (exchange of cash flows), and credit default swaps (insurance against default). Options grant the buyer optionality; the seller (writer) receives a premium in exchange for bearing that obligation.

Financial intermediaries — brokers, dealers, exchanges, clearinghouses, securitizers, depository institutions, and insurance companies — reduce transaction costs, provide liquidity, and manage counterparty risk. Brokers match buyers and sellers for a commission; dealers hold inventory and profit from the bid-ask spread. Clearinghouses act as the central counterparty to both sides of a trade, virtually eliminating counterparty default risk. Securitizers pool assets (e.g., mortgages) and sell interests to investors, redistributing risk and improving liquidity.

Positions in assets can be long (ownership, benefit from price increase) or short (borrowed and sold, benefit from price decrease). Leveraged positions amplify both gains and losses. Margin accounts allow investors to borrow from brokers to purchase securities, with the initial margin requirement setting the minimum equity percentage. If the account equity falls below the maintenance margin, a margin call requires the investor to deposit additional funds or have positions liquidated. Short sellers must also post margin and are subject to margin calls if the shorted security rises in price.
key_points: Three functions of the financial system: (1) facilitate saving/borrowing/risk management/asset exchange, (2) determine equilibrium interest rates, (3) allocate capital efficiently.
Asset classification: Financial assets (securities, derivatives, currencies) vs. Real assets (real estate, commodities, equipment); debt securities = promises to repay, equity = ownership.
Market types to memorize: spot vs. forward/futures/options; primary vs. secondary; money market (≤1 yr) vs. capital market (>1 yr); traditional vs. alternative.
Derivative types: forwards (OTC, customized), futures (exchange-traded, standardized), options (right not obligation — buyer pays premium), swaps (exchange cash flows), CDSs (default insurance).
Intermediaries: Brokers earn commissions (no inventory); Dealers hold inventory and profit from bid-ask spread; Clearinghouses are central counterparties that eliminate counterparty risk.

Margin mechanics: Initial margin = minimum equity to open position; Maintenance margin = minimum to keep position; margin call triggered when equity falls below maintenance margin — investor must deposit funds or positions are closed.
Short selling: Borrow shares, sell them, repurchase later to close. Profit if price falls. Subject to margin requirements and margin calls if price rises.

readings.title: Security Market Indexes Module 42.1: Index Weighting Methods
readings.summary:
A security market index represents the performance of an asset class, market, or market segment by tracking a portfolio of constituent securities. Index values are calculated from market prices at a point in time, and index returns measure percentage changes over a period. Returns can be price returns (price changes only) or total returns (prices plus income such as dividends and interest), with total return always greater than or equal to price return when income is generated. Multi-period returns are geometrically linked by compounding: (1 + R1)(1 + R2) − 1.

Index construction requires decisions on: the target market definition, which securities to include, how to weight them, how often to rebalance, and when to reconstitute. The target market can be broad or narrow, defined by geography, sector, or market-cap tier. Security selection can be rules-based or committee-driven.

The five main weighting methods are: price weighting, equal weighting, market-cap weighting, float-adjusted market-cap weighting, and fundamental weighting. Price-weighted indexes (e.g., DJIA, Nikkei 225) average raw prices — higher-priced stocks dominate regardless of company size. A matching portfolio holds an equal number of shares of each constituent. Equal-weighted indexes average returns equally across all stocks, requiring frequent rebalancing and over-weighting small-cap stocks relative to their true market share.

Market-cap weighting (value weighting) assigns weights proportional to each stock's market capitalization relative to total index market cap. It best reflects aggregate investor wealth and requires no adjustment for splits or stock dividends. Float-adjusted market-cap weighting refines this by excluding closely held shares (controlled by founders, governments, or corporations) to better reflect shares actually available to investors — this is now the dominant methodology for major global indexes.

Fundamental weighting uses accounting metrics (earnings, book value, dividends, revenue) to set weights, removing the momentum bias inherent in market-cap methods. The tradeoff is that value-weighted indexes can be distorted by overvalued large-cap stocks, while fundamental-weighted indexes have higher turnover and transaction costs.
key_points: Price return uses only price changes; total return adds income (dividends/interest). Total return >= price return. Multi-period returns are geometrically linked: (1+R1)(1+R2)−1.

Price-weighted index = arithmetic average of constituent prices. Higher-priced stocks have more weight. Divisor is adjusted for splits/composition changes. Matching portfolio: equal number of shares in each stock. Examples: DJIA (30 stocks), Nikkei 225.

Equal-weighted index = arithmetic average of returns. Matching portfolio: equal dollar value in each stock, requiring frequent rebalancing. Over-weights small-cap, under-weights large-cap relative to true market proportions. Examples: Value Line Composite, FT Ordinary Share Index.

Market-cap weighted index weights each stock by its market cap / total index market cap. No adjustment needed for splits or stock dividends. Better reflects aggregate investor wealth. Primary disadvantage: overvalued stocks receive excess weight (momentum bias).

Float-adjusted market-cap weighting excludes shares held by controlling shareholders, governments, and corporations. 'Free float' further excludes shares unavailable to foreign buyers. This is the dominant method for major global indexes (S&P 500, MSCI).

Fundamental weighting uses metrics like earnings, book value, dividends, or revenue. Eliminates price-momentum bias but results in higher turnover and transaction costs, and tilts toward value stocks.

Index construction decisions: (1) target market definition, (2) security selection (all vs. sample, rules vs. committee), (3) weighting method, (4) rebalancing frequency, (5) reconstitution schedule — all are potential exam topics.

readings.title: Market Efficiency Module 43.1: Market Efficiency
readings.summary:
An informationally efficient capital market is one where current security prices fully, quickly, and rationally reflect all available information. In such a market, prices are unbiased estimates of intrinsic value, meaning no investor can consistently earn positive risk-adjusted returns — the popular notion of 'you can't beat the market.' The speed at which new information is incorporated into prices is a key measure of efficiency; in highly efficient markets like foreign exchange, this lag can be under a minute. Critically, only unexpected new information moves prices — anticipated announcements have no effect because they are already priced in.

Market value is simply the current price of an asset, while intrinsic (fundamental) value is what a fully informed, rational investor would pay. In efficient markets, these two values converge. Active managers attempt to exploit gaps between market value and intrinsic value, buying undervalued assets and selling overvalued ones. However, intrinsic value can never be known with certainty, varies across investors, and changes continuously as new information arrives.

Several factors determine a market's degree of efficiency: the number of active participants, the availability and fairness of information (e.g., SEC Regulation FD mandates equal disclosure), impediments to arbitrage and short selling, and the overall cost of information and trading. The more participants, the more information available, and the lower the transaction costs, the more efficient the market. Emerging markets and OTC-traded instruments tend to be less efficient due to fewer participants and less information transparency.

Eugene Fama identified three forms of market efficiency. Weak-form EMH holds that all past price and volume data is already reflected in prices, making technical analysis unable to generate excess returns. Semi-strong-form EMH states that all publicly available information — including financial statements, news, and economic data — is rapidly priced in, rendering fundamental analysis unable to generate excess returns on average. Strong-form EMH asserts that even private (insider) information is reflected in prices, implying no investor group can consistently earn abnormal returns. In practice, evidence supports weak and semi-strong forms but not strong-form efficiency, as insider trading laws themselves acknowledge that private information is not yet priced in.
key_points: Efficient market = prices fully, quickly, and rationally reflect ALL available information; only UNEXPECTED new information moves prices.

Three EMH forms by information set — Weak: past price/volume data (defeats technical analysis); Semi-strong: all public information (defeats fundamental analysis); Strong: all public + private info (defeats everything, including insider trading).
Market value = current price; Intrinsic value = rational, fully informed estimate of worth. Active managers exploit the gap; efficient markets minimize the gap.
Factors that INCREASE efficiency: more market participants, greater information availability, lower transaction costs, fewer short-selling restrictions, and active arbitrage activity.
Factors that DECREASE efficiency: barriers to foreign investor participation, restricted short selling, high transaction/information costs, and impediments to arbitrage.
Regulation FD (Fair Disclosure) requires firms to disclose material information equally to the public and analysts — designed to level the information playing field.
In a perfectly efficient market, passive investing (index strategy) dominates active strategies because active management fees and transaction costs erode any potential alpha.

readings.title: Overview Of Equity Securities
readings.summary:
Equity securities represent ownership interests in firms, with common shares being the most prevalent form. Common shareholders hold residual claims on assets after debtholders and preferred stockholders, and exercise governance through voting rights on the board of directors, mergers, and auditor selection. Two voting systems exist: statutory voting (one vote per share per director seat) and cumulative voting (total votes = shares × directors elected), which allows minority shareholders to concentrate votes and gain proportional board representation.

Preferred shares (preference shares) are hybrid securities combining features of both equity and debt. They typically pay fixed periodic dividends without a contractual obligation, lack voting rights, and have priority over common stock in dividends and liquidation. Key variants include cumulative (unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before common dividends), non-cumulative (missed dividends do not accumulate), participating (share in excess profits and liquidation upside), non-participating (limited to par value in liquidation), callable (issuer can repurchase at set price), putable (holder can sell back at set price), and convertible (exchangeable into common stock at a preset ratio).

Convertible preferred shares are especially important in venture capital and private equity financing because they offer downside protection via the fixed preferred dividend while providing upside participation through the conversion feature. As the common stock price rises, the conversion option gains value, compensating investors for taking on higher risk in early-stage or risky firms.

Public equity trades openly on exchanges with price discovery, regulatory disclosure requirements, and high liquidity. Private equity, by contrast, is sold via private placements to institutional investors, offering less liquidity, negotiated pricing, lower reporting burdens, weaker governance scrutiny, and a longer investment horizon. The three main private equity strategies are venture capital (early-stage funding), leveraged buyouts (LBOs, where firms are acquired using significant debt), and private investments in public equity (PIPEs, where public firms sell discounted equity quickly to raise capital).
key_points: Cumulative voting = shares × number of directors = total votes that can be concentrated on one candidate; statutory voting = one vote per share per seat — cumulative favors minority shareholders.

Cumulative preferred: missed dividends accumulate as arrears and must be fully paid before common dividends. Non-cumulative: missed dividends are gone but current period must still be paid before common.
Participating preferred shareholders receive extra dividends if profits exceed a threshold AND may receive above-par value in liquidation; non-participating are capped at par value.
Preferred dividend formula: Par Value × Dividend Rate = Annual Dividend (e.g., $80 par × 10% = $8/year).
Convertible preferred is favored in venture capital/private equity: fixed dividend provides downside protection; conversion feature provides upside when common stock appreciates.
Private equity vs. public equity: private has less liquidity, no market-determined price, lower disclosure/reporting costs, weaker governance, longer horizon, and potentially higher returns post-IPO.

Three private equity types: Venture Capital (seed/early-stage funding, 3–10 year lock-up), LBO (all-equity purchase using debt; MBO if management are buyers), PIPE (discounted equity sold quickly by public firms needing capital).

readings.title: Company Analysis: Past And Present
readings.summary:
A thorough company research report includes an analyst's valuation and investment recommendation based on projected earnings, cash flows, and financial position. An initial report (initiating coverage) is comprehensive, covering front matter, recommendation rationale, company description, industry overview, financial analysis, valuation, ESG factors, and key risks. Subsequent reports are more focused, highlighting changes in recommendation, variance analysis of expected vs. actual results, and updated risks and valuation.

A company's business model describes how it creates value and drives its income statement and balance sheet. Analysts examine products/services, customers, sales channels, pricing, and supplier relationships—including bargaining power on both sides. Information sources range from company filings and investor presentations to public third-party data, proprietary services like Bloomberg, and primary research such as surveys.

Revenue analysis begins with identifying revenue drivers using bottom-up (price, volume, segment) or top-down (GDP, market share) approaches. Pricing power—the ability to raise prices without losing sales—is central to revenue forecasting. It is higher in less competitive markets characterized by product differentiation, high switching costs, few substitutes, and strong barriers to entry. In highly competitive (commoditized) markets, companies are price takers and earn near-zero economic profit in the long run.

Operating profitability and working capital management are evaluated using key ratios. Gross margin reflects pricing power and production efficiency; operating margin captures overhead control. Working capital metrics such as days sales outstanding, days inventory on hand, and days payable outstanding are used to assess the cash conversion cycle. Efficient working capital management frees up cash and reduces financing needs, directly impacting a company's financial health and valuation.
key_points: Initial research reports (initiating coverage) are comprehensive; subsequent reports focus on changes to recommendation, variance analysis, and updated risks—not full industry overviews.
Bloomberg and similar services are proprietary third-party sources, not public sources or primary research.
Bottom-up revenue analysis uses price/volume/segment drivers; top-down uses macro variables like GDP growth or market share.
Pricing power is highest in markets with product differentiation, high switching costs, few substitutes, and high barriers to entry (monopoly/oligopoly/monopolistic competition).
In highly competitive (commoditized) markets, companies are price takers; long-run returns approach the cost of capital (zero economic profit), except for a sustainable low-cost producer.
Rising profit margins over time—where price increases outpace cost increases—signal strong pricing power and the ability to pass costs to customers.
The cash conversion cycle (DIO + DSO − DPO) measures working capital efficiency; a shorter cycle means faster cash generation and less need for external financing.

readings.title: Industry And Competitive Analysis
readings.summary:
Industry and competitive analysis is a macro-level framework used to assess what drives industry size, profitability, and market share, and to determine individual companies' positions within their industries. Different industries have structurally different long-run profitability levels, and competition tends to push company returns toward an industry baseline over time. While industry factors set a ceiling on profitability, company-specific factors are more often responsible when firms underperform relative to peers. The primary goal of this analysis is to identify an industry's base rate of profitability and project how it may evolve.

The five-step process for industry and competitive analysis begins with defining the industry, followed by surveying its size, growth, and trends. The analyst then applies a structural framework—most commonly Porter's Five Forces—to identify the key drivers of industry profitability. External macro influences are examined using the PESTLE framework (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental). Finally, individual companies' competitive strategies and advantages are evaluated relative to the broader industry context.

Companies are classified into industries using commercial systems such as GICS (S&P/MSCI), ICB (FTSE Russell), and TRBC (Refinitiv). All three use hierarchical, multi-tier structures and share 11 common top-level sectors including Energy, Financials, Health Care, and Information Technology. Classification is typically based on the principal business activity—the segment generating the most revenue. If no single segment exceeds 60% of revenue, a 50% threshold using revenue, profits, or assets is applied; otherwise, judgment or a conglomerate designation is used.

Classification systems have notable limitations. Groupings may be too broad or too narrow for analytical purposes. Multi-division conglomerates are assigned to a single industry, which can distort comparisons (e.g., Amazon classified as consumer discretionary despite earning most profits from cloud services). Geographic scope matters too—some industries like health care are inherently local, while others like media have become global. Periodic updates to classification systems can also disrupt historical comparability, requiring analysts to adjust data for consistency.
key_points: Know the 5 steps of industry analysis: (1) Define the industry, (2) Survey size/growth/profitability, (3) Apply Porter's Five Forces, (4) Examine PESTLE external factors, (5) Analyze companies' competitive strategies.
The three major commercial classification systems are GICS (S&P/MSCI), ICB (FTSE Russell), and TRBC (Refinitiv)—all share 11 common top-level sectors.
Classification hierarchy for GICS: Sector → Industry Group → Industry → Sub-Industry (4 tiers); ICB uses Industry → Supersector → Sector → Subsector.

Primary classification rule: assign a firm to the business line comprising >60% of revenue; if no line meets 60%, use the line >50% of revenue/profits/assets; otherwise use judgment or classify as conglomerate.
Industry factors set a long-run profitability ceiling for all firms in that industry; company-specific factors are more responsible when firms underperform their industry peers.

Key limitations of classification systems: overly broad/narrow groupings, multi-product companies forced into one category (e.g., Amazon), geographic scope mismatches, and periodic reclassifications that break historical data continuity.

Industry analysis serves two main investment purposes: improving financial forecasts by incorporating industry-level data, and identifying overlooked investment opportunities; it also allows portfolio construction with targeted industry-level risk exposure.

readings.title: Company Analysis: Forecasting
readings.summary:
Financial statement forecasts serve two primary purposes: valuation and investment recommendations. Analysts must identify the right 'forecast objects'—these fall into four categories: line items with clear drivers (e.g., sales modeled as stores × sales-per-store), items without clear drivers (forecast directly or via management estimates), summary measures (e.g., EPS, free cash flow), and ad hoc items (contingent liabilities, expected windfalls). The guiding principle is to keep models as simple as necessary—complexity does not guarantee accuracy and increases maintenance burden.

Four core approaches exist for building forecasts, and they are often combined. The historical results method extrapolates past performance into the future; it works well for mature, non-cyclical companies but breaks down during industry transitions or cyclical turning points. The historical base rate convergence method assumes a metric will revert to an industry average or GDP growth rate over time—appropriate for established industries with stable structures, but unsuitable for cyclical or rapidly evolving sectors.

Management guidance is a valuable input because managers possess private information unavailable to the public. However, analysts must scrutinize the assumptions embedded in guidance (e.g., GDP growth, pricing) and watch for systematic bias—management tends to shade revenue guidance low and expense guidance high to engineer 'beats.' Guidance is most reliable when verified against a track record of budget-vs.-actual variance analyses. For situations where all three prior methods fall short—cyclical firms, companies with no peers, or those in major operational transitions—analysts rely on discretionary forecasts derived from surveys, scenario models, or probability distributions.

Forecast horizon is determined by investor time horizon, industry cyclicality, and company-specific factors. For cyclical industries, the horizon must span at least one full business cycle midpoint to avoid distortion. Revenue forecasting itself can proceed top-down (macro GDP → industry → company market share) or bottom-up (unit volumes × prices, or individual segment build-ups), with hybrid approaches combining both. Top-down forecasts are best for macro-sensitive businesses, while bottom-up forecasts provide granularity when product-level or segment-level data is available.
key_points: Four forecast objects: (1) line items with clear drivers, (2) items without clear drivers, (3) summary measures (EPS, FCF), (4) ad hoc items (contingent liabilities, expected gains/losses).

Four forecast approaches: historical results (best for mature/non-cyclical), base rate convergence (best for stable industries with many public peers), management guidance (best when management has a verified track record), and discretionary (best for cyclical, no-peer, or transitioning companies).
Management guidance bias: revenue guidance is shaded LOW and expense guidance is shaded HIGH so management appears to beat expectations—analysts must adjust accordingly.

Historical results and base rate convergence are both INAPPROPRIATE for cyclical industries; base rate convergence is also problematic when one dominant firm accounts for most of the industry average.
Forecast horizon for cyclical industries must be long enough to encompass at least the midpoint of a full business cycle to avoid over- or under-estimating results.

Top-down revenue forecasting starts at GDP/macro level, flows to industry size, then to company market share; bottom-up builds from unit volumes and prices or individual segments—hybrid approaches blend both.

Keep forecasting models as simple as the task requires: complexity increases maintenance cost without necessarily improving accuracy, and granular detail is only valuable when underlying data is readily available and recurring.

readings.title: Equity Valuation: Concepts And Basic Tools
readings.summary:
This reading introduces the foundational framework for equity valuation, starting with the concept of intrinsic (fundamental) value — the rational value an investor would assign a stock with full information. Analysts estimate intrinsic value using valuation models and compare it to market price to judge whether a stock is overvalued, undervalued, or fairly valued. Key considerations before acting on a perceived mispricing include: the magnitude of the difference, confidence in the model and its inputs, the model's sensitivity to input changes, why the mispricing exists, and whether the price will actually converge to intrinsic value within the investment horizon.

The reading surveys three major categories of valuation models. Discounted cash flow (DCF) models estimate value as the present value of future cash flows to shareholders — either dividends (dividend discount models) or free cash flow to equity. Multiplier models use ratios such as P/E, P/Sales, P/Book, or P/Cash Flow to compare a stock's price to fundamentals, or enterprise-value multiples like EV/EBITDA to value the whole firm before backing out equity. Asset-based models estimate equity value as fair-value total assets minus liabilities and preferred stock, and are most applicable when assets are tangible and market-observable.

The reading then covers corporate actions that affect share price and shareholder wealth. Cash dividends (regular, special/extra, irregular) distribute cash and reduce equity value. Stock dividends and stock splits increase share count while proportionally reducing price, leaving total wealth unchanged. Reverse splits reduce share count and raise price, again with no wealth effect. Share repurchases are economically equivalent to cash dividends of the same size and are often preferred in tax regimes that favor capital gains over dividend income.

Finally, the dividend payment chronology defines four critical dates: the declaration date (board approves dividend), the ex-dividend date (first day a buyer does NOT receive the dividend — stock price typically drops by approximately the dividend amount on this date), the holder-of-record date (shareholders on record who receive the dividend, one to two business days after ex-date), and the payment date (cash is actually distributed). Understanding these dates is essential for determining who receives a dividend when shares change hands around the announcement.
key_points: A stock is undervalued if market price < intrinsic value estimate, overvalued if market price > intrinsic value estimate; five factors determine whether to act on the gap (magnitude, model confidence, input confidence, reason for mispricing, convergence likelihood).

Three valuation model categories: (1) DCF/present value models (DDM, FCFE), (2) multiplier/market multiple models (P/E, P/B, P/S, P/CF; EV/EBITDA, EV/Revenue), (3) asset-based models (fair-value assets minus liabilities and preferred stock).

Enterprise value = market value of all outstanding securities MINUS cash and short-term investments; equity value is then estimated by subtracting liabilities and preferred stock from enterprise value.
Stock dividends, stock splits, and reverse stock splits do NOT change shareholder wealth — they only change the number of shares and price per share proportionally.

Share repurchases have the same effect on shareholder wealth as an equivalent cash dividend; they are preferred when capital gains are taxed at lower rates than dividends, or when management wants to signal undervaluation.

Ex-dividend date is 1–2 business days BEFORE the holder-of-record date; a buyer must purchase the stock BEFORE the ex-dividend date to receive the dividend. The stock price typically falls by approximately the dividend amount on the ex-dividend date.
Special (extra/irregular) dividends are one-time payments used when a firm has unusually high profits; cyclical firms use them to share profits without committing to a higher regular dividend.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Instrument Features
readings.summary:
Fixed-income instruments are debt agreements where an issuer borrows capital (principal/par/face value) from investors and promises to repay it with interest. The two main forms are loans (private, nontradable) and bonds (standardized, tradable securities). Major issuers include sovereign governments, corporations, local governments, supranational entities, and special purpose entities that issue asset-backed securities.

Key bond features include maturity (the date of final cash flow; tenor is time remaining), principal (repaid at maturity or gradually), coupon rate and frequency (fixed, floating, or zero), seniority (senior vs. subordinated debt), and contingency provisions (embedded options like calls, puts, or convertibility). Floating-rate notes (FRNs) pay a market reference rate (MRR) plus a fixed spread in basis points. Zero-coupon bonds pay no periodic interest and are issued at a discount to par.

Bond yields and prices are inversely related: when prices fall, yields rise, and vice versa. The yield curve plots yields against maturity for a given issuer. A normal (upward-sloping) yield curve reflects investor demand for higher returns at longer maturities due to greater uncertainty. An inverted (downward-sloping) yield curve is less common. Government bonds typically serve as the benchmark (lowest credit risk), and corporate spreads are measured against them.

The bond indenture is the legal contract governing the issuer-bondholder relationship. It specifies sources of repayment (taxes for sovereigns, operating cash flows and/or collateral for corporates, pooled assets for ABS) and bond covenants. Affirmative covenants require the issuer to take certain actions (e.g., maintain insurance, pay taxes), while negative covenants restrict the issuer from actions that could harm bondholders (e.g., limits on additional debt issuance, asset sales, or dividend payments).
key_points: Bond prices and yields are INVERSELY related: higher price = lower yield, lower price = higher yield. This is foundational for all fixed-income topics.

Know the bond feature definitions: maturity vs. tenor (tenor = time remaining after issuance), money market securities (original maturity ≤1 year) vs. capital market securities (>1 year), and perpetual bonds (no maturity).
FRNs pay MRR + fixed spread (in basis points). Zero-coupon bonds are issued at a discount and pay par at maturity with no periodic coupons.
Seniority hierarchy matters for credit risk: equity < subordinated (junior) debt < senior debt. Senior debt is less risky than subordinated debt.

A normal yield curve slopes upward (longer maturity = higher yield); an inverted yield curve slopes downward and is less common. Government yields serve as the benchmark for credit spread calculations.

Affirmative covenants = things the issuer MUST do (maintain assets, pay taxes). Negative covenants = things the issuer CANNOT do (take on excess debt, sell key assets). Negative covenants are typically more restrictive and protective for bondholders.
Secured bonds have a lien/pledge on specific collateral; unsecured bonds rely solely on operating cash flow. ABS repayment comes from the pooled financial assets held by the special purpose entity.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Cash Flows And Types
readings.summary:
Fixed-income instruments come in several principal repayment structures. A bullet bond returns the full par value in a single payment at maturity, with periodic coupons representing pure interest. Amortizing bonds instead blend principal and interest into each periodic payment: fully amortizing bonds retire the entire principal through level payments (calculated with FV=0 on a financial calculator), while partially amortizing bonds reduce principal over time but leave a balloon payment at maturity. Sinking fund provisions require the issuer to retire portions of an issue periodically, lowering credit risk but introducing reinvestment risk when rates fall. Waterfall structures, used in ABSs and MBSs, prioritize principal repayments to senior tranches before junior tranches receive any principal.

Beyond bullet and amortizing structures, bonds differ widely in how coupons are set. Floating-rate notes (FRNs) pay a market reference rate (MRR) plus a fixed credit spread quoted in basis points, resetting each period. Step-up coupon bonds increase the coupon on a predetermined schedule. Credit-linked notes and leveraged loans tie coupon changes to the issuer's credit rating or leverage metrics, penalizing deterioration with higher spreads. Payment-in-kind (PIK) bonds allow issuers to pay interest by issuing additional bonds rather than cash, signaling elevated credit risk and carrying higher yields. Green bonds may include coupon step-ups if environmental targets are missed.

Index-linked bonds protect investors from inflation by tying cash flows to a published price index. The two main structures are interest-indexed bonds, which adjust the coupon rate while keeping par fixed, and capital-indexed bonds (such as U.S. TIPS), which keep the coupon rate fixed but adjust the principal for inflation or deflation. For TIPS, each coupon is paid on the inflation-adjusted principal, and at maturity investors receive the greater of the adjusted principal or the original par value. This makes capital-indexed bonds the more complete inflation hedge because both coupons and principal grow with inflation.

Contingency provisions embedded in bonds can benefit either the issuer or the investor. Call provisions give the issuer the right to redeem bonds early, typically when rates fall, allowing refinancing at lower cost — this hurts bondholders through reinvestment risk and potential capital loss. Put provisions benefit investors by allowing them to sell bonds back to the issuer at par if rates rise or credit quality falls. Convertible bonds give investors the right to convert into equity, offering upside participation. These provisions affect the bond's yield and price, and the CFA exam tests the ability to identify who benefits from each provision and under what market conditions.
key_points: Bullet vs. amortizing: bullet bonds return full par at maturity with interest-only coupons; fully amortizing bonds use level payments (FV=0) that blend principal and interest; partially amortizing bonds end with a balloon payment (FV > 0).
Sinking fund provisions reduce credit risk (less principal outstanding) but increase reinvestment risk for bondholders when interest rates decline.
FRNs pay MRR + fixed spread in basis points; quarterly coupon = (MRR + spread) / 4 applied to par. The spread compensates for the issuer's credit risk above the benchmark rate.

Capital-indexed bonds (TIPS) adjust principal for inflation/deflation; coupon rate stays fixed but is applied to the adjusted principal. At maturity, investors receive max(adjusted principal, original par).
PIK bonds pay interest with additional bonds rather than cash, indicating potential cash flow stress and commanding higher yields due to elevated credit risk.
Call provisions benefit issuers (can refinance when rates fall); put provisions benefit investors (can redeem at par when rates rise or credit deteriorates) — know which party holds each option.

Waterfall structures in ABS/MBS prioritize senior tranches for principal repayment; junior tranches receive principal only after senior tranches are fully repaid, though all tranches continue to receive interest.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Issuance And Trading
readings.summary:
Global bond markets are segmented primarily by issuer type (governments, corporates, special purpose entities), credit quality (investment-grade vs. high-yield), and maturity (money market <1 year, intermediate 1–10 years, long-term >10 years). Investment-grade bonds carry ratings of BBB-/Baa3 or higher, while high-yield ('junk') bonds are rated BB+/Ba1 or lower. Fallen angels are formerly investment-grade bonds downgraded to high-yield status. A company's access to capital markets and intended use of proceeds drives which type of bond it issues.
