Investor positioning in the credit/maturity spectrum reflects each institution's liability structure and risk tolerance. Pension funds and insurance companies favor long-term investment-grade bonds to match long-term liabilities and are often prohibited from owning high-yield. Corporations invest excess liquidity in short-term instruments like commercial paper and repos. Central banks use intermediate-term Treasuries for monetary policy, while banks use Treasuries across all maturities to manage interest rate and liquidity risk. Hedge funds and distressed debt funds operate at the riskier, high-yield end of the spectrum.

Fixed-income indexes differ from equity indexes in three key ways: they have far more constituents (one issuer may have many bond series), they experience higher turnover as bonds mature and new ones are issued, and they are heavily weighted toward sovereign issuers. Aggregate indexes like the Bloomberg Barclays Aggregate cover broad markets across sectors and currencies, while narrower indexes focus on geography, credit quality, sector, or maturity. ESG indexes add screens for sustainability ratings and exclude certain business sectors.

In primary markets, bonds are sold via public offerings or private placements, typically through investment banks. Underwritten offerings guarantee the issue price to the issuer, while best-efforts offerings do not. Secondary markets provide liquidity after issuance, with most bond trading occurring over-the-counter (OTC) between dealers and investors rather than on centralized exchanges, unlike equities. This OTC structure means bond markets are generally less transparent and less liquid than equity markets, with transaction costs that can be significant for smaller or illiquid issues.
key_points: Investment-grade cutoff: BBB-/Baa3 or higher (S&P/Moody's); anything BB+/Ba1 or lower is high-yield ('junk'); fallen angels are downgraded former investment-grade bonds.
Maturity segments: money market = original maturity <1 year; intermediate = 1–10 years; long-term = >10 years.
Pension funds and insurance companies must hold long-term investment-grade bonds to match liabilities and are often PROHIBITED from owning high-yield by regulation.
Fixed-income indexes vs. equity indexes: more constituents, higher turnover (bonds mature), and large sovereign weights — bond tracker funds use sampling, not full replication.
Primary market: underwritten offering = intermediary GUARANTEES issue price; best-efforts = no price guarantee, issuer bears placement risk.
Secondary bond markets are primarily OTC (dealer-to-investor), not exchange-traded — less transparent and less liquid than equity markets.
A benchmark index for a bond fund must match the fund's sector focus, credit quality, and maturity exposure to be a valid performance benchmark.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Markets For Corporate Issuers
readings.summary:
Corporations raise short-term external funds through two main channels: loan financing and security-based financing. Bank lines of credit come in three forms: uncommitted lines (flexible but unreliable, no fees beyond interest), committed lines (reliable for a fixed term, ~50 bps commitment fee, renewal risk at maturity), and revolving lines (most reliable, longer-term, often with restrictive covenants). Weaker credits must pledge collateral for secured loans, while factoring allows firms to sell receivables outright to a 'factor' at a discount reflecting customer creditworthiness and collection costs.

High-quality corporations can bypass banks entirely by issuing commercial paper (CP) — short-term unsecured discount securities typically maturing in under three months — at rates below bank loan rates. CP is frequently rolled over at maturity, creating rollover risk, which firms manage by maintaining backup liquidity lines with banks. CP issued outside the U.S. is called Eurocommercial paper (ECP). CP used as a temporary bridge before long-term issuance is called bridge financing.

Financial institutions have additional funding sources not available to nonfinancial firms. These include demand deposits (no interest, immediate access), operational deposits from large corporate clients, and negotiable certificates of deposit (CDs) that can be sold before maturity. Banks also borrow from each other in the interbank market at a market reference rate (MRR) such as SOFR, or from the central bank's funds market (with the central bank acting as lender of last resort via the discount window at a premium rate). Financial institutions also sponsor asset-backed commercial paper (ABCP) by transferring loan collateral to a special purpose entity (SPE), which then issues CP to investors.

Repurchase agreements (repos) are a critical secured short-term funding mechanism. In a repo, a security seller agrees to repurchase the security at a higher price on a future date — effectively a collateralized loan. The repo rate is the implied interest rate; the repo margin (haircut) protects the buyer against collateral price declines. Repos are flexible in term (overnight to longer-dated 'term repos') and allow both parties to benefit: the seller gets cheap secured financing and the buyer earns a return on idle cash with low credit risk. Key risks include counterparty default risk and collateral price risk, both managed through haircuts and daily margining.
key_points: Three bank credit line types in order of reliability and cost: uncommitted (no fee, bank can refuse) < committed (50 bps fee, renewal risk) < revolving (most reliable, longest term, restrictive covenants).

Commercial paper (CP) is short-term unsecured discount debt (<3 months) for high-credit issuers; rollover risk is managed with backup liquidity lines; Eurocommercial paper (ECP) is the international equivalent.
Factoring = outright sale of receivables to a factor at a discount; differs from assigning receivables as collateral — the factor assumes credit and collection risk.

Asset-backed commercial paper (ABCP) is issued by a special purpose entity (SPE) funded with collateral transferred from a sponsoring financial institution — the SPE is off-balance-sheet for the sponsor.

A repo is a collateralized loan: seller (borrower) sells a security and agrees to repurchase it at a higher price; the difference implies the repo rate; the haircut (repo margin) protects the lender against collateral value declines.
The central bank acts as lender of last resort via discount window lending at a rate above the central bank funds rate, typically with extra scrutiny attached.
Negotiable CDs can be sold in secondary markets before maturity (key distinction from nonnegotiable CDs); they are an important wholesale funding tool for banks in both domestic and Eurobond markets.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Markets For Government Issuers
readings.summary:
Sovereign governments issue bonds to fund public spending and infrastructure, backed by their taxing power, making them the highest-rated issuers in their domestic markets. A key distinction exists between developed market sovereigns—which have stable economies, transparent fiscal policy, and debt in reserve currencies—and emerging market sovereigns, which face less stable revenues and issue both domestic debt (in home currency, held locally) and external debt (owed to foreign creditors, often in a reserve currency). Analysts must look beyond standard financial statements to an 'economic balance sheet' that includes implied assets like future tax revenues and implied liabilities like promised future expenditures.

Governments issue debt across a range of maturities to balance rollover risk (too much short-term debt) against the benefits of liquidity. Short-term sovereign debt serves as a near-cash alternative for investors, while longer-term sovereign debt functions as a benchmark for pricing corporate credit risk, a tool for interest rate risk management, and collateral in repurchase agreements. The concept of Ricardian equivalence suggests governments should be indifferent to debt maturity, but its strict assumptions (rational expectations, perfect capital markets, altruism toward future generations) do not hold in practice.

Beyond central governments, the fixed-income market includes nonsovereign issuers: local/regional governments issuing general obligation bonds (backed by taxing power) or revenue bonds (backed by project fees); quasi-government agency bonds created for specific national purposes (e.g., Ginnie Mae) often with implicit sovereign backing; and supranational bonds issued by multinational institutions like the World Bank and IMF, which carry high credit quality.

Government debt is primarily issued via regular public auctions, unlike corporate debt which is raised as needed. Auctions accept competitive bids (which set price) and noncompetitive bids (guaranteed allocation at the clearing price). In a single-price (Dutch) auction, all successful bidders pay the same cut-off price—the lowest price at which the full offering is allocated. In a multiple-price auction, each bidder pays their own bid price. On-the-run securities are the most recently issued and most liquid benchmark issues; older issues become off-the-run and trade at a slight yield premium due to lower liquidity.
key_points: Sovereign debt is backed by taxing power; developed market sovereigns use reserve currencies, while emerging market sovereigns face currency and revenue instability with both domestic and external debt.

External debt denominated in a reserve currency shifts direct currency risk away from foreign investors but leaves indirect risk: can the EM government generate enough reserve-currency flows to service the debt?

Ricardian equivalence implies debt maturity is irrelevant, but only under four unrealistic assumptions; in practice, governments manage maturity mix to avoid rollover risk while maintaining benchmark yield curves.
General obligation (GO) bonds are backed by the issuer's full taxing authority; revenue bonds are repaid solely from fees generated by the funded project (e.g., tolls).
In a single-price (Dutch) government auction, all bidders pay the cut-off yield/price; in a multiple-price auction, each competitive bidder pays their own bid price.
On-the-run sovereigns are the most recently issued benchmark securities and are the most liquid; off-the-run issues trade at a yield premium reflecting lower liquidity.
Agency/quasi-government bonds (e.g., Ginnie Mae) are created by sovereigns for specific purposes; when backed by the government, their yields and ratings closely mirror sovereign debt.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Bond Valuation: Prices And Yields
readings.summary:
Bond valuation is rooted in discounting all future cash flows (coupons and par repayment) at the bond's yield-to-maturity (YTM). The inverse price-yield relationship is fundamental: when YTM rises above the coupon rate, the bond trades at a discount; when YTM falls below the coupon rate, the bond trades at a premium; when YTM equals the coupon rate, the bond prices at par. This relationship is convex, meaning price increases from a yield decrease are larger in magnitude than price decreases from an equal yield increase.

For semiannual coupon bonds, adjustments are straightforward: divide the annual coupon and YTM by 2, and double the number of periods. The stated annualized YTM is simply the periodic rate multiplied by the number of periods per year. To solve for YTM given a price, the same TVM framework applies — input N, PMT, FV, and PV (as a negative cash outflow) to solve for I/Y, then annualize.

When bonds trade between coupon dates, pricing must account for accrued interest owed to the seller. The flat price (clean price) is the quoted market price, which excludes accrued interest. The full price (dirty price or invoice price) is what the buyer actually pays and equals flat price plus accrued interest. Day-count conventions (actual/actual or 30/360) determine the exact accrued interest amount. The full price is calculated by compounding the bond's value from the last coupon date forward using the fractional period elapsed, then accrued interest is subtracted to get the flat price.

To actually earn the stated YTM, three conditions must hold: the investor holds the bond to maturity, the issuer makes all promised payments on time, and all interim coupon cash flows are reinvested at the same YTM rate. The reinvestment assumption is a key limitation of YTM as a return measure.
key_points: Coupon = YTM → price at par; Coupon > YTM → premium; Coupon < YTM → discount. Memorize this relationship cold.
Price-yield relationship is convex: a yield decrease raises price more than an equal yield increase lowers it.
For semiannual bonds: N×2, PMT÷2, I/Y÷2. Always annualize the computed periodic I/Y by multiplying by 2 to get the stated YTM.
Full price (dirty/invoice) = Flat price (clean/quoted) + Accrued interest. Bonds are quoted at flat price to avoid artificial day-to-day price drift.
Accrued interest = Coupon × (days since last coupon / days in coupon period). Know both actual/actual and 30/360 day-count methods.
Between coupon dates, compute full price by compounding the last-coupon-date value forward by the fractional period: PV × (1 + r)^(t/T).
Three conditions to earn YTM: hold to maturity, no default, reinvest all coupons at the same YTM — the reinvestment assumption is a key exam trap.

readings.title: Yield And Yield Spread Measures For Fixed-Rate Bonds
readings.summary:
Yield to maturity (YTM) is the discount rate that equates a bond's present value of cash flows to its market price. For bonds with different payment frequencies, the YTM is quoted as an annualized rate by multiplying the periodic rate by the number of periods per year (periodicity). Higher periodicity means more compounding, resulting in a higher effective annual yield (EAY) even for the same quoted YTM. To compare bonds with different periodicities, you must convert yields to a common basis using the EAY formula: EAY = (1 + periodic rate)^n - 1.

Beyond YTM, several alternative yield measures exist. Current yield (income yield) measures only annual coupon income divided by flat price, ignoring capital gains and reinvestment income. Simple yield improves on current yield by amortizing any discount or premium on a straight-line basis over the bond's life. Street convention yield uses scheduled coupon dates, while true yield uses actual payment dates (accounting for weekends/holidays), making true yield slightly lower.

For callable bonds, yield to call (YTC) is calculated for each possible call date and price. The yield to worst (YTW) is the minimum of YTM and all yields to call — it represents the worst-case yield an investor can expect. Callable bonds can be decomposed as: callable bond value = straight bond value − call option value. The option-adjusted yield removes the embedded option's effect, allowing apples-to-apples comparison with option-free bonds; it will be lower than the callable bond's raw yield because the call option depresses price.

Yield spreads measure the difference between a bond's yield and a benchmark. The G-spread is the spread over an interpolated government bond yield. The I-spread is the spread over the swap rate. The Z-spread (zero-volatility spread) is the constant spread added to each spot rate on the benchmark curve that makes the bond's discounted cash flows equal its price — it is more rigorous than the G-spread. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) removes the value of any embedded option from the Z-spread, isolating the pure credit/liquidity spread; for callable bonds, OAS < Z-spread because the call option has positive value to the issuer.
key_points: Periodicity conversion formula: (1 + YTM_A/m)^m = (1 + YTM_B/n)^n — higher periodicity → higher EAY for the same quoted rate.
Current yield = Annual coupon / Flat price. It ignores capital gains/losses and reinvestment income — the most limited yield measure.
Simple yield = (Annual coupon + Annual straight-line discount amortization) / Flat price — accounts for discount/premium but not compounding.
Yield to worst (YTW) = minimum of YTM and all yields to call — always use this as the conservative yield for callable bonds.
Option-adjusted yield < callable bond yield: 'option-adjusted' means the option is REMOVED, not added. OAS isolates credit/liquidity spread by stripping the embedded option.
Z-spread is the constant spread added to every point on the spot curve to match the bond's market price — more precise than G-spread (which uses a single interpolated yield).
G-spread = bond yield minus interpolated government bond yield; I-spread = bond yield minus swap rate; OAS = Z-spread minus option value (for callable bonds, OAS < Z-spread).

readings.title: Yield And Yield Spread Measures For Floating-Rate
readings.summary:
Floating-rate notes (FRNs) have more stable values than fixed-rate bonds because their coupon rates reset periodically based on a market reference rate (MRR). The coupon equals MRR plus a quoted margin (QM), which is fixed at issuance. The discount margin (DM) is the margin required to price the FRN at par given current market conditions. At issuance, QM equals DM and the FRN trades at par. Post-issuance, if credit quality deteriorates, DM rises above QM and the FRN trades at a discount; if credit quality improves, DM falls below QM and the FRN trades at a premium.

To value an FRN on a reset date, estimate future cash flows using MRR + QM as the coupon rate, then discount those cash flows at MRR + DM. For example, a 5-year semiannual FRN with QM = 1.2% and DM = 1.5% when MRR = 3.0% produces a coupon of 2.1% per period discounted at 2.25% per period, yielding a price below par (approximately 98.67% of face value).

Money market instruments (maturity ≤ 1 year) use several distinct yield conventions. Bank CDs and repos use add-on yields; T-bills and commercial paper use discount yields. Some use a 360-day year, others a 365-day year. The bond equivalent yield (BEY) is defined as an add-on yield annualized on a 365-day basis, and serves as a standard for comparison across instruments.

Converting between yield conventions is a critical skill. To compare a discount yield to an add-on yield, first find the actual dollar discount, then compute the HPY from the purchase price, then annualize over 365 days. To compare a money market yield to a semiannual bond YTM, convert the HPY to an effective semiannual yield and double it. Because money market instruments have a higher periodicity than semiannual bonds, their simple annual rate will be lower than the equivalent semiannual bond basis yield.
key_points: QM (quoted margin) is fixed at issuance; DM (discount margin) reflects current required yield. FRN trades at par when QM = DM, at discount when DM > QM, at premium when DM < QM.
FRN valuation on reset date: coupon cash flows = MRR + QM; discount rate = MRR + DM. Use standard TVM (N, I/Y, FV, PMT, CPT PV).
Bond Equivalent Yield (BEY) = add-on yield annualized on a 365-day basis. This is the standard comparison benchmark for money market instruments.

T-bills use discount yields on a 360-day basis. To find price: Discount = face × (discount rate) × (days/360); Price = face − discount. HPY = (face − price)/price, which is slightly higher than the quoted discount.
To convert a 360-day add-on yield to BEY (365-day): multiply by 365/360.
To compare a money market yield to a semiannual bond YTM: compute HPY → effective annual yield → effective semiannual yield → multiply by 2 to get semiannual bond basis yield.
Higher periodicity of money market instruments means their quoted simple annual rate understates the equivalent semiannual bond basis yield — always convert before comparing to bond YTMs.

readings.title: The Term Structure Of Interest Rates: Spot, Par, And Forward Curves
readings.summary:
Spot rates are the discount rates applied to a single cash flow occurring at a specific future date, derived from zero-coupon bond yields. Unlike YTM, which applies one flat discount rate to all cash flows, spot rates vary by maturity and reflect the true time-value cost for each payment period. To price a bond using spot rates, each cash flow is discounted at its corresponding spot rate and the present values are summed. This produces the no-arbitrage price — if the market price differs, arbitrage opportunities exist.

Par rates represent the coupon rate a hypothetical bond at a given maturity would need in order to be priced exactly at par, given the prevailing spot curve. Conceptually, the par rate at each maturity is equivalent to the YTM of a par-priced bond. To solve for a par rate algebraically, you set the bond's price equal to 100 and solve for the coupon payment — but on the exam, plugging in answer choices is faster.

Forward rates are interest rates for a loan that begins at some future date. The notation (e.g., 2y1y) identifies when the loan starts and how long it lasts. The foundational no-arbitrage principle is that investing at a multi-period spot rate must yield the same terminal value as a sequence of successive short-term forward rates: (1 + S3)^3 = (1 + S1)(1 + 1y1y)(1 + 2y1y). This allows you to derive spot rates from forward rates and vice versa.

Forward rates can be extracted from spot rates using the relationship (1 + Sn)^n = (1 + Sm)^m × (1 + mf(n-m)), where mf(n-m) is the forward rate starting m periods from now for a duration of (n-m) periods. Bonds can also be priced by discounting each cash flow using the compounded product of relevant forward rates rather than spot rates, yielding the same no-arbitrage price. Understanding how spot, par, and forward curves interrelate is essential for interpreting yield curve shape and expectations about future interest rates.
key_points: Bond price using spot rates: each cash flow is discounted at its maturity-matched spot rate (zero rate); this equals the no-arbitrage price.
Forward rate notation: the first number = when the loan starts, the second = loan length (e.g., 2y1y = 1-year loan starting 2 years from now).
Key no-arbitrage identity: (1 + Sn)^n = (1 + Sm)^m × (1 + forward rate); use this to convert between spot and forward rates in both directions.
To find a forward rate from spot rates: isolate the forward rate — e.g., 2y1y = [(1 + S3)^3 / (1 + S2)^2] − 1.
To find a spot rate from forward rates: take the geometric mean of compounded forward rates — S3 = [(1+f1)(1+f2)(1+f3)]^(1/3) − 1; approximate with simple average.
Par rate at each maturity = coupon rate that prices a hypothetical bond at exactly 100 given the spot curve; on the exam, plug in answer choices rather than solving algebraically.
YTM is a weighted average of spot rates across maturities, weighted by cash flow size; since par repayment dominates, YTM is closest to the longest maturity spot rate.

readings.title: Interest Rate Risk And Return
readings.summary:
A fixed-rate bond investor has three sources of return: coupon and principal payments, reinvestment income on coupons, and capital gain or loss if the bond is sold before maturity. The key analytical tool is the horizon yield, which is the compound annual return earned over the investor's holding period. It is calculated by comparing the purchase price to the total end value (coupons + reinvestment income + sale/par proceeds).

When the YTM remains unchanged throughout the holding period, an investor always earns exactly the YTM at purchase — whether held to maturity or sold early. This is because the bond's price follows a constant-yield price trajectory (its carrying value), and any capital gain or loss is measured relative to that carrying value, not par. If sold at the same YTM, there is no capital gain or loss, and reinvestment at YTM ensures the annualized return equals the original YTM.

When YTM rises after purchase, two opposing forces work against each other: higher reinvestment income (positive) and a lower bond sale price (negative). For an investor holding to maturity, only the reinvestment effect matters — higher rates mean more reinvestment income, so the realized return exceeds the original YTM. For a short holding period, the price loss dominates and the realized return falls below the original YTM.

Conversely, when YTM falls after purchase, reinvestment income decreases (negative) but the bond's sale price rises (positive). Holding to maturity locks in lower reinvestment returns, so the realized return falls below the original YTM. But selling early at the now-higher price can push returns above the original YTM. This price effect vs. reinvestment effect trade-off is central to understanding interest rate risk for bond investors.

The concept of Macaulay duration captures the point in time at which these two effects exactly offset each other — the investment horizon at which the investor is immunized against interest rate changes. When the holding period equals the bond's Macaulay duration, price risk and reinvestment risk cancel out, and the investor earns the original YTM regardless of rate movements.
key_points: Three sources of bond return: (1) coupon + principal, (2) reinvestment income on coupons, (3) capital gain/loss on sale before maturity.
If YTM is unchanged, horizon yield = YTM at purchase regardless of holding period — no capital gain/loss occurs because the bond sells at its carrying value.
If YTM rises after purchase: holding to maturity → realized return > original YTM (reinvestment effect dominates); selling early → realized return < original YTM (price effect dominates).
If YTM falls after purchase: holding to maturity → realized return < original YTM (lower reinvestment income); selling early → realized return > original YTM (higher sale price dominates).
Capital gain or loss is always measured relative to the bond's carrying value (constant-yield price trajectory), NOT relative to par value.
Macaulay duration is the investment horizon at which price risk and reinvestment risk exactly offset — the investor earns the original YTM regardless of a one-time parallel rate shift.
For exam calculations: horizon yield = (Total End Value / Purchase Price)^(1/n) − 1, where Total End Value = FV of reinvested coupons + sale price (or par if held to maturity).

readings.title: Yield-Based Bond Duration Measures And Properties
readings.summary:
Modified duration (ModDur) is derived from Macaulay duration by dividing by (1 + periodic yield). It measures the approximate percentage price change for a 1% change in YTM: %ΔPrice ≈ −ModDur × ΔYTM. For semiannual bonds, the semiannual ModDur is annualized by dividing by 2. Because ModDur is a linear approximation of a convex price-yield relationship, it is accurate for small yield changes but increasingly imprecise for larger moves.

Approximate ModDur can be computed numerically using two bond prices — one for a small YTM increase (V+) and one for a small YTM decrease (V−): ApproxModDur = (V− − V+) / (2 × V0 × ΔYTM). This avoids needing Macaulay duration directly and is useful when cash flows are complex or irregular.

Money duration (dollar duration) scales ModDur to a currency amount: Money Duration = ModDur × Full Price of Position. Multiplying money duration by a yield change (in decimal) gives the estimated dollar change in bond value. The Price Value of a Basis Point (PVBP) is the average dollar price change for a 1 bp shift in YTM, computed as (V− − V+) / 2 per $100 of par, then scaled to position size.

A bond's interest rate risk is shaped by three factors: maturity, coupon, and yield level. Longer maturity generally means higher duration and more price sensitivity, though discount bonds can see duration peak and then decline at very long maturities, approaching the perpetuity duration of (1 + YTM)/YTM. Higher coupon rates pull more cash flow to earlier periods, reducing duration. For floating-rate notes, duration equals only the time to the next coupon reset, making price risk minimal.

Yield level itself affects interest rate risk: at lower yields, the price-yield curve is steeper, so the same basis point move causes a larger price change. At higher yields, the curve flattens, reducing price sensitivity. These three drivers — maturity, coupon, yield level — interact together to determine a bond's total interest rate risk.
key_points: ModDur = MacDur / (1 + YTM/m), where m is periodicity; for annual-pay bonds ModDur = MacDur / (1 + YTM)
Approximate price change: %ΔPrice ≈ −ModDur × ΔYTM; ModDur understates price gains and overstates price losses due to convexity
ApproxModDur = (V− − V+) / (2 × V0 × ΔYTM); ΔYTM must be entered as a decimal (e.g., 0.005 for 50 bps)
Money Duration = ModDur × Full Price of bond position; dollar change = Money Duration × ΔYTM (decimal)
PVBP = (V− − V+) / 2 per $100 par; scale by position size and 0.0001 to get dollar change per basis point
Duration increases with longer maturity (usually), decreases with higher coupon, and decreases as YTM rises
FRN Macaulay duration ≈ time to next coupon reset date; zero-coupon bond duration equals its maturity

readings.title: Yield-Based Bond Convexity And Portfolio Properties
readings.summary:
Modified duration provides a linear approximation of the price-yield relationship, but the true relationship is curved (convex). Convexity measures this curvature and allows for more accurate price change estimates, especially for larger yield movements. For a single cash flow at period t, convexity equals t(t+1)/(1+r)^2, and a bond's total convexity is the weighted average of its individual cash flow convexities, using present value weights — the same weights used for Macaulay duration.

To estimate the percentage price change of a bond, both duration (first-order) and convexity (second-order) effects are combined: %ΔPrice ≈ (–ModDur × Δy) + (½ × Convexity × Δy²). The convexity adjustment is always positive regardless of the direction of the yield change — it adds to price when yields fall (duration underestimates gains) and offsets price loss when yields rise (duration overestimates losses). Money convexity extends this to dollar terms by multiplying annual convexity by the full market value of the position.

Convexity is increased by longer maturity, lower coupon rate, and lower YTM — the same factors that increase duration. For two bonds with equal duration, the bond with more dispersed cash flows has greater convexity. For non-annual coupon bonds, the calculated convexity must be divided by the number of periods per year squared to annualize it (e.g., divide by 4 for semiannual bonds).

Portfolio duration and convexity can be estimated either from aggregate portfolio cash flows (theoretically correct) or as a weighted average of individual bond durations and convexities (used in practice). The weighted-average approach assumes a parallel shift in the yield curve — all yields change by the same amount. Since real yield curve changes often involve twists or steepening rather than parallel shifts, this is a key limitation of the weighted-average portfolio duration and convexity measures.
key_points: Convexity formula for a single cash flow: t(t+1)/(1+r)^2; bond convexity = PV-weighted average of all cash flow convexities (same weights as Macaulay duration).
Approximate convexity formula: (V+ + V– – 2V0) / (V0 × Δy²); memorize this for exam calculations.
Full price change estimate: %ΔPrice ≈ (–ModDur × Δy) + (½ × Convexity × Δy²); convexity adjustment is always POSITIVE for both yield increases and decreases.
Annualizing convexity: divide by periodicity squared (semiannual bonds divide by 4; annual bonds need no adjustment).
Convexity increases with: longer maturity, lower coupon, lower YTM. For equal-duration bonds, more dispersed cash flows = more convexity.
Money convexity = annual convexity × full market value of position; used with money duration to estimate dollar price change.
Portfolio weighted-average duration/convexity assumes a parallel yield curve shift — this is the key limitation; non-parallel shifts (twists, steepening) make the measure unreliable.

readings.title: Curve-Based And Empirical Fixed-Income Risk Measures
readings.summary:
Bonds with embedded options (callable, putable, MBS) have uncertain future cash flows and redemption dates, making traditional YTM-based duration measures inappropriate. Because these bonds lack a single well-defined yield, interest rate risk is measured relative to shifts in the benchmark yield curve rather than changes in the bond's own YTM. Effective duration (EffDur) and effective convexity (EffCon) are calculated using bond pricing models that reprice the bond at benchmark curve shifts of +/- ΔCurve, capturing how embedded options alter cash flow timing and magnitude. Importantly, effective duration isolates sensitivity to benchmark yield changes only, holding credit and liquidity spreads constant — unlike modified duration, which does not distinguish between the two sources of yield change.

Callable bonds can exhibit negative convexity at low yield levels because the call option caps price appreciation: as yields fall, the issuer is more likely to call the bond, limiting upside. This makes the price increase from a yield decline smaller than the price decrease from an equal yield increase. Putable bonds, by contrast, always exhibit positive convexity because the put option limits price declines at high yields — the investor's right to sell back the bond at par provides a price floor. For option-free bonds, ModDur and EffDur are nearly equal but not identical unless the yield curve is flat, because a parallel shift in the par curve produces a nonparallel shift in spot rates, causing risky bond yields to move slightly differently than the original ΔCurve.

Effective duration assumes parallel shifts in the benchmark curve. To measure the impact of nonparallel shifts (shaping risk), key rate duration (also called partial duration) is used. Each key rate duration captures price sensitivity to a change in yield at one specific maturity while holding all other maturities constant. The sum of all key rate durations equals the bond's effective duration. Key rate durations are particularly valuable for managing portfolio exposure to yield curve reshaping — steepening, flattening, or humping — rather than simple level shifts.

Empirical duration uses historical price and yield data to estimate duration statistically, rather than relying on pricing models. It is especially useful for securities like high-yield bonds and MBS, where the theoretical relationship between price and benchmark yields breaks down due to credit spread movements, prepayment behavior, or liquidity effects. Empirical duration can differ substantially from analytical (model-based) duration — for example, high-yield bonds tend to show lower empirical duration because their prices move more with credit spreads than with risk-free rates. While empirical duration reflects real-world price behavior, it is backward-looking and may not be stable across different market environments.
key_points: Effective duration uses benchmark yield curve shifts (ΔCurve) and a pricing model to compute V- and V+; formula mirrors approximate modified duration but replaces ΔYTM with ΔCurve — use this for any bond with embedded options.

Callable bonds can have NEGATIVE convexity at low yields (call cap limits price upside); putable bonds always have POSITIVE convexity (put floor limits price downside) — know the price-yield curve shapes for both.
Effective duration isolates benchmark yield sensitivity only, holding spreads constant; modified duration blends benchmark yield changes AND spread changes into a single number.

Key rate duration (partial duration) measures price sensitivity to a change at ONE specific benchmark maturity, holding others constant; sum of all key rate durations = effective duration; use it to assess shaping risk from nonparallel curve shifts.

For option-free bonds, ModDur ≈ EffDur but they are NOT exactly equal unless the yield curve is flat, because a par curve shift causes a nonparallel spot curve shift affecting risky bond yields differently.

Empirical duration is estimated from historical price/yield regressions — useful for high-yield bonds and MBS where model-based duration overstates actual rate sensitivity because prices respond more to spread changes than to benchmark rate changes.
Price change estimate using EffDur and EffCon: %ΔPrice ≈ –EffDur × ΔCurve + ½ × EffCon × (ΔCurve)² — same formula structure as modified duration/convexity price approximation.

readings.title: Credit Risk Module 62.1: Credit Risk
readings.summary:
Credit risk is the risk of loss to fixed income investors when a borrower fails to make scheduled interest or principal payments (i.e., defaults). The drivers of credit risk fall into two categories: bottom-up (borrower-specific) factors captured by the '5 Cs' — Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Covenants, and Character — and top-down (macro) factors captured by additional Cs: Conditions, Country, and Currency. At its core, credit risk arises when a borrower's cash flow sources are insufficient to service debt.

The nature of credit risk differs by issuer type. For corporate issuers, secured debt is backed by operating cash flows plus pledged collateral, while unsecured debt relies solely on operating cash flows and secondary sources like asset sales. Sovereign issuers rely on tax revenues and tariffs, with secondary sources including additional borrowing or privatization. Analysts must distinguish between illiquidity (inability to raise cash) and insolvency (assets below liabilities), as an illiquid issuer can default without being insolvent. Bond indenture clauses such as cross-default (default on one issue triggers default on all) and pari passu (equal ranking among similar bonds) are critical in determining outcomes upon default.

Credit risk is quantified through the expected loss formula: Expected Loss = Probability of Default (PD) × Loss Given Default (LGD). The recovery rate is the fraction of principal recovered after default; loss severity equals 1 minus the recovery rate. LGD as a percentage equals expected exposure × (1 − recovery rate). This framework allows analysts to estimate a fair credit spread: Credit Spread ≈ PD × LGD%. If the actual market spread exceeds the estimated spread, investors are more than compensated for credit risk; if it is lower, the bond does not adequately reward investors.

Probability of default is assessed via quantitative capacity metrics such as EBIT margin, interest coverage ratio (EBIT/interest), debt/EBITDA leverage, and cash flow to net debt ratios. LGD is driven by a bond's position in the capital structure — senior secured debt has higher recovery rates and lower LGD than junior unsecured debt. Interestingly, while investment-grade issuers have lower PD than high-yield issuers, high-yield issuers often issue secured debt, which can result in lower LGD for some high-yield bonds compared to unsecured investment-grade bonds.
key_points: Expected Loss = Probability of Default × Loss Given Default (LGD); credit spread ≈ PD × LGD% — memorize this formula and be able to calculate whether a bond fairly compensates investors.
The 8 Cs of credit analysis: bottom-up (Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Covenants, Character) and top-down (Conditions, Country, Currency).
LGD% = Expected Exposure × (1 − Recovery Rate); Loss Severity = 1 − Recovery Rate — know how to move between recovery rate, loss severity, and LGD.
Distinguish illiquidity (can't raise cash, may still be solvent) from insolvency (assets < liabilities) — an issuer can default while solvent if illiquid.
Cross-default clause: default on one bond triggers default on all bonds of the issuer. Pari passu clause: all bonds of a given type rank equally in default — both are key indenture provisions.

Senior secured debt → higher recovery rate → lower LGD; junior unsecured debt → lower recovery rate → higher LGD. High-yield issuers often issue secured debt, so high-yield LGD can be lower than unsecured IG bonds.
If actual credit spread > estimated credit spread (PD × LGD%), investors are more than fairly compensated; if actual < estimated, avoid the bond.

readings.title: Credit Analysis For Government Issuers
readings.summary:
Sovereign government credit analysis centers on a government's ability and willingness to service debt, primarily through taxation. Unlike corporate issuers, sovereigns possess sovereign immunity — bondholders have no legal recourse if a government refuses to pay — making willingness to repay a distinct and critical factor. Analysts evaluate sovereigns across five qualitative factors: (1) institutions and policy stability, (2) fiscal flexibility, (3) monetary effectiveness (especially central bank independence), (4) economic flexibility, and (5) external/currency status including reserve currency privilege.

On the quantitative side, three categories matter: fiscal strength (debt-to-GDP, interest-to-revenue ratios), economic growth and stability (real GDP growth, per-capita income, volatility), and external stability (foreign exchange reserves relative to GDP and external debt). Countries with reserve currencies enjoy a structural advantage — they can issue debt in their own currency to foreign investors and sustain larger deficits. Commodity-dependent exporters face correlated sovereign and commodity price risk.

Non-sovereign government debt includes agencies, government sector banks, supranational entities (e.g., World Bank), and regional governments. Agencies and government sector banks carry implicit sovereign support and typically receive sovereign-level credit ratings. Supranationals depend on the collective backing of their member governments. Regional governments (states, provinces, municipalities) cannot use monetary policy and generally must balance operating budgets, making their credit quality closely tied to their local tax base.

Regional government bonds fall into two main categories: general obligation (GO) bonds, backed by the full taxing power of the issuer, and revenue bonds, which are repaid solely from cash flows generated by a specific project (airport, toll road, hospital). Revenue bonds generally carry higher credit risk than GO bonds because repayment depends on a single revenue stream. Revenue bond analysis mirrors corporate bond analysis, with a focus on the debt-service coverage ratio (net revenues divided by debt service).
key_points: Sovereign issuers have two unique advantages over municipals: (1) the ability to print money and (2) sovereign immunity — bondholders cannot force repayment, so willingness to pay is a separate analytical factor.

5 qualitative sovereign factors: Institutions/policy, Fiscal flexibility, Monetary effectiveness (independent central bank = lower inflation risk), Economic flexibility, External/currency status (reserve currency = lower risk).

3 quantitative sovereign factors: Fiscal strength (low debt/GDP, low interest/revenue), Economic growth & stability (high real GDP growth, high per-capita GDP), External stability (high FX reserves/GDP and FX reserves/external debt).
Higher debt burden ratios (debt/GDP, debt/revenue) and higher debt affordability ratios (interest/GDP) signal LOWER credit quality; higher FX reserve ratios signal HIGHER credit quality.
Agencies and government sector banks carry implicit (not explicit) sovereign support and receive near-sovereign credit ratings; supranational ratings depend on collective member-government backing.
GO bonds are secured by taxing power (lower risk); revenue bonds are repaid only from project cash flows (higher risk) — analyze revenue bonds using the debt-service coverage ratio.
Regional governments cannot print money and typically must balance operating budgets, so their credit quality is driven entirely by the strength of their local tax base.

readings.title: Credit Analysis For Corporate Issuers
readings.summary:
Credit analysis for corporate issuers combines qualitative and quantitative factors to evaluate the likelihood and severity of default. Qualitative factors include the stability of the business model, competitive intensity within the industry, business risk from issuer-specific or external sources, and the quality of corporate governance. Key governance concerns include debt covenants and accounting policies — aggressive revenue recognition, off-balance-sheet financing, and frequent auditor changes are red flags indicating management character risk.

Quantitative analysis forecasts future financial statements and cash flows using top-down (macro, industry, event risk) and bottom-up (issuer-specific revenues, costs, balance sheet) inputs — or a hybrid of both. Higher credit quality is associated with strong recurring operating profits, low leverage, high debt service coverage, and ample liquidity. Key cash flow measures used in ratio analysis include EBITDA, CFO, FFO, FCF, and RCF, each capturing a different aspect of cash generation and availability to debtholders.

Debt seniority and security structure directly affect recovery rates and credit ratings. The priority of claims in bankruptcy runs from senior secured, to senior unsecured, to subordinated debt, to equity. Higher seniority means lower loss given default, which supports better credit ratings. Notably, all debt within the same seniority tranche is treated equally — this is the cross-default provision concept underlying notching.

Credit ratings from agencies like Moody's, S&P, and Fitch are assigned at the issuer and issue level. Investment-grade bonds (BBB-/Baa3 and above) reflect low default probability, while high-yield (speculative-grade) bonds face higher default risk but offer higher yields as compensation. Rating agencies notch issue ratings up or down from the issuer rating based on seniority and collateral. Analysts must also assess event risks — leveraged buyouts, litigation, and regulatory changes — that can rapidly impair creditworthiness.
key_points: Qualitative credit factors: business model stability, industry competition, business risk, and corporate governance (covenants + accounting policies). High-yield issuers typically have negative covenants; investment-grade issuers primarily have affirmative covenants.

Key cash flow definitions: EBITDA = EBIT + D&A (ignores capex/working capital); FCF = CFO − capex + net interest; RCF = operating cash flow − dividends; FFO = net income + D&A + deferred taxes + noncash items (excludes working capital changes unlike CFO).

Core credit ratios — Profitability: EBIT margin, EBITDA margin; Coverage: EBIT/interest, EBITDA/interest, FFO/debt; Leverage: debt/EBITDA, debt/capital, FFO/debt, RCF/net debt. Higher coverage and lower leverage = higher credit quality.

Priority of claims in bankruptcy (senior to junior): senior secured → senior unsecured → subordinated → equity. Higher seniority = lower loss given default = better issue-level credit rating (notching up).
Investment-grade = BBB−/Baa3 and above; High-yield (speculative) = BB+/Ba1 and below. Fallen angels are former investment-grade issuers downgraded to high-yield; rising stars are the reverse.
Issue ratings are notched relative to the issuer rating based on collateral and seniority. Secured debt is notched up; subordinated debt is notched down from the issuer's baseline rating.
Event risk — LBOs, hostile takeovers, litigation, and regulatory changes — can cause sudden credit deterioration. Analysts must assess these tail risks even for currently high-rated issuers.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Securitization Module 65.1: Fixed-Income Securitization
readings.summary:
Securitization is a process by which an originator (a bank or corporation) pools debt-based assets and sells them to a Special Purpose Entity (SPE), which then issues Asset-Backed Securities (ABSs) to investors. The SPE — also called a trust, special purpose company, or SPV — is a legally separate entity that is bankruptcy remote from the originator, meaning the originator's financial troubles do not affect investors' claims on the collateral. Key parties include the seller/depositor (originator), the issuer/trust (SPE), the servicer (who collects payments and handles delinquencies), and a disinterested trustee who oversees safekeeping of collateral and cash flows.

Benefits to issuers (originators) are significant: securitization increases business activity by recycling capital to make new loans, improves profitability through origination and servicing fees, reduces regulatory capital reserve requirements by removing credit risk from the balance sheet, and improves liquidity by converting illiquid loan portfolios into cash proceeds.

Investors benefit from access to diversified loan pools without needing origination or servicing expertise, tailored risk/return profiles through structured tranches, and greater liquidity since ABSs trade more easily than underlying loans. At the economy-wide level, securitization lowers financing costs, reduces originator leverage, improves market efficiency through price discovery, and decreases liquidity risk in financial markets overall.

However, securitization carries important risks. Cash flows to ABS investors are uncertain in both timing and amount — for example, prepayment risk on mortgage loans. Credit risk of the underlying collateral is passed through to investors. The 2007–2009 financial crisis demonstrated how systemic buildup of credit risk within ABS structures can destabilize the broader financial system.
key_points: Securitization steps: originator pools assets → sells to SPE → SPE issues ABSs to investors. The SPE is the actual issuer of the ABS; 'issuer' in exam questions about benefits typically refers to the originator.
SPE is bankruptcy remote: legally separate from the originator, so originator insolvency does not impair ABS investors' claims on the collateral pool.
Four key parties: seller/depositor (originator), issuer/trust (SPE), servicer (collects payments, handles delinquencies), and disinterested trustee (oversees collateral and cash flow safekeeping).
Benefits to originators (issuers): increased lending capacity, fee income, lower regulatory capital requirements, and improved liquidity from converting illiquid loans to cash.
Benefits to investors: access to diversified loan exposure without origination/servicing expertise, tailored risk/return, and greater secondary market liquidity vs. holding loans directly.
Macro benefits: lower financing costs, reduced originator leverage, improved price discovery/market efficiency, and decreased systemic liquidity risk.
Key risks: uncertain cash flow timing/size (e.g., prepayment risk) and credit risk pass-through to investors; systemic ABS credit risk concentration contributed to the 2007–2009 financial crisis.

readings.title: Asset-Backed Security (ABS) Instrument And Market
readings.summary:
Asset-backed securities (ABS) are created through securitization, where financial assets are pooled and sold to a special purpose entity (SPE), which then issues securities backed by those cash flows. A key alternative structure is the covered bond, where the underlying assets (the cover pool) remain on the issuer's balance sheet rather than being transferred to an SPE. Covered bond investors benefit from dual recourse—claims on both the cover pool and the issuer's unencumbered assets—making them lower risk than comparable ABSs. Covered bonds require the issuer to maintain the cover pool by replacing non-performing or prepaid assets, and come in three default structures: hard-bullet (immediate acceleration), soft-bullet (maturity postponed up to one year), and conditional pass-through (converts to pass-through if payments remain due at maturity).

Credit enhancement is central to ABS structuring and comes in internal and external forms. The three primary internal enhancements are overcollateralization (collateral value exceeds face value of ABS issued), excess spread (income earned on collateral exceeds coupon paid to investors, building a reserve), and credit tranching/subordination (structuring multiple tranches where junior tranches absorb losses first). In a waterfall structure, the most junior 'equity' tranche absorbs losses first, then mezzanine tranches, and finally the senior tranche—allowing senior tranches to achieve higher credit ratings than the originating institution itself.

Non-mortgage ABSs can be backed by a wide variety of assets including credit card receivables, auto loans, solar panel loans, and even music royalties. A critical distinction is whether the underlying loans are amortizing (scheduled principal repayment, e.g., auto loans) or non-amortizing (no scheduled principal repayment, e.g., credit card receivables). Credit card ABSs use a lockout/revolving period where principal repayments are reinvested in new receivables rather than passed through to investors, followed by a principal amortization period. They also include early amortization triggers to protect investors if the excess spread turns negative or the issuer faces financial distress.

Residential solar ABSs are backed by loans to homeowners for solar panel installation. These are amortizing loans with prepayment risk, similar to mortgage-backed securities. The creditworthiness depends on borrower ability to repay and, uniquely, on the energy production of the solar panels. CDOs (Collateralized Debt Obligations) are a broader category of ABS where the collateral consists of debt obligations such as bonds or loans, with cash CDOs backed by actual debt instruments and synthetic CDOs using credit derivatives to gain exposure to reference assets without actual ownership.
key_points: Covered bonds vs. ABS: covered bonds keep assets ON the issuer's balance sheet (no SPE), provide dual recourse (cover pool + unencumbered assets), require dynamic pool maintenance, and generally have lower yields due to stronger credit protection.

Three internal credit enhancements: (1) overcollateralization — collateral > face value of ABS; (2) excess spread — collateral income > coupon paid to investors; (3) subordination/tranching — junior tranches absorb losses first (waterfall structure).

Covered bond default structures: hard-bullet = immediate default/acceleration; soft-bullet = maturity postponed up to 1 year; conditional pass-through = converts to pass-through at maturity if payments remain due.

Amortizing vs. non-amortizing collateral: auto loans are amortizing (prepayment risk key); credit card receivables are non-amortizing (principal repaid at borrower's discretion, revolving structure with lockout period).

Credit card ABS lockout/revolving period: principal repayments are reinvested in new receivables (not passed to investors); after lockout ends, principal amortization begins. Early amortization triggers protect investors if excess spread turns negative.

In a CDO waterfall, the equity/residual tranche bears first loss and earns the highest spread; the senior tranche is most protected and earns the lowest spread. Senior tranches can be rated higher than the originator itself due to the SPE's bankruptcy-remote structure.

Excess spread is the primary first line of defense against collateral losses; if it turns negative in a credit card ABS, early amortization is triggered — a critical risk signal to monitor on the exam.

readings.title: Derivative Instrument And Derivative Market Features
readings.summary: A derivative is a financial security whose value is derived from an underlying asset or variable (such as a stock price, bond price, index level, interest rate, or currency) at a specified future date. The most basic example is a forward contract, which obligates one party to buy and another to sell an underlying asset at a predetermined forward price on a future settlement date. At initiation, the forward price is set so that neither party pays anything — the contract has zero value to both sides at inception.
key_points: A derivative derives its value from an underlying asset or variable; the forward price is set at initiation so that the contract has zero initial value to both parties.

Forward contract P&L: the BUYER profits when spot price > forward price (long exposure); the SELLER profits when spot price < forward price (short exposure). Gains of one party exactly equal losses of the other (zero-sum).
Deliverable vs. cash-settled contracts: deliverable requires physical exchange of asset and cash; cash-settled requires only the net gain/loss to change hands — both are economically equivalent.
Hedging = using a derivative to offset an existing risk (fully or partially). Speculation = using a derivative to take on NEW risk with no offsetting position in the underlying.
Key advantages of derivatives over cash market transactions: (1) low-cost leveraged exposure, (2) lower transaction costs, (3) less market-price impact on the underlying.

Exchange-traded derivatives (futures, standardized options) are standardized and traded on exchanges with a clearinghouse guaranteeing performance; OTC derivatives are customized bilateral contracts with counterparty credit (default) risk.
Underlying assets can be equities, bonds, indexes, currencies, interest rates, or commodities — each transfers a specific type of price or rate risk between hedgers and speculators.

readings.title: Forward Commitment And Contingent Claim Features
readings.summary:
Forward commitments are contracts that obligate both parties to transact at a future date. A forward contract is a private, bilateral agreement where the buyer commits to purchase and the seller commits to sell an asset at a specified price on a specified future date. The buyer holds long exposure (profits if price rises above forward price) and the seller holds short exposure (profits if price falls). Because forwards are privately negotiated, they carry significant counterparty credit risk and have limited liquidity.

Futures contracts are economically similar to forwards but are standardized and exchange-traded. Key differences include: futures trade in a liquid secondary market, are regulated, and are backed by a central clearinghouse that virtually eliminates counterparty risk. Futures require both parties to post initial margin and are subject to daily mark-to-market (marking-to-market), where gains and losses are settled in cash each day. If the margin balance falls below the maintenance margin level, the account holder receives a margin call and must restore the balance to the initial margin level — not just the maintenance margin level, unlike equity margin calls.

Swaps are agreements to exchange a series of cash flows over multiple settlement dates. The most common type is a fixed-for-floating interest rate swap, where one party pays a fixed rate (the swap rate) and the other pays a floating rate (e.g., SOFR) based on a notional principal. At each settlement date, only the net payment changes hands. The swap rate is set so that the swap has zero value at inception. A swap is economically equivalent to a series of forward contracts and can be used to convert floating-rate liabilities into fixed-rate liabilities (or vice versa).

Options are contingent claims — unlike forward commitments, they give the buyer the right but not the obligation to transact. A call option gives the right to buy; a put option gives the right to sell. The buyer pays a premium upfront. Options can be European (exercise only at expiration) or American (exercise any time before or at expiration). The maximum loss for the option buyer is the premium paid; for the seller (writer), the risk can be substantial. Credit derivatives, such as credit default swaps (CDS), allow one party to transfer credit risk to another — the protection buyer pays periodic premiums and receives a payoff if a reference entity defaults.
key_points: Forwards vs. Futures: Forwards are private/OTC with counterparty risk; futures are exchange-traded, standardized, backed by a clearinghouse, and require daily mark-to-market. Futures have greater liquidity and transparency.
Futures margin calls require restoration to INITIAL margin (not maintenance margin) — this is a classic exam trap distinguishing futures from equity margin calls.
Mark-to-market mechanics: gains/losses are settled daily in cash based on the new settlement price; a margin call occurs only when balance falls BELOW maintenance margin.
Swaps are zero-value at inception; the swap rate is set to make both sides equal in present value. Only net payments are exchanged at each settlement date.
A fixed-for-floating interest rate swap can convert a floating-rate liability to a fixed-rate liability — the floating-rate payer hedges interest rate risk by becoming the fixed-rate payer.
Options are contingent claims (buyer has right, not obligation); the buyer's maximum loss equals the premium paid. Call = right to buy; Put = right to sell.
A credit default swap (CDS) transfers credit risk: protection buyer pays periodic premiums; protection seller pays if the reference entity defaults — analogous to insurance on a bond.

readings.title: Derivative Benefits, Risks, And Issuer And Investor Uses
readings.summary:
Derivatives offer significant advantages over direct cash market transactions. Key benefits include the ability to alter risk exposure without transacting in the underlying asset (e.g., hedging FX risk with forwards, converting floating-rate debt to fixed via swaps), information discovery (implied volatility from options prices, expected future rates from interest rate futures), and operational advantages such as ease of short selling, lower transaction costs, greater leverage, and improved liquidity. These features collectively improve overall market efficiency by reducing the cost of exploiting mispricings.

However, derivatives carry meaningful risks that must be understood. Implicit leverage is a primary concern—futures margins of 3–12% imply leverage ratios of 8:1 to 33:1, amplifying both gains and losses. Basis risk arises when the derivative's underlying does not perfectly match the hedged position (e.g., hedging a 50-stock portfolio with an S&P 500 forward) or when settlement dates differ from the hedging horizon. Liquidity risk is unique to derivatives: even a well-constructed hedge can fail if margin calls cannot be met in cash, forcing premature close-out.

Counterparty credit risk differs by contract type. Option sellers bear no counterparty risk once the premium is received; option buyers face risk if the option expires in the money. Both parties in a forward contract can face counterparty risk. Futures mitigate this through initial margin, daily mark-to-market, and central clearinghouse guarantees. Systemic risk—the potential for widespread market disruption from excessive speculation—has prompted regulatory responses such as mandatory central clearing for swaps.

Issuers (typically corporations) use derivatives to manage earnings volatility and balance sheet risk: hedging FX income with forwards, using interest rate swaps to convert fixed-rate debt into floating-rate liabilities with lower duration, or reducing commodity input cost volatility. Investors use derivatives to adjust portfolio exposures—buying puts to protect downside, using calls to gain upside without full capital outlay, and employing futures for efficient market exposure. The core distinction is that issuers focus on stabilizing cash flows and reported financials, while investors focus on return enhancement and risk management within portfolios.
key_points: Four operational advantages of derivatives over cash markets: (1) easier short selling, (2) lower transaction costs, (3) greater leverage, (4) greater liquidity — all improve market efficiency.
Implicit leverage is the primary risk: CME futures margins of 3–12% imply 8:1 to 33:1 leverage; a 1% price move can cause a 25% change in margin value.
Basis risk arises when the derivative's underlying differs from the hedged exposure (asset mismatch) OR when settlement dates differ from the hedging horizon (date mismatch).
Liquidity risk in derivatives: a hedge can be technically correct but fail if cash margin calls cannot be met, forcing the position to close before expiration (e.g., farmer short wheat futures).
Counterparty credit risk asymmetry: option sellers face ZERO counterparty risk; option buyers face risk if in-the-money at settlement; both parties in a forward face bilateral credit risk.
Futures reduce counterparty risk through three mechanisms: initial margin deposit, daily mark-to-market (variation margin), and central clearinghouse guarantee.

Issuers use derivatives to smooth earnings and manage balance sheet values (FX forwards, interest rate swaps); investors use derivatives to adjust portfolio risk/return profiles (protective puts, calls for upside exposure, futures for index exposure).

readings.title: Arbitrage, Replication, And The Cost Of Carry In Pricing
readings.summary:
Derivative pricing rests on the no-arbitrage condition rather than discounting expected future cash flows. Arbitrage is the simultaneous purchase and sale of equivalent assets at different prices to earn a risk-free profit. Because such opportunities are exploited instantly by market participants, prices quickly adjust to eliminate them. This principle — the law of one price — says that two portfolios with identical future payoffs in all scenarios must have the same price today. Transaction costs create a small band around the theoretical price within which arbitrage is not worth pursuing.

The no-arbitrage forward price is derived by comparing two strategies that deliver the same payoff at expiration. For a non-dividend-paying stock trading at S0, buying the stock outright and holding it produces the same payoff (S1) as buying a zero-cost forward and investing the present value of the forward price in a risk-free bond. Setting these two costs equal gives the fundamental forward pricing formula: F0(T) = S0(1 + Rf)^T. If the forward trades above this price, the arbitrage is to sell the forward and buy the stock (funded by borrowing). If it trades below, the arbitrage is to buy the forward and short-sell the stock, investing the proceeds at the risk-free rate.

Replication means constructing a cash-market portfolio whose payoffs exactly mirror a derivative under all future outcomes. A long forward is replicated by borrowing S0 at Rf and buying the underlying; a short forward is replicated by shorting the underlying and investing the proceeds. Because the replicating portfolio and the derivative have identical payoffs, they must have identical prices — confirming F0(T) = S0(1 + Rf)^T.

The cost of carry framework extends this logic to assets with additional holding costs or benefits. Storage, insurance, and spoilage costs (PV of costs) increase the no-arbitrage forward price because the arbitrageur must pay them when holding the physical asset. Conversely, cash benefits like dividends or coupon payments, and non-monetary benefits like convenience yield, reduce the forward price because the cash-and-carry arbitrageur forgoes them. The general formula becomes F0(T) = [S0 + PV0(costs) − PV0(benefits)](1 + Rf)^T. For equities with continuous dividends or bonds with known coupons, benefits are subtracted directly from S0 before compounding.
key_points: No-arbitrage forward price formula: F0(T) = S0(1 + Rf)^T for an asset with no carrying costs or benefits. This is the foundational equation — memorize it.

If forward price > no-arbitrage value: sell the forward, buy (borrow to buy) the underlying — 'cash-and-carry' arbitrage. If forward price < no-arbitrage value: buy the forward, short-sell the underlying — 'reverse cash-and-carry' arbitrage.

Replication: a long forward = borrow S0 at Rf + buy underlying; a short forward = short-sell underlying + invest proceeds at Rf. The payoff of the replicating portfolio equals the derivative payoff for any future price.
Cost of carry adjustment: storage/insurance INCREASES the forward price (F0 = [S0 + PV(costs)](1 + Rf)^T); cash benefits like dividends or coupons DECREASE it (F0 = [S0 − PV(benefits)](1 + Rf)^T).

Convenience yield is a non-monetary benefit of physically holding a commodity (e.g., during a supply shortage). It acts like a dividend — it reduces the forward price below the pure cost-of-carry price.
Transaction costs prevent perfect arbitrage: small price deviations from the no-arbitrage value persist as long as the deviation is smaller than the cost of executing the arbitrage trade.

The spot price and expected future price differ because of carry costs/benefits: F0(T) ≠ E[ST] in general; the forward price is cost-of-carry adjusted, not a simple expectation of the future spot price.

readings.title: Pricing And Valuation Of Forward Contracts And For
readings.summary: A forward contract's value at initiation is zero when priced at the no-arbitrage forward price F0(T) = S0(1 + Rf)^T. This is because the present value of the forward price exactly equals the current spot price, leaving no immediate gain or loss for either party. The forward price is set so that neither buyer nor seller has an economic advantage at the start.
key_points: At initiation, the value of a forward contract is zero: V0(T) = S0 - F0(T)(1+Rf)^-T = 0
During the life of the contract, value to the buyer = St - F0(T)(1+Rf)^-(T-t); rises when spot price rises, which benefits the long (buyer)
At expiration, value to buyer = ST - F0(T); the long gains if ST > F0(T), loses if ST < F0(T); gains/losses are zero-sum between buyer and seller
Forward rate notation: Fx,y means a y-year loan starting x years from now (e.g., F2,3 = 3-year loan starting in 2 years, covering years 2 through 5)
Implied forward rate no-arbitrage condition: (1+Zn)^n = (1+Zm)^m × (1+Fm,n-m); solve by equating terminal values of two lending strategies
In an FRA, the fixed-rate payer (long) pays the forward rate; settlement is the PV of the interest differential on the notional, discounted at the realized reference rate
FRAs are used by financial institutions to hedge interest rate risk on assets/liabilities and are equivalent to a single-period interest rate swap

readings.title: Pricing And Valuation Of Futures Contracts
readings.summary:
This reading distinguishes between the price and value of forward and futures contracts. For a standard forward contract with no interim cash flows, the forward price is fixed at initiation and remains constant throughout the contract's life, while the contract's value fluctuates daily as the underlying asset price moves. At settlement, the payoff equals the difference between the spot price and the original forward price. Futures contracts behave differently: both the price and value change each day, but the daily mark-to-market settlement process resets the contract's value to zero at the end of each trading day.

Interest rate futures are a special category of futures that are quoted on a price basis rather than a rate basis. The futures price is expressed as 100 minus the annualized market reference rate (MRR) for the relevant period. The basis point value (BPV) of an interest rate futures contract is calculated as: notional principal × period × 0.01%, representing the dollar change in contract value for each one basis point move in the MRR.

The key reason forward and futures prices can differ is the daily settlement feature of futures. When interest rates are positively correlated with futures prices, long futures holders receive daily gains precisely when rates are high (so those gains can be reinvested at higher rates) and pay losses when rates are low (lower opportunity cost). This asymmetry makes futures more attractive than equivalent forwards in a positive correlation environment, and less attractive in a negative correlation environment. If rates are constant or uncorrelated with futures prices, forward and futures prices are identical.

A further distinction arises with interest rate instruments due to convexity bias. Forward rate agreements (FRAs) settle by discounting the interest differential at the realized MRR, creating an asymmetric payoff profile similar to bond convexity — a rate increase reduces the FRA's value by less than an equal rate decrease raises it. Futures contracts do not have this discounting feature; their payoffs are linear in basis points. This convexity bias causes interest rate futures prices to differ from equivalent FRA prices, with the gap growing larger for longer-dated contracts.
key_points: Forward price is FIXED at initiation; forward VALUE fluctuates over time. Futures price AND value both change daily, but daily mark-to-market resets futures value to zero each day.
Interest rate futures are quoted as: Futures Price = 100 − (100 × MRR). Example: price of 97 implies MRR = 3%. This is the opposite direction from rates — price up means rates down.
BPV (Basis Point Value) for interest rate futures = Notional Principal × Period × 0.0001. For €1M on a 6-month contract: BPV = €1,000,000 × 0.5 × 0.0001 = €50 per basis point.

Futures > Forwards when interest rates and futures prices are POSITIVELY correlated (gains reinvested at high rates). Futures < Forwards when NEGATIVELY correlated. Equal when uncorrelated or constant.

Convexity bias: FRA (forward) payoffs are discounted at the realized MRR, creating bond-like convexity — rate increases hurt less than equal rate decreases help. Futures payoffs are linear, so no such convexity exists.

The arbitrage-free forward price for an asset with no holding costs or benefits equals the FUTURE VALUE of the current spot price compounded at the risk-free rate — not the expected future spot price.
In practice, forward-futures price differences are negligible for short-maturity contracts, but convexity bias can be significant for long-dated interest rate instruments.

readings.title: Pricing And Valuation Of Interest Rates And Other
readings.summary:
An interest-rate swap is an agreement where one party pays a fixed rate and the other pays a floating rate (tied to a market reference rate, MRR) on a notional principal. Only the net difference between the fixed and floating payments is exchanged at each settlement date. The notional principal itself never changes hands; it serves only as the basis for calculating payment amounts.

A fixed-for-floating swap can be decomposed into a series of forward rate agreements (FRAs). The first payment is known at initiation because it is based on the current spot MRR. Each subsequent payment is equivalent to a long FRA position (for the fixed-rate payer) with a contract rate equal to the swap fixed rate. The key difference from a simple series of FRAs is that all FRAs in the equivalent series share the same contract rate, which means individual FRAs are not each zero-value at initiation — some will have positive value and some negative, but their sum equals zero.

The swap fixed rate that sets the total value of the swap to zero at initiation is called the par swap rate. It is derived using no-arbitrage pricing: the present value of all fixed payments must equal the present value of all expected floating payments, discounted using current spot rates. Conceptually, the fixed-rate payer replicates the swap by borrowing at a fixed rate and lending at the floating rate.

Once a swap is live, its value fluctuates with changes in expected future short-term rates. The price of the swap (the fixed rate stated in the contract) remains constant, but the value of each party's position changes. Rising expected future rates increase the value of the fixed-rate payer position (they locked in a lower rate), while falling expected rates decrease it. Mark-to-market valuation equals the present value of expected future floating payments minus the present value of future fixed payments, using current spot and implied forward rates.
key_points: A fixed-for-floating swap exchanges net payments: fixed-rate payer receives (MRR - F) each period; only the difference is settled, not the notional principal.
A swap is equivalent to a series of FRAs all with the same contract rate (the swap fixed rate) — unlike standalone FRAs, individual component FRAs are NOT zero-value at initiation.
The par swap rate is the fixed rate that makes total swap value = 0 at initiation: PV(fixed payments) = PV(expected floating payments), solved using current spot rates.
PRICE of a swap = the fixed rate specified in the contract (constant); VALUE of a swap = changes daily based on shifts in expected future floating rates.
Fixed-rate payer benefits from rising rates (value of their position increases); fixed-rate receiver benefits from falling rates.
The fixed-rate payer position is synthetically replicated by borrowing at a fixed rate and lending at a floating rate — or equivalently, issuing a fixed-rate bond and buying a floating-rate note.
Mark-to-market swap value = PV(expected future floating payments) − PV(future fixed payments), recalculated using current spot rates and implied forward rates at the valuation date.

readings.title: Pricing And Valuation Of Options
readings.summary: Options are contingent claims whose value depends on whether the holder chooses to exercise. Moneyness describes the relationship between the underlying asset price (S) and the exercise price (X): a call is in-the-money when S > X, and a put is in-the-money when X > S. The exercise value (intrinsic value) equals the in-the-money amount if positive, or zero otherwise. Before expiration, an option's market price (premium) exceeds its exercise value by the time value, which reflects the probability that future price movements could increase the payoff. At expiration, time value is zero and the option is worth only its exercise value or zero.
key_points: Moneyness: Call ITM when S > X (payoff = S − X); Put ITM when X > S (payoff = X − S); both ATM when S = X.
Option Premium = Exercise Value (Intrinsic Value) + Time Value. Time value is always ≥ 0 and decays to zero at expiration.
Upper bound for European call: c₀ ≤ S₀ (never pay more than the stock itself). Upper bound for European put: p₀ ≤ X(1+Rf)^-T (PV of exercise price, since you must wait until expiration).
Lower bound for European call: c₀ ≥ Max[0, S₀ − X(1+Rf)^-T]. Lower bound for European put: p₀ ≥ Max[0, X(1+Rf)^-T − S₀]. These are derived via no-arbitrage portfolios.
Put-Call Parity: c₀ + X(1+Rf)^-T = p₀ + S₀. A fiduciary call (long call + long risk-free bond) equals a protective put (long put + long stock) in value.
Option pricing uses replication rather than the simple no-arbitrage cost-of-carry model used for forwards, because options have asymmetric payoffs and a positive initial premium paid by the buyer.

Key factors increasing option value: higher volatility increases both call and put values; longer time to expiration increases time value; higher risk-free rate increases call value and decreases put value.

readings.title: Option Replication Using Put–Call Parity
readings.summary:
Put–call parity is derived by comparing two portfolios that produce identical payoffs at expiration: a fiduciary call (a European call option plus a zero-coupon risk-free bond paying X at expiration) and a protective put (a share of stock plus a European put option with strike X). Regardless of whether the stock price ends above or below X, both portfolios pay the same amount — either S (when S ≥ X) or X (when S < X). Because no-arbitrage requires identical-payoff portfolios to have equal prices, we get the core equation: c + X(1 + Rf)^(–T) = S + p.

From this single equation, any one of the four components can be isolated and synthetically replicated using the other three.
