A long stock position, for example, equals a long call, short put, and long bond (S = c – p + X(1+Rf)^(–T)). The sign in each rearrangement tells you whether to go long (+) or short (–) each instrument. This synthetic replication framework is central to derivatives pricing and risk management.

Put–call–forward parity extends the concept by replacing the underlying asset with a synthetic asset built from a forward contract. Since a long forward plus a risk-free bond that pays F0(T) at maturity replicates owning the asset at time T, we substitute F0(T)(1+Rf)^(–T) for S in the parity equation. This gives: c0 + X(1+Rf)^(–T) = F0(T)(1+Rf)^(–T) + p0, or equivalently p0 – c0 = [X – F0(T)](1+Rf)^(–T).

Finally, put–call parity has a powerful application in corporate finance: equity can be viewed as a call option on firm value (with strike = face value of debt D), and debt can be viewed as a risk-free bond minus a put option on firm value. This framework links option pricing theory directly to capital structure analysis and credit risk assessment.
key_points: Core put–call parity equation: c + X(1+Rf)^(–T) = S + p — memorize this and know that it applies ONLY to European options with the same strike and expiration.
Fiduciary call = call + risk-free bond paying X; Protective put = stock + put at X. Both have identical payoffs, forcing equal prices (no-arbitrage).

Synthetic replication: isolate any security by rearranging parity. Long stock = long call + short put + long bond; Long call = long stock + long put + short bond, etc. The sign (+ or –) tells you long vs. short.
Put–call–forward parity replaces S with F0(T)(1+Rf)^(–T): c0 + X(1+Rf)^(–T) = F0(T)(1+Rf)^(–T) + p0. The underlying asset itself is NOT held — it is replaced by a forward contract plus a bond.
Simplified put–call–forward parity: p0 – c0 = [X – F0(T)](1+Rf)^(–T). If forward price equals strike, put and call prices are equal.
Corporate finance application: Equity = call option on firm value V with strike D (debt face value); Debt = long risk-free bond – short put on V with strike D.
Exam calculation tip: to find a missing option price, rearrange parity algebraically (e.g., c = S + p – X(1+Rf)^(–T)) and plug in given values — always discount the strike price to present value.

readings.title: Valuing A Derivative Using A One-Period Binomial Model
readings.summary:
The one-period binomial model prices options by assuming the underlying asset can move to only one of two possible values over a single period. To apply the model, you need the current price of the underlying, the option's exercise price, the up-move and down-move return factors, and the risk-free rate. Actual probabilities of an up- or down-move are not needed for the no-arbitrage approach.

The no-arbitrage method constructs a hedge portfolio by buying h shares of the underlying and writing one call option. The hedge ratio h is chosen so the portfolio has the same value regardless of which price move occurs, eliminating risk. Because the portfolio is riskless, it must earn the risk-free rate. Setting the discounted certain payoff equal to the initial portfolio cost allows you to back out the option's fair value: c₀ = hS₀ − V₀.

An alternative but equivalent approach uses risk-neutral probabilities derived from the up/down move sizes and the risk-free rate. These pseudo-probabilities are not the real-world likelihoods of price moves; they are mathematical constructs that emerge from the no-arbitrage hedge. Once calculated, the option value equals the risk-neutral probability-weighted average of the expiration payoffs, discounted at the risk-free rate.

The same framework prices both calls and puts — the only difference is the payoff function at expiration (call: max(0, S−X); put: max(0, X−S)). In practice, many short periods are chained together to form a multi-period binomial tree, but the one-period model captures the core logic: no-arbitrage pricing via a riskless hedge, with the risk-free rate as the only discount rate needed.
key_points: The no-arbitrage binomial model does NOT require actual (real-world) probabilities of an up- or down-move — only the size of the moves and the risk-free rate are needed.
Hedge ratio h = (c_u − c_d) / (S_u − S_d): the number of shares to buy per call written that makes the portfolio riskless over one period.
Option value via no-arbitrage: V₀ = (certain portfolio payoff) / (1 + Rf), then c₀ = hS₀ − V₀.
Risk-neutral probabilities are pseudo-probabilities derived from move sizes and Rf; they are NOT estimates of actual up/down probabilities.
Risk-neutral pricing steps: (1) find risk-neutral π_u and π_d, (2) compute expected option payoff = π_u × c_u + π_d × c_d, (3) discount at Rf.
Both methods (no-arbitrage hedge and risk-neutral probabilities) yield the same option value — they are mathematically equivalent.
The risk-free rate is used as the discount rate in binomial option pricing because the hedge portfolio is riskless, not because we assume investors are risk-neutral in real life.

readings.title: Alternative Investment Performance And Returns
readings.summary:
Alternative investments carry additional risks beyond traditional long-only investments, stemming from irregular cash flow timing, leverage use, illiquid/hard-to-value assets, and complex fee structures. These risk factors mean that evaluating alternative investment returns without risk adjustment is naïve and potentially misleading. Investors must understand these dimensions to properly appraise performance.

The J-curve effect describes the typical life cycle of a private fund: early negative returns during the capital commitment phase (capital calls, no income yet), continued negative or flat returns in the capital deployment phase (investments being built), and eventual positive, accelerating returns in the capital distribution phase (exits and income). Because fund managers control the timing of cash flows, IRR (money-weighted return) is the most appropriate performance measure over a fund's life. The multiple of invested capital (money multiple) is a simpler alternative but ignores timing.

Leverage amplifies both gains and losses. The leveraged return formula is: r_L = r + (V_B/V_0)(r - r_B), where V_B is borrowed capital, V_0 is initial equity, r is portfolio return, and r_B is the borrowing rate. Leverage enables funds to exploit small pricing anomalies but creates risks such as margin calls, forced liquidations at unfavorable prices, and restricted access to future borrowing.

Valuation of illiquid alternative investments follows a three-level fair value hierarchy. Level 1 assets have quoted market prices; Level 2 use observable inputs via models; Level 3 require unobservable inputs (e.g., private equity, real estate). Level 3 valuations tend to be sticky near cost, causing reported returns to appear smoother, higher, and less correlated with public markets than they truly are — a phenomenon called return smoothing.

Fee structures in alternative investments (management fees, performance fees, high-water marks, hurdle rates, and waterfall distributions) significantly affect net investor returns and are negotiable. Lockup periods, notice periods, redemption fees, and gates are mechanisms funds use to manage redemption risk and protect against forced liquidations. Different investors in the same fund may face different fee terms and thus realize materially different net returns.
key_points: J-curve: alternative fund returns are typically negative early (capital commitment/deployment phases) and turn positive in the capital distribution phase — memorize the three phases and their return patterns.

IRR (money-weighted return) is the preferred performance measure for alternative investments because managers control cash flow timing; money multiple (total distributions + residual value ÷ total paid-in capital) is simpler but ignores timing.
Leveraged return formula: r_L = r + (V_B/V_0)(r − r_B). Leverage magnifies gains when r > r_B and magnifies losses when r < r_B; margin calls can force liquidations at unfavorable prices.

Fair value hierarchy: Level 1 = quoted market prices, Level 2 = observable model inputs, Level 3 = unobservable inputs. Level 3 valuations (private equity, real estate) cause return smoothing — overstating Sharpe ratios and understating correlations with public markets.
Lockup periods prevent early redemptions; notice periods (30–90 days) give managers time for orderly liquidation; gates temporarily restrict redemptions; redemption fees offset transaction costs.

High-water marks ensure managers only earn performance fees on new profits above a prior peak NAV, protecting investors from paying twice for recovered losses. Hard hurdle rates mean fees are earned only on returns above the hurdle; soft hurdle rates allow fees on all returns once the hurdle is cleared.

Different limited partners in the same fund can negotiate different fee terms (e.g., lower fees for early capital commitments or larger allocations), resulting in materially different net returns for investors in the same fund.

readings.title: Investments In Private Capital: Equity And Debt
readings.summary: Private capital refers to funding raised outside public markets, encompassing both private equity and private debt. Private equity funds invest in private companies or take public companies private. The two dominant fund types are leveraged buyout (LBO) funds, which acquire companies using significant debt financing with the goal of improving operations and cash flows before exiting, and venture capital (VC) funds, which provide early-stage financing to startups in exchange for equity or convertible securities. LBO sub-types include management buyouts (MBOs), where existing management participates in the purchase, and management buy-ins (MBIs), where new management is brought in.
readings.summary_continued: Venture capital investments are categorized by the portfolio company's development stage. The formative stage includes pre-seed (angel investing at the idea stage), seed-stage (product development and market research), and early-stage (pre-revenue operations). Later-stage or expansion VC supports companies that have begun production and sales. Mezzanine-stage financing prepares a firm for an IPO and is distinct from mezzanine financing (hybrid debt/equity instruments). Private equity firms can also take minority stakes in public companies through PIPEs (private investments in public equity), which offer faster execution and lower costs than public offerings.
readings.summary_paragraph3: Private equity firms typically exit investments after an average holding period of five years. Exit strategies include: trade sales (selling to a strategic buyer, often at a premium but with limited buyers); public listings via IPO (highest price realization but costly and complex), direct listing (lower cost, no new capital raised), or SPAC (flexible but dilutive with regulatory scrutiny); recapitalization (issuing debt to pay a dividend to equity holders—not a true exit); secondary sales (to another PE firm); and write-offs/liquidations for failed investments.
readings.summary_paragraph4: Private debt includes instruments such as direct lending, mezzanine debt, venture debt, and distressed debt. Direct lending typically involves senior secured loans to mid-market companies, while mezzanine debt sits between senior debt and equity in the capital structure and often includes equity participation features like warrants. Distressed debt investing targets companies in or near bankruptcy and requires specialized workout expertise. Private debt generally offers higher yields than comparable public debt to compensate for illiquidity.
readings.summary_paragraph5: Risk and return characteristics of private capital are important exam topics. Private equity has historically outperformed public equity on average, but with higher volatility and illiquidity risk. Performance measures are complicated by infrequent revaluations, self-reporting biases, survivorship bias, and backfill bias—all of which tend to overstate reported returns and understate volatility. Common performance metrics include IRR (internal rate of return) and MOIC/investment multiples, while the J-curve effect describes early negative returns in a fund's life before value is realized.
key_points: LBO funds acquire companies using high leverage; MBO = existing management buys in; MBI = new management replaces existing team. Value is added by improving operations and cash flows to service debt.

VC stages in order: Pre-seed (angel, idea stage) → Seed (product/market research, first VC entry) → Early-stage (pre-revenue operations) → Later-stage/Expansion (post-revenue growth) → Mezzanine-stage (pre-IPO prep). Mezzanine-stage ≠ mezzanine financing.

Exit strategy comparison: IPO = highest price but most costly/complex; Trade sale = fastest with strategic premium but limited buyers; Direct listing = no underwriters, no new capital; SPAC = flexible but dilutive; Recapitalization = NOT a true exit (fund retains control); Secondary sale = sell to another PE firm.
PIPE (Private Investment in Public Equity) = minority stake purchase in a public company via private offering; faster and cheaper than a public offering with fewer disclosures.

Private equity return measures are biased upward due to: (1) survivorship bias (failed funds not reported), (2) backfill bias (only successful track records added retroactively), and (3) infrequent portfolio company revaluations understating true volatility.

Private debt hierarchy: Senior secured (lowest risk/yield) → Mezzanine debt (subordinated, often with equity warrants) → Distressed debt (highest risk, targets near-bankrupt companies). All offer illiquidity premium over public debt.

J-curve effect: PE funds show negative returns early in their life (fees + unrealized investments) before turning positive as portfolio companies mature and are exited. IRR and investment multiples (MOIC) are the primary PE performance metrics.

readings.title: Real Estate And Infrastructure
readings.summary:
Real estate investments can be organized along two dimensions: public vs. private and debt vs. equity. Private real estate involves direct ownership or lending (mortgages), while public real estate involves securities like REITs, ETFs, MBSs, and CMBSs. Equity investors hold ownership interests and control key decisions, while debt investors (lenders) have priority claims in default — meaning equity value equals property value minus outstanding debt. Private investments tend to be larger, illiquid, and require specialized management, whereas public investments offer liquidity and divisibility.

Direct real estate investment offers benefits including portfolio control, diversification (low correlation with stocks/bonds), and tax advantages from depreciation deductions. However, it comes with significant drawbacks: illiquidity, price opacity, high capital requirements, management complexity, and concentration risk. Indirect investment vehicles — including limited partnerships, joint ventures, and REITs — address many of these drawbacks by offering professional management and broader diversification.

REITs are the primary indirect real estate vehicle, exempt from double taxation and exchange-traded (avoiding redemption risk). They come in three types: equity REITs (own properties), mortgage REITs (lend or hold MBS/CMBS), and hybrid REITs (both). REITs pursue strategies ranging from low-risk core (stable, high-quality properties, open-end structure) to core-plus, value-add, and opportunistic (higher risk, closed-end structure with finite lives).

Infrastructure investments include transportation (roads, airports, railways), utilities (gas, electric, waste), information/communication technology (telecom towers), and social infrastructure (schools, prisons, hospitals). These can be accessed by building and selling/leasing assets to governments, buying existing government assets, or via public-private partnerships. Infrastructure cash flows are often inflation-linked and regulated, providing stable, predictable income streams.

In terms of risk hierarchy, first mortgages and investment-grade CMBSs are least risky (bond-like), followed by core strategies (stable rental income), then value-add, and finally opportunistic strategies (most equity-like). REITs provide diversification and liquidity, but their correlation with equities is higher than direct investment — and correlations increase during market downturns. Despite this, real estate and infrastructure both improve portfolio risk-return profiles relative to portfolios holding only traditional assets.
key_points: Real estate quadrant: two axes are Public/Private AND Debt/Equity — know all four quadrants and examples (e.g., private equity = direct ownership; public debt = CMBS/mortgage REITs).
Equity investor value = Property value minus outstanding debt; debt investors have priority claim in default.
Direct investment benefits: control, diversification, tax (depreciation deductions even as property appreciates); drawbacks: illiquidity, high capital, concentration risk, management complexity.
REITs avoid double taxation and redemption/liquidation risk (exchange-traded); three types: equity (own property), mortgage (lend/hold MBS), hybrid (both).
REIT risk spectrum: core (open-end, stable, least risky) → core-plus → value-add → opportunistic (closed-end, highest risk, most equity-like).
Risk ranking from least to most: first mortgage/investment-grade CMBS < core < core-plus < value-add < opportunistic strategies.

Infrastructure key trait: cash flows are often inflation-linked and regulated, providing stable/predictable income; accessible via construction, buying government assets, or public-private partnerships.

readings.title: Natural Resources Module 82.1: Farmland, Timberland, And
readings.summary:
Natural resources as an investment class include raw land, farmland, timberland, and commodities. All three land types are illiquid and location-driven, but farmland and timberland generate additional income from their output (crops and timber) beyond lease income and price appreciation. Farmland is primarily held by individuals, while institutions dominate raw land and timberland. Timberland requires specialized expertise and larger lot sizes, leading to the use of TIMOs (Timberland Investment Management Organizations). A key flexibility advantage of timberland is that harvest timing can be adjusted based on prices and expected growth rates, unlike most agricultural crops that must be harvested quickly. Both farmland and timberland have ESG appeal since they consume carbon dioxide.

Commodities are divided into metals (base and precious), agricultural products, and energy products. Direct investment is possible but derivatives—especially futures—are the most common vehicle. Storage and transportation costs are real considerations for physical commodities. Other investment vehicles include ETPs (ETFs/ETNs), managed futures funds (CTAs), separately managed accounts (SMAs), and specialized sector funds. Futures trade on exchanges and carry no counterparty risk, while forwards and options on futures are also available.

Commodity futures pricing is governed by the cost-of-carry model: futures price ≈ spot price × (1 + risk-free rate) + storage costs − convenience yield. When convenience yield is low, net cost of carry is positive and futures trade above spot (contango), which hurts long-only investors via negative roll yield. When convenience yield is high, futures trade below spot (backwardation), which benefits long-only investors. Convenience yield reflects the non-monetary benefit of holding physical inventory.

Commodity prices are driven by supply and demand, with supply often inelastic in the short run due to long production lead times, making prices highly sensitive to demand shocks or supply disruptions (weather, geopolitics). Historically, commodities have delivered higher returns and higher volatility than global stocks and bonds. Timberland and farmland offer higher returns than bonds with lower volatility than equities. Crucially, commodity returns have low correlation with equities and bonds, providing strong diversification benefits. Commodities also serve as an inflation hedge, outperforming stocks and bonds during high-inflation periods.
key_points: Timberland offers a 'flexibility option': harvest timing can be delayed when prices are low and accelerated when prices are high — unlike agricultural crops that must be harvested on fixed schedules.

Futures pricing formula: Futures Price ≈ Spot Price × (1 + Rf) + Storage Costs − Convenience Yield. Contango = futures > spot (low convenience yield); Backwardation = futures < spot (high convenience yield). Contango hurts long investors; backwardation helps them.

Commodity supply is inelastic in the short run (long lead times for oil wells, crop planting), making prices volatile during demand swings or supply shocks such as weather events or geopolitical disruptions.

Adding commodities to a portfolio provides diversification because their returns have historically had low correlation with global equities and bonds, and they act as an inflation hedge (outperform during high inflation).

Investment vehicles for commodities: futures/forwards/options (exchange-traded futures have no counterparty risk), ETPs (ETFs/ETNs), managed futures (CTAs), SMAs for large investors, and specialized sector funds.

Farmland is primarily held by individuals; institutions favor raw land and timberland. Timberland investors lacking expertise can use TIMOs. Both farmland and timberland have limited financing options (mainly bank loans or private debt), though newer farmland REITs now offer retail access.

Commodity sectors: metals (base/industrial + precious), agricultural products, and energy. Government policy (subsidies, price supports, resource access controls, climate regulation) materially affects commodity supply and demand.

readings.title: Hedge Funds Module 83.1: Hedge Funds
readings.summary:
Hedge funds are private pooled investment vehicles available only to qualified or accredited investors. Unlike mutual funds, they are lightly regulated and grant managers wide latitude to pursue diverse strategies using long/short positions, leverage, and derivatives across traditional and alternative asset classes. Their primary return drivers are market inefficiency and price volatility, and they are evaluated on a total return or risk-adjusted return basis rather than against a benchmark. Managers frequently invest their own capital alongside clients, and performance fees are often tied to a high-water mark, meaning fees are only earned when the fund surpasses its prior peak value.

Hedge funds are organized into four broad strategy categories. Equity hedge strategies (fundamental long/short, growth, value, market neutral, short bias) exploit mispricings in publicly traded equities. Event-driven strategies (merger arbitrage, distressed/restructuring, activist shareholder, special situations) profit from corporate actions and restructurings. Relative value strategies (convertible arbitrage, fixed income arbitrage, multistrategy) capture pricing discrepancies between related securities. Opportunistic strategies (global macro, managed futures/CTAs) exploit broad macroeconomic trends and commodity price dynamics, often using ETFs or derivatives.

Hedge funds differ from other private vehicles in several structural ways. They carry higher costs due to management fees plus incentive fees. Liquidity is restricted through lockup periods (during which redemptions are prohibited or penalized), notice periods (time needed to fulfill a redemption request), and liquidity gates (partial restrictions on redemptions). These mechanisms allow managers to unwind positions orderly, but can trap investors during periods of poor performance. Transparency is intentionally limited to protect proprietary strategies, complicating accurate valuation.

Structurally, hedge funds may be organized as commingled funds pooling capital from multiple investors, or as funds of funds (FoFs) that invest across multiple underlying hedge funds for diversification. FoFs add a second layer of fees but provide access, diversification, and due diligence benefits for investors who lack the resources to evaluate individual hedge funds directly.
key_points: High-water mark: performance fees are only payable when fund NAV exceeds its prior peak value — know this definition precisely.
Four hedge fund strategy types: Equity Hedge, Event-Driven, Relative Value, and Opportunistic (macro/managed futures) — memorize subcategories for each.
Lockup period vs. notice period vs. liquidity gate: lockup = no redemptions initially; notice period = advance warning required; liquidity gate = partial redemption restriction.
Merger arbitrage is NOT risk-free — deals can fall apart or terms can change, exposing the fund to loss despite the 'arbitrage' label.
Hedge funds vs. private equity: hedge funds invest in liquid assets with shorter horizons and periodic redemptions; PE funds have longer horizons and capital lock-up.
Managed futures funds (CTAs) trade commodity or financial futures; commodity prices are mean-reverting by nature (high prices reduce demand, low prices reduce supply).
Hedge funds are evaluated on total return or risk-adjusted return (not vs. a benchmark), and managers typically co-invest their own capital alongside LPs.

readings.title: Introduction To Digital Assets
readings.summary:
Digital assets are electronically created, stored, and transferred assets secured by distributed ledger technology (DLT), also called blockchain technology. A distributed ledger is a shared database giving all participants identical copies of transaction records. Key DLT benefits include accuracy, transparency, security, rapid ownership transfer, and peer-to-peer interactions, while drawbacks include privacy concerns, data protection issues, and high computational energy demands. DLT networks consist of a digital ledger, a consensus mechanism, and a participant network, and can support smart contracts that self-execute based on predetermined conditions.

Blockchains record transactions sequentially in cryptographically linked blocks. Two main consensus protocols govern how blocks are added: Proof of Work (PoW), where miners solve cryptographic puzzles to validate transactions (energy-intensive, most widely used), and Proof of Stake (PoS), where validators pledge collateral (staking) to authenticate transactions and are rewarded with returns on their stake. Both protocols protect against manipulation; PoW requires a single party to control 51% of the network to corrupt it, making larger networks more secure.

DLT networks are either permissionless (open to all users, trustless, decentralized — e.g., Bitcoin) or permissioned (restricted access with tiered controls, more cost-effective). Digital asset categories include cryptocurrencies (privately issued, no central bank backing, near-real-time P2P transfers), stablecoins (pegged to another asset like USD, backed by a reserve basket, not exchangeable for fiat), and meme coins (entertainment-driven, highly speculative). Bitcoin and Ether dominate the market, with Ethereum's programmable blockchain enabling smart contract applications beyond simple value storage.

Additional digital asset types include security tokens (blockchain-based ownership of traditional assets like real estate or equity, subject to securities regulation), utility tokens (grant access to products/services on a specific platform, not classified as securities), non-fungible tokens (NFTs, unique assets representing ownership of digital or real-world items), and asset-backed tokens (stablecoins maintaining price parity with a target asset such as gold). These categories illustrate how DLT is expanding into broad financial applications including asset ownership, fundraising, and decentralized finance (DeFi).
key_points: Distinguish PoW vs. PoS: PoW uses miners solving cryptographic puzzles (energy-intensive, most common); PoS uses validators who stake collateral and earn returns — know both mechanics and trade-offs.
Permissionless networks are open/decentralized (Bitcoin); permissioned networks have tiered access controls and are more cost-effective — know which features belong to each.

Stablecoins are pegged to an asset (e.g., USD) and backed by a reserve basket; they cannot be exchanged for fiat currency and have no legal/regulatory backing — algorithmic stablecoins (e.g., TerraUSD) failed due to loss of peg.

Security tokens represent ownership of real assets (equity, real estate) on a blockchain and ARE subject to securities regulation; utility tokens grant platform access and are NOT classified as securities.
NFTs are non-fungible (unique, non-interchangeable) digital assets representing ownership of digital or physical items; fungible tokens (cryptocurrencies) are interchangeable with identical units.
Smart contracts are self-executing programs on a blockchain triggered by predetermined conditions — applications include automating collateral transfers and contingent claims in default events.
Bitcoin and Ether are the two most dominant cryptocurrencies (>80% of market as of 2022); Ether's value extends beyond store-of-value because Ethereum supports programmable smart contracts.

readings.title: Portfolio Management: An Overview
readings.summary:
The portfolio perspective evaluates individual investments by their contribution to the overall portfolio's risk and return, rather than in isolation. Harry Markowitz's foundational research in the early 1950s demonstrated that diversification reduces portfolio risk as long as asset returns are not perfectly positively correlated. This work was extended in the 1960s into Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT), which establishes that equilibrium expected returns are a linear function of each security's market (non-diversifiable) risk. The diversification ratio—the standard deviation of an equally weighted portfolio divided by the average standard deviation of individual securities—quantifies this benefit; a lower ratio means greater risk reduction. Importantly, diversification benefits erode during market crises, as correlations among assets tend to rise during periods of financial stress.

The portfolio management process consists of three steps: planning, execution, and feedback. The planning step involves assessing the investor's risk tolerance, return objectives, time horizon, liquidity needs, tax situation, and unique constraints, culminating in a written Investment Policy Statement (IPS) that includes a benchmark. The execution step involves top-down asset allocation across asset classes (cash, equities, fixed income, real assets, etc.) followed by bottom-up security selection within those classes. The feedback step requires ongoing monitoring, periodic rebalancing to target allocations, and performance measurement against the IPS benchmark.

Different types of investors have distinct characteristics and needs. Individual investors invest for goals like retirement or education, often through defined contribution plans. Endowments and foundations have long horizons, high risk tolerance, and focus on preserving real portfolio value. Banks seek to earn a spread over deposit costs while maintaining liquidity. Insurance companies invest premiums to fund future claims—life insurers have longer horizons than property and casualty insurers. Sovereign wealth funds and investment companies (mutual funds) manage pooled assets with mandates specific to their mission or investment style.

Portfolio managers are classified as buy-side (managing money for institutions or individuals) or sell-side (providing research and recommendations to support sales of securities). Asset management firms range from full-service to specialty boutiques. Key relationships in the industry include the interactions among asset managers, financial advisors, and the end clients whose capital is ultimately being managed.
key_points: Diversification ratio = (std dev of equally weighted portfolio) / (average std dev of individual securities); a ratio below 1.0 indicates risk-reduction benefit; a ratio of 1.0 means no benefit.
Diversification reduces unsystematic (firm-specific) risk but NOT systematic (market) risk; MPT holds that only systematic risk is rewarded with higher expected returns.
Diversification benefits decrease during market crises because correlations among assets tend to increase, reducing the risk-reduction effect.

The three steps of portfolio management are: (1) Planning → produces the IPS; (2) Execution → top-down asset allocation + bottom-up security selection; (3) Feedback → rebalancing and performance measurement.
The IPS documents the investor's objectives and constraints, specifies a benchmark, and should be reviewed at least every few years or when circumstances change significantly.
Endowments and foundations have long time horizons, high risk tolerance, and low liquidity needs (beyond planned spending); banks and P&C insurers have shorter horizons and higher liquidity needs.
Defined benefit pension plans promise a specific retirement benefit (liability-driven); defined contribution plans shift investment risk to the employee (no guaranteed benefit).

readings.title: Basics Of Portfolio Planning And Construction
readings.summary:
A written Investment Policy Statement (IPS) is the foundation of sound portfolio management. It begins by jointly establishing the client's risk and return objectives, recognizing that high return and low risk goals are often mutually exclusive. The IPS ensures the portfolio manager fully understands the client's needs, circumstances, and constraints before making any investment decisions.

The major components of an IPS include: client description and objectives, purpose statement, duties and responsibilities of all parties, procedures for updates, investment objectives and constraints, guidelines on permitted asset types and leverage, performance evaluation benchmarks, and appendices on strategic asset allocation and rebalancing rules. At minimum, any IPS must contain client circumstances, an investment strategy, and a performance benchmark.

Risk and return objectives can be stated in absolute or relative terms. Absolute risk objectives set strict limits (e.g., 'no loss greater than 2% in any 12-month period') or probabilistic limits (e.g., 'no more than 5% probability of returns below -5%'). Return objectives can be nominal, real, or relative to a benchmark such as the S&P 500. Peer performance benchmarks are discouraged because they are not investable portfolios — you cannot replicate 'top quartile endowment returns' before the fact.

A critical distinction exists between an investor's ability (capacity) and willingness to take risk. Ability depends on objective financial factors: investment horizon, wealth relative to liabilities, job security, and insurance coverage. Willingness is subjective, based on attitudes and beliefs, often assessed via questionnaire. When the two conflict, the adviser generally defaults to the lower of the two — although if willingness is low but ability is high, the adviser may educate the client to correct misconceptions, without attempting to change core personality traits.

Investment constraints are summarized by the mnemonic R-R-T-T-L-L-U: Risk, Return, Time horizon, Tax situation, Liquidity, Legal restrictions, and Unique constraints. Longer time horizons allow more risk and less liquidity. Tax concerns affect after-tax return calculations and asset location decisions. Legal and regulatory factors may restrict certain asset types for institutional investors. Unique constraints capture personal preferences, ESG considerations, or concentrated stock positions that require special handling.
key_points: IPS minimum requirements: client circumstances/constraints, an investment strategy, and a performance benchmark — memorize these three.
Risk objectives can be absolute (strict limits or probabilistic) or relative (tied to a benchmark like MSCI World); peer benchmarks are invalid because they are not investable.
Ability to take risk = objective financial factors (horizon, wealth, job security); Willingness = subjective attitudes. When they conflict, use the LOWER of the two.
If ability is HIGH but willingness is LOW, the adviser may educate but must not override the client's risk personality — conform to the lower level.
Mnemonic R-R-T-T-L-L-U covers all IPS constraint categories: Risk, Return, Time horizon, Tax, Liquidity, Legal, Unique.
Illiquid investments (hedge funds, private equity) are unsuitable for investors with near-term or unpredictable cash needs — liquidity constraint governs asset eligibility.
Return objectives stated relative to peer portfolios (e.g., 'top quartile of endowments') are problematic for the exam: they cannot be replicated in advance by portfolio construction.

readings.title: The Behavioral Biases Of Individuals
readings.summary:
Behavioral finance challenges the traditional finance assumption that individuals are perfectly rational. Pioneered by Kahneman and Tversky, research shows that human decision-making has systematic, predictable biases. These biases fall into two broad categories: cognitive errors (stemming from faulty reasoning, flawed information processing, or memory errors) and emotional biases (stemming from feelings and intuition). Cognitive errors are more amenable to correction through education and awareness, while emotional biases are deeply ingrained and often must be accommodated rather than eliminated. When a bias has both cognitive and emotional elements, focusing on the cognitive component offers the best chance of mitigation.

Cognitive errors are subdivided into belief perseverance biases and information processing errors. Belief perseverance biases include: conservatism (failing to update forecasts when new information arrives), confirmation (seeking only information that supports existing views), representativeness (categorizing investments based on superficial similarities, ignoring base rates or small sample sizes), illusion of control (overestimating one's ability to influence outcomes), and hindsight bias (believing past events were predictable after the fact). These biases collectively cause investors to be slow to revise positions, overconfident in their views, and prone to pattern-seeking in noisy data.

Information processing errors include anchoring and adjustment (over-relying on an initial reference point), mental accounting (treating money differently depending on its source or intended use), framing (being swayed by how a question or choice is presented rather than its substance), and availability bias (overweighting information that is easily recalled, such as vivid or recent events). These errors distort how investors analyze data, leading to suboptimal portfolio decisions such as ignoring diversification benefits or making inconsistent risk assessments.

Emotional biases include loss aversion (feeling losses more acutely than equivalent gains, leading to holding losers too long and selling winners too soon), overconfidence (overestimating one's own analytical skill and precision of forecasts), self-control bias (favoring short-term gratification over long-term goals), status quo bias (preferring the current state to avoid the discomfort of change), endowment bias (overvaluing assets simply because one owns them), and regret aversion (avoiding decisions that might lead to regret, resulting in herding behavior or excessive caution). Emotional biases are harder to correct and often require advisors to work around them through portfolio structure and client communication strategies.
key_points: Cognitive errors (faulty reasoning) can be reduced with education/awareness; emotional biases (feelings/intuition) are harder to overcome and may need to be accommodated — when a bias is both, target the cognitive component.
Belief perseverance biases (CCRIH): Conservatism, Confirmation, Representativeness, Illusion of control, Hindsight — all involve clinging to prior beliefs or misjudging information.

Representativeness has two sub-forms: base-rate neglect (ignoring population frequency) and sample-size neglect (drawing conclusions from too-small samples); both lead to faulty categorization of investments.
Information processing errors (AMFA): Anchoring & adjustment, Mental accounting, Framing, Availability — these distort how data is analyzed rather than which data is sought.
Loss aversion (emotional bias) causes investors to hold losers too long and sell winners too soon (disposition effect); losses loom roughly twice as large psychologically as equivalent gains.

Overconfidence bias leads to excessive trading, under-diversification, and underestimation of risk; it is an emotional bias that is difficult to correct and can manifest as illusion of knowledge or illusion of control.

Regret aversion causes herding behavior (following the crowd so outcomes feel less personally blameworthy) and excessive caution; status quo and endowment biases similarly create inertia and reluctance to rebalance portfolios.

readings.title: Introduction To Risk Management
readings.summary:
Risk management is not about eliminating all risk, but about identifying an organization's risk tolerance, measuring existing risks, and adjusting exposures to achieve an optimal bundle of risks. Organizations may deliberately increase exposure to risks they manage well and reduce exposure to risks they do not, using tools like insurance and hedging. Returns above the risk-free rate require taking on risk, so risk management is fundamentally about optimizing risk-taking, not avoiding it.

A comprehensive risk management framework includes establishing risk governance policies, determining risk tolerance, identifying and measuring risks, managing and mitigating risks, monitoring exposures over time, communicating across the organization, and performing strategic risk analysis. Risk governance refers to senior management's oversight role in setting the organization's risk tolerance and overall risk strategy, often supported by a risk management committee.

Risk budgeting is the process of allocating firm resources across assets by considering their risk characteristics so the overall portfolio matches the organization's risk tolerance while maximizing expected returns. Risk budgets can be expressed as a single metric (e.g., portfolio beta, VaR, duration) or allocated across asset categories or specific risk factors like interest rate, equity market, and foreign exchange risk.

Financial risks arise from exposure to financial markets and include credit risk (counterparty default), liquidity risk (forced sale below fair value), and market risk (price and rate uncertainty). Non-financial risks stem from operations or external sources and include operational risk, solvency risk, regulatory risk, political/tax risk, legal risk, model risk, tail risk, and accounting risk. These risk types often interact — an operational failure, for example, can trigger liquidity and solvency risk simultaneously. For individuals, mortality risk and longevity risk are especially important planning considerations, addressed through life insurance and lifetime annuities respectively.
key_points: Risk management seeks to identify risk tolerance, measure risks, and modify exposures to achieve an OPTIMAL bundle of risks — not to minimize or eliminate all risk.

Risk governance is senior management's responsibility: setting risk tolerance, oversight strategy, and the overall risk management framework; a risk management committee facilitates cross-organizational coordination.
Risk budgeting allocates acceptable risk across investments/assets to maximize expected returns; common metrics include portfolio beta, VaR, duration, and returns variance.
Financial risks: Credit risk (counterparty default), Liquidity risk (sale below fair value), Market risk (prices, rates, currencies) — memorize these three categories.

Non-financial risks include Operational, Solvency, Regulatory, Political/Tax, Legal, Model, Tail, and Accounting risk — tail risk specifically refers to underestimating the probability of extreme outcomes, often by incorrectly assuming a normal distribution.
For individuals, mortality risk (dying too soon) is managed with life insurance; longevity risk (outliving assets) is managed with lifetime annuities.
Financial and non-financial risks interact: an operational failure can cascade into liquidity and solvency risk, so risks must be considered in combination, not in isolation.

readings.title: Ethics And Trust In The Investment Profession
readings.summary:
Ethics is defined as a set of shared beliefs about acceptable and unacceptable behavior, with ethical conduct balancing self-interest against impact on stakeholders. Stakeholders in investment management include clients, coworkers, employers, and the profession itself. A code of ethics is a written set of moral principles that guides behavior, communicates values to the public, and in some cases sets minimum behavioral standards that members must meet.

A profession is an occupational group requiring specialized knowledge, a code of ethics, a regulatory body, client focus, and often continuing education requirements. Professions establish trust by demanding high expertise, setting ethical standards, monitoring conduct, and prioritizing client needs. Investment management qualifies as a profession under these criteria, and its practitioners are held to either a suitability standard (matching recommendations to client risk/return profile) or the stronger fiduciary standard (acting in the client's best interest).

High ethical standards are especially critical in investment management because professionals are entrusted with clients' wealth, and investment advice is an intangible product whose quality is hard for clients to evaluate. Unethical behavior damages client wealth, harms firms' reputations, and has broad societal consequences: it reduces the pool of capital flowing to markets, raises the cost of capital, and causes misallocation of investment away from the most productive uses, slowing economic growth.

Challenges to ethical behavior include overconfidence in one's own ethical quality and an underestimation of situational influences such as social pressure, loyalty to employers, and financial incentives. Firms that focus exclusively on rules-based compliance risk creating a culture where employees only ask 'what can I do?' rather than 'what should I do?', leaving ethical principles unexamined. Recognizing these biases is the first step to maintaining genuinely ethical conduct.

Ethical standards and legal standards are related but distinct. Not all unethical acts are illegal, and not all illegal acts are unethical. Ethical principles generally set a higher bar than law; new regulations often follow high-profile unethical behavior (e.g., the Securities Acts of the 1930s post-Great Depression). Investment professionals must therefore look beyond mere legal compliance and apply ethical principles in all decisions.
key_points: Ethics balances self-interest with impact on stakeholders; ethical conduct improves outcomes for all parties affected, not just the decision-maker.
A profession is distinguished by: specialized knowledge, a code of ethics, a regulatory body, client focus, service to society, and continuing education requirements.
Suitability standard = recommendations match client risk/return profile; Fiduciary standard = higher bar, must act in the client's BEST interest — know the difference.
Investment advice is intangible, making quality hard to assess; this makes trust and ethical standards MORE important in finance than in many other industries.
Unethical behavior has macro-level consequences: reduces capital formation, raises cost of capital, and causes misallocation of resources that harms economic growth.
Key challenge to ethics: individuals overestimate their own ethical behavior and underestimate situational influences (social pressure, loyalty, financial incentives).
Ethical standards ≠ legal standards: ethics often sets a higher bar; something can be legal but unethical (undisclosed conflict of interest) or illegal but ethical (whistleblowing).

readings.title: Code Of Ethics And Standards Of Professional Conduct
readings.summary: The CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program operates under the CFA Institute Bylaws and Rules of Procedure, governed by the Board of Governors and enforced by the Disciplinary Review Committee. Inquiries can be triggered by self-disclosure, written complaints, public evidence of misconduct, exam proctor reports, or CFA Institute's own monitoring of exam materials and social media. After an inquiry, staff may issue no sanction, a cautionary letter, or formal discipline — and if a member rejects a proposed sanction, the matter goes to a peer review panel.
key_points: There are 6 components of the Code of Ethics: integrity/competence/diligence, client interests above own, independent judgment, ethical practice, promote market integrity, maintain competence.

There are 7 Standards: I. Professionalism, II. Integrity of Capital Markets, III. Duties to Clients, IV. Duties to Employers, V. Investment Analysis/Recommendations/Actions, VI. Conflicts of Interest, VII. Responsibilities as CFA Member/Candidate.
Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law: comply with the MORE STRICT of applicable law or CFA Standards; dissociate from any known violation.
Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information: members must NOT act or cause others to act on material nonpublic information that could affect investment value.
Standard III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care: client interests come BEFORE employer's and member's own interests.

Standard III(C) Suitability: must assess client's investment experience, risk/return objectives, and financial constraints BEFORE making recommendations; for managed portfolios, stay consistent with stated mandate.
Disciplinary process: inquiry → investigation → staff decision (no action / cautionary letter / sanction) → member may accept or reject → if rejected, goes to peer disciplinary review panel.

readings.title: Guidance For Standards I–VII Module 91.1: Guidance For Standards I(A)
readings.summary:
The Standards of Professional Conduct consist of seven Standards (I–VII) with 22 subsections, as detailed in the Standards of Practice Handbook (11th Edition). This reading serves as an introduction to how the Standards are applied in practice, emphasizing that violations are generally clear-cut and that surrounding circumstances or motivations rarely change whether a violation has occurred. The only notable exception is when intent is an explicit element of the Standard itself (e.g., intent to mislead).

Standard I(A) – Knowledge of the Law requires members and candidates to understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations governing their professional activities, including the CFA Institute Code and Standards. When multiple regulatory regimes apply (home country, host country, and CFA Institute Standards), members must follow the most strict set of rules. Any violation of the Code and Standards automatically constitutes a violation of Standard I(A) as well.

When a member becomes aware of ongoing violations by colleagues or clients, they are strongly encouraged (though not required by the Standards) to report the violations to their supervisor or compliance department. If internal remediation is not possible, the member must dissociate from the violating activity—this could mean refusing to work on certain accounts, avoiding certain marketing materials, or in extreme cases, resigning from the firm entirely. Members should document violations during dissociation.

The reading also clarifies that terms like 'reasonable,' 'adequate,' and 'token' appear throughout the Standards but are intentionally left without rigid numerical thresholds. Exam questions will be constructed so the answer does not hinge on gray-area judgment calls—clue words like 'lavish' or 'luxury' signal a clear violation, while a routine business dinner signals a permissible token gift. Knowing the category of violation (e.g., Professionalism vs. Duties to Clients) is sufficient; memorizing Standard numbers is not required.
key_points: When laws of multiple jurisdictions conflict, always follow the MOST STRICT rule—never the least restrictive.
Any violation of the Code and Standards also automatically violates Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law.
Members are STRONGLY ENCOURAGED (not required by the Standards) to report violations; however, local law may independently require reporting.
If dissociation from a violation is impossible within the firm, resignation may be required to stay in compliance with Standard I(A).
Intent is generally irrelevant to whether a violation occurs—if the Standard prohibits it, it is a violation regardless of motive. Exception: Standards where intent to mislead is an explicit element.

Exam tip: Watch for trigger words like 'lavish,' 'luxury,' or 'token'—these signal the exam writer's intended answer on gift/entertainment questions without requiring you to define an exact dollar threshold.

Recommended best practices include maintaining copies of current regulations, having a written compliance program reviewed regularly, and consulting legal/compliance counsel when legality is uncertain.

readings.title: Introduction To The Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS)
readings.summary:
GIPS were created to address the problem of inconsistent and potentially misleading investment performance reporting. Without a standard, firms could cherry-pick top-performing portfolios, exclude terminated accounts (often closed due to poor performance), or select favorable time periods to make their results look better than they actually were. GIPS provides a voluntary but standardized framework that makes performance comparable across firms and protects both current and prospective clients from misrepresentation.

The GIPS standards for firms consist of eight sections covering: (1) Fundamentals of Compliance, (2) Input Data and Calculation Methodology, (3) Composite and Pooled Fund Maintenance, (4-7) reporting requirements for composites and pooled funds using both time-weighted and money-weighted returns, and (8) GIPS Advertising Guidelines. Firms may only claim GIPS compliance if they comply fully and on a firmwide basis — partial compliance is not permitted.

A composite is a grouping of discretionary portfolios managed under the same strategy or objective. Composites must include all fee-paying, discretionary portfolios (current and past) managed under a given strategy. Critically, portfolios must be assigned to a composite before their performance is known, preventing firms from gaming composite construction to inflate returns. Composite returns are calculated as asset-weighted averages, not simple averages.

The definition of the firm for GIPS purposes must reflect the entity as presented to clients — including all geographic locations operating under the same name. Discretion determines which portfolios are included in composites; if a client's restrictions prevent a manager from executing the intended strategy, the portfolio may be deemed nondiscretionary and excluded. Independent third-party verification, while not required, is strongly encouraged and must cover the entire firm — not just selected composites.
key_points: GIPS compliance is VOLUNTARY, but a firm may only CLAIM compliance if it complies FULLY and on a FIRMWIDE basis — no partial compliance claims allowed.
Only investment management firms (those managing assets for others) can claim GIPS compliance; related parties (e.g., software developers) may endorse but NOT claim compliance.
Composites must include ALL fee-paying, discretionary portfolios managed under the same strategy — current AND past (terminated accounts cannot be excluded).
Portfolio assignment to a composite must occur BEFORE performance is known, preventing survivorship bias and cherry-picking of results.
Composite returns are ASSET-WEIGHTED averages of included portfolio returns, not simple averages.
Independent verification is NOT required for GIPS compliance, but if pursued, it must be done by a THIRD PARTY on a FIRMWIDE basis — not on individual composites.
A nondiscretionary portfolio (one where client restrictions prevent the manager from executing the intended strategy) may be excluded from composites.

readings.title: Ethics Application Module 93.1: Ethics Application
readings.summary:
Reading 93 applies the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct through concrete case studies across all major Standards. Under Standard I (Professionalism), members must actively investigate suspicious activity rather than ignore it due to client relationships, cannot forge documents for convenience, must avoid conflicts of interest such as political contributions that could influence business awards, cannot guarantee returns, and must disclose material changes like key personnel departures. Civil disobedience arrests do not automatically constitute misconduct unless they involve dishonesty or reflect adversely on professional integrity.

Standard II (Integrity of Capital Markets) cases illustrate that material nonpublic information obtained even incidentally—such as overhearing a conversation—cannot be traded upon. Selective sharing of information with a specific analyst group is treated as nonpublic disclosure, not public disclosure. Market manipulation includes fraudulent representation of shareholder counts to meet exchange listing requirements.

Standard III (Duties to Clients) emphasizes that members cannot contractually opt out of acting in clients' best interests or restrict clients' legal rights. Suitability must be independently assessed even when a client self-directs a trade request. Performance presentation must not blur the distinction between a fund's actual track record and a composite of prior separately managed accounts. Confidentiality obligations extend to compliance officers who fail to implement adequate controls, not just individuals who breach security directly.

Standard IV (Duties to Employers) and Standard V (Investment Analysis) cases show that members may not disparage their current employer while still employed, even when planning to leave. However, whistleblowing to regulators is protected when internal remedies have failed. Members must distinguish between acceptable expert network consultations and impermissible receipt of material nonpublic information, and must disclose all relevant risks—including model and liquidity risks—in investment recommendations.

Standards VI and VII address conflicts of interest and responsibilities as a CFA candidate. Members must disclose referral arrangements and gifts that could compromise objectivity. Candidates must not share exam questions or misrepresent the CFA designation's meaning. The 'CFA' designation must always follow the charterholder's name and cannot be used as a standalone noun. Expressing disagreement with GIPS or CFA Institute policies is permitted, but members must still comply with applicable Standards.
key_points: Selective disclosure to a specific analyst group does NOT constitute public disclosure — trading on such information violates Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information.
Members cannot contractually 'opt out' of the Standards — any client agreement clause that limits fiduciary duty or restricts clients' legal rights violates Standard III(A).
Misrepresentation by omission is a violation — failing to inform a prospective client of a key personnel departure during an active proposal process violates Standard I(C).

Civil disobedience arrests are NOT automatically a Standards violation; misconduct under Standard I(D) requires dishonesty, fraud, deceit, or conduct that adversely reflects on professional integrity.
Performance presentation must clearly distinguish a fund's own track record from a composite of prior separately managed accounts — blurring the two violates Standard III(D).
Confidentiality obligations extend to compliance officers: failure to implement adequate data controls (e.g., allowing downloads to personal servers) is itself a violation of Standard III(E).
'CFA' must always be used as an adjective following the charterholder's name (e.g., 'Jane Smith, CFA') and never as a standalone noun or verb — misuse violates Standard VII(B).
